name: DESIGN AND MAINTENANCE OF GREEN HOUSE

topics.title: History and Types of Greenhouses
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEAGT721 - DESIGN AND MAINTENANCE OF GREEN HOUSE
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** History and Types of Greenhouses

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Greenhouse (KTU 2024 Definition):** A *greenhouse* is a framed or inflated structure covered with a transparent or translucent material (glass, plastic film, or rigid plastic panels) in which environmental conditions such as temperature, humidity, light intensity, and carbon dioxide concentration can be regulated for the optimized cultivation of plants, vegetables, flowers, and high-value horticultural crops.

The term **"Greenhouse Effect"** in horticulture refers to the ability of the covering material to transmit short-wave solar radiation while absorbing/reflecting the long-wave (thermal) radiation re-emitted by the soil and plants, thereby trapping heat within the enclosed structure.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain-English Explanation)

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Car-in-Sun" Analogy:** Think of a car parked in direct sunlight with its windows closed. The sunlight passes through the glass, warms the seats and dashboard, but the heat cannot escape. The interior becomes much hotter than the outside air. A greenhouse works in **exactly the same way** — the sun's rays enter through transparent walls, warm the plants and soil, and the heat gets trapped inside.

In simple words: A greenhouse is essentially a **"trap" for sunlight** — letting light in but not letting warmth out. By controlling this trap (opening vents, adding fans, heating, or shading), farmers can grow crops **anywhere, anytime**, regardless of the weather outside.

### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics in Greenhouse Engineering

The following metrics are internationally accepted and frequently referenced in KTU board examinations:

- **PAR (Photosynthetically Active Radiation):** 400–700 nm wavelength range
- **Global Solar Constant:** **1361 W/m²** (used as the extraterrestrial irradiance reference)
- **Standard Greenhouse Day Temperature:** **21–27 °C** for most crops
- **Standard Greenhouse Night Temperature:** **16–18 °C** for most crops
- **Optimum Relative Humidity (RH):** **60–80 %**
- **CO₂ Enrichment Level:** **800–1200 ppm** (vs. ambient 400 ppm)

### 1.4 One-Line Definition Table for Quick Recall

| Term | One-Line Definition |
|---|---|
| Greenhouse | A structure used for growing plants under controlled environmental conditions |
| Cold Frame | A simple, low, unheated greenhouse for hardening seedlings |
| Hot Bed | A greenhouse with bottom heating (manure or electric) for raising seedlings |
| Conservatory | A decorative greenhouse attached to a residence for ornamentals |
| Glasshouse | A greenhouse covered specifically with glass panels |
| Polyhouse | A greenhouse covered with polyethylene film (most common in India) |
| Shade Net House | A structure covered with shade-cloth for light reduction in tropical zones |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Historical Evolution of Greenhouses (Chronological Deep-Dive)

The history of greenhouses mirrors the history of **human desire to control nature**. Below is a structured, exam-ready chronology:

#### Era 1 — Ancient Roots (Roman Times to 13th Century)
- **30 AD — Roman Emperor Tiberius:** Historical records show Tiberius demanded his favourite cucumber (*cucumis*) daily. Roman gardeners developed **mobile, translucent beds** (called *specularia*) made of sheets of mica, alabaster, or oiled cloth that could be wheeled into sunlight by day and stored in heated rooms at night. This is the **proto-greenhouse**.
- **13th Century — Italy:** Glass manufacturing advances allowed Italian botanists to construct *giardini botanici* (botanical gardens) with glazed enclosures for exotic medicinal plants brought from the tropics.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The Romans understood the *greenhouse principle* nearly 2000 years before the term "greenhouse" was ever coined — the term itself appeared in the **English language only around 1684**.

#### Era 2 — The Scientific Revolution (17th Century)
- **1599 — Padua, Italy:** The **Botanical Garden of Padua** (Orto Botanico di Padova) built the first **botanical greenhouse** with heated walls.
- **1681 — UK:** Joseph Fatio built a "glazed" structure in Zurich-like style.
- **1684 — UK:** The term *"greenhouse"* first appeared in print.
- **1692 — UK:** The first **heated greenhouse** was constructed using a *flue* (chimney) running through the floor to distribute heat.

#### Era 3 — Industrial Age (19th Century)
- **1800s — Europe:** Glass tax was lifted in the UK (1845), triggering a **glasshouse boom**. The Crystal Palace (1851) showcased mass glass engineering.
- **1850s — Netherlands:** The Dutch developed large-scale glasshouses for cut flowers and vegetables. This laid the foundation of today's **Westland glasshouse region** (world's largest cluster).
- **1880s — USA:** Commercial greenhouses appeared in New York, New Jersey, and Massachusetts for cut flowers and tomatoes.

#### Era 4 — Modern Plastic Revolution (20th Century)
- **1950s — USA:** **Polyethylene (PE) film** was first used to cover greenhouses — a watershed moment that made protected cultivation affordable globally.
- **1960s — Israel & Netherlands:** **Drip irrigation** and **climate control automation** were integrated.
- **1970s — Japan & Europe:** **Computerized climate control**, **CO₂ enrichment**, and **hydroponics** transformed greenhouses into high-tech agri-factories.
- **1980s — India:** Protected cultivation entered India via the **National Horticulture Board** and **IARI (Pusa, Delhi)**.
- **2000s–Present:** **Smart greenhouses** with IoT sensors, AI-based climate prediction, vertical farming, and automated nutrient dosing.

### 2.2 Classification of Greenhouses — KTU High-Yield Master Table

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Greenhouses are classified by **shape, material, temperature, structure, and function**. Board exam questions almost always ask for classification with **examples and diagrams**. Master the table below.

| Classification Basis | Type | Key Feature | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **By Shape** | Lean-to | Attached to one wall of an existing building | Small home gardens, hobby use |
| | Even-span (A-frame) | Symmetric roof, two equal slopes | Most common commercial type |
| | Uneven-span | Roof slopes of unequal length | Hilly/sloping sites |
| | Ridge and Furrow (Sawtooth) | Multiple connected spans | Large commercial (Netherlands) |
| | Quonset | Curved arch (half-cylinder) | Low-cost, plastic-covered |
| | Gothic Arch | Pointed arch shape | Better snow shedding |
| | Mansard | Double-pitched roof | Research, high light transmission |
| **By Covering Material** | Glasshouse | Glass panes (highest light transmission, **~90 %**) | Premium flowers, research |
| | Polyhouse | Polyethylene film (**~80–85 %** transmission) | Most common in India |
| | Polycarbonate | Rigid twin-wall PC sheets | Permanent structures |
| | Acrylic | UV-stable acrylic panels | Premium installations |
| | Net house | Shade net (30–90 % shade) | Tropical vegetables, orchids |
| **By Temperature** | Cold Greenhouse | No heating, just solar gain | Hardening, cool-season crops |
| | Cool Greenhouse | **> 7 °C** maintained | Spinach, lettuce |
| | Warm (Temperate) Greenhouse | **> 13 °C** maintained | Tomatoes, cucumbers |
| | Hot Greenhouse | **> 18 °C** maintained | Tropical fruits, orchids |
| **By Structure** | Free-standing | Independent structure | Small farms |
| | Gutter-connected | Multiple spans share a gutter | Large commercial complexes |
| **By Ventilation** | Naturally ventilated | Roof + side vents | Most common worldwide |
| | Mechanically ventilated | Fans + louvers | High-temperature regions |
| **By Function** | Shade Net House | Reduces light intensity by 30–90 % | Cut foliage, capsicum |
| | Hydroponic Greenhouse | Soilless cultivation | Lettuce, strawberries |
| | Screen House | Insect-proof netting | Virus-free vegetable production |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Greenhouse Heat-Loss Basics)

Although Module 1 is largely descriptive, the following formulas from the heat-transfer domain are *frequently asked in Part A (3-mark)* questions and form the basis of Module 2 (Design).

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T$ | Overall heat loss through covering | W |
| 2 | $A_g = L \cdot W$ | Floor area of even-span greenhouse | m² |
| 3 | $V = A_g \cdot H$ | Volume of greenhouse (H = average height) | m³ |
| 4 | $SA = 2 \cdot A_g + P \cdot H$ | Approx. surface area (P = perimeter) | m² |
| 5 | $R = \dfrac{\Delta T}{Q}$ | Thermal resistance | °C/W |
| 6 | $U = \dfrac{1}{R}$ | Overall heat transfer coefficient | W/m²·K |
| 7 | $I = S \cdot \tau$ | Inside PAR (S = outside solar, $\tau$ = transmittance) | W/m² |
| 8 | $V_{air} = \dfrac{Q}{\rho \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T}$ | Required air exchange rate | m³/s |

> [!NOTE]
> **Notation Key:**
> - $Q$ = Heat transfer rate (W)
> - $U$ = Overall heat transfer coefficient (W/m²·K)
> - $A$ = Surface area (m²)
> - $\Delta T$ = Temperature difference (K or °C)
> - $\rho$ = Air density = **1.2 kg/m³** (at 20 °C)
> - $c_p$ = Specific heat of air = **1005 J/(kg·K)**
> - $\tau$ = Cover transmittance (dimensionless, 0–1)
> - $I$ = Inside solar radiation (W/m²)

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The classification system directly informs:
- **Design load calculations** (snow load for Gothic arch vs. Quonset)
- **Capital cost estimation** (glass > polycarbonate > PE film)
- **Crop selection** (cool house for lettuce, hot house for orchids)
- **Site selection** (lean-to on south-facing wall for cold climates)
- **Energy budgeting** (cold frame uses 0 kWh, hot greenhouse uses 200–500 kWh/m²/year)

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Matrix — Pros and Cons of Each Greenhouse Type

This matrix is a **high-frequency KTU exam topic** ("Compare glasshouse and polyhouse" or "List advantages of gutter-connected greenhouse").

#### Matrix 3.1.A: Covering Material Comparison

| Parameter | Glass | Polyethylene Film | Polycarbonate | Net |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Light Transmittance | **88–92 %** | **80–85 %** | **75–85 %** | **30–90 %** (variable) |
| Lifespan | **25–35 years** | **2–4 years** | **10–15 years** | **3–5 years** |
| Cost (₹/m², India) | 1500–3000 | 40–80 | 400–800 | 50–150 |
| UV Degradation | Negligible | High (must use UV-stabilized) | Low | Moderate |
| Thermal Insulation | Low (single pane) | Moderate (with air gap) | High (twin-wall) | Low |
| Diffused Light | Low (clear glass) | Low–Medium (diffused films available) | Medium (twin-wall scatters) | High |
| Wind Resistance | Excellent (heavy) | Moderate | Excellent | Poor–Moderate |
| Maintenance | Glass cleaning, breakage | Frequent replacement | Periodic cleaning | Cleaning, replacement |

#### Matrix 3.1.B: Shape-Based Comparison

| Shape | Strength | Weakness | Best Suited For |
|---|---|---|---|
| Lean-to | Cheap, easy to build, shares heat from house | Limited size, only one slope | Home gardening |
| Even-span | Maximum light, easy layout | Higher material cost | Commercial vegetable production |
| Quonset | Lowest cost, easy to build | Limited headroom, weak against wind | Low-budget polyhouses |
| Gothic Arch | Excellent snow shedding | Complex framing | Cold, snowy regions (Kashmir, Himachal) |
| Ridge & Furrow | Massive area, shared walls, low heating cost | Complex gutter drainage, high initial cost | Dutch-style mega greenhouses |
| Sawtooth | Fixed natural ventilation | Modern glazing needed | Tropical/sub-tropical commercial |

### 3.2 Step-by-Step Derivation: Greenhouse Floor Area and Volume

The most basic quantitative design step in greenhouse engineering is calculating the **floor area, volume, and surface area** — these feed into heat-loss, ventilation, and lighting design in later modules.

#### Derivation 3.2.A — Floor Area of an Even-Span Greenhouse

**Problem Setup:** An even-span greenhouse has a length $L$ and a width $W$ measured at ground level.

**Step 1 — Define variables.**
- Let $L$ = length of greenhouse (m)
- Let $W$ = width of greenhouse (m)

**Step 2 — Floor area formula.**
Since the floor is rectangular:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_g &= L \times W
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute typical values.** For a standard Indian polyhouse of $L = 30$ m and $W = 8$ m:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_g &= 30 \times 8 \\
    &= 240 \; \text{m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Logic:** The floor area is the basis of *crop planning* — you can grow roughly **3–4 tomato plants per m²** in a high-tech polyhouse, so $240 \; \text{m}^2$ supports ~720–960 plants.

---

#### Derivation 3.2.B — Volume of the Greenhouse

**Step 1 — Determine the average height $H$.**
For a Quonset (curved) greenhouse, the average height is roughly **0.6 × peak height**.
For an A-frame (even-span) greenhouse with ridge height $H_r$ and gutter (eaves) height $H_e$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
H &= \frac{H_e + H_r}{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Volume formula.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V &= A_g \times H
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Numerical Example.**
Take $H_e = 3.0$ m, $H_r = 5.0$ m, $L = 30$ m, $W = 8$ m:

$$
\begin{aligned}
H &= \frac{3.0 + 5.0}{2} = 4.0 \; \text{m} \\
A_g &= 30 \times 8 = 240 \; \text{m}^2 \\
V &= 240 \times 4.0 = 960 \; \text{m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

---

#### Derivation 3.2.C — Approximate Surface Area of Covering

**Step 1 — Approximate roof area (two equal slopes).**
For an even-span greenhouse with each roof slope at angle $\theta$ from horizontal:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{roof} &= 2 \cdot L \cdot \left(\frac{W/2}{\cos\theta}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Side-wall area (two end walls + two side walls).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{side} &= 2 \cdot W \cdot H + 2 \cdot L \cdot H
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Total covering area.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{total} &= A_{roof} + A_{side}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Numerical Example (for $L = 30$ m, $W = 8$ m, $H = 4$ m, $\theta = 30°$).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{roof} &= 2 \times 30 \times \frac{4.0}{\cos 30°} \\
         &= 60 \times \frac{4.0}{0.866} \\
         &= 60 \times 4.619 \\
         &\approx 277.1 \; \text{m}^2 \\
A_{side} &= 2 \times 8 \times 4 + 2 \times 30 \times 4 \\
         &= 64 + 240 \\
         &= 304 \; \text{m}^2 \\
A_{total} &= 277.1 + 304 \\
           &\approx 581.1 \; \text{m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters:** The covering area determines **heat loss**, **material cost**, and **condensation drainage design** — three of the most exam-tested topics in Module 2.

---

#### Derivation 3.2.D — Inside PAR Calculation

**Step 1 — Define the problem.**
Outside solar PAR intensity $S = 600$ W/m²; covering transmittance $\tau = 0.85$.

**Step 2 — Apply the transmittance equation.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{inside} &= S \times \tau
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{inside} &= 600 \times 0.85 \\
            &= 510 \; \text{W/m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world interpretation:** A glasshouse ($\tau = 0.90$) delivers 540 W/m², while a low-cost polyhouse ($\tau = 0.75$) delivers only 450 W/m². The **10–15 % light loss** is the primary reason premium flower growers prefer glass.

### 3.3 Analytical Hierarchy — Choosing a Greenhouse Type

A structured decision tree is essential for both KTU case-study questions and real-world farm design.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1} &: \text{Climate of the region?} \\
&\quad \rightarrow \text{Cold/Snowy} \Rightarrow \text{Gothic arch or Quonset with twin-wall PC} \\
&\quad \rightarrow \text{Moderate} \Rightarrow \text{Even-span with PE film} \\
&\quad \rightarrow \text{Tropical/Coastal} \Rightarrow \text{Sawtooth or Net house} \\
\\
\text{Step 2} &: \text{Budget?} \\
&\quad \rightarrow \text{Low} \Rightarrow \text{Quonset polyhouse} \\
&\quad \rightarrow \text{Medium} \Rightarrow \text{Even-span polyhouse} \\
&\quad \rightarrow \text{High} \Rightarrow \text{Glasshouse / Gutter-connected glass} \\
\\
\text{Step 3} &: \text{Crop type?} \\
&\quad \rightarrow \text{Vegetables} \Rightarrow \text{Polyhouse (warm house)} \\
&\quad \rightarrow \text{Flowers} \Rightarrow \text{Glasshouse (cool/warm house)} \\
&\quad \rightarrow \text{Orchids/Exotic} \Rightarrow \text{Hot glasshouse with shading} \\
&\quad \rightarrow \text{Seedlings} \Rightarrow \text{Cold frame / Hot bed}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 4.4 Diagram D — Inside Airflow and Energy Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Sun[Sun Short Wave] -->|passes through cover| Inside[Greenhouse Interior]
    Inside -->|absorbed| Plants[Plants and Soil]
    Plants -->|emit long wave| Cover[Covering Material]
    Cover -->|re-radiates back| Inside
    Vent[Roof and Side Vents] -->|excess heat escape| Atmosphere
    Heat[Heating System] -->|warm air| Inside
    Cool[Cooling Pad and Fan] -->|cool air| Inside
    Inside -->|CO2 depleted| CO2[CO2 Enrichment]
    CO2 -->|CO2 injected| Inside
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this diagram matters:** This is the *most commonly drawn diagram* in KTU Module 1 viva voce and 2-mark questions. Practice drawing it from memory — examiner gives full marks for a clean version with all 6 energy flow arrows labeled.

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2023]**
*Define a greenhouse. Briefly explain the principle of the greenhouse effect with respect to cultivation of horticultural crops.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **greenhouse** is a framed or inflated structure covered with transparent or translucent material in which environmental conditions like temperature, humidity, light, and CO₂ can be regulated for plant growth.

**Principle of greenhouse effect:**
1. **Short-wave solar radiation** from the sun passes through the transparent covering and is absorbed by plants, soil, and internal surfaces.
2. These surfaces **re-radiate energy as long-wave (infrared) radiation**, which is absorbed and reflected back by the covering material.
3. This **traps heat** inside the structure, raising the internal temperature above the ambient outside temperature.

This artificial warm microclimate enables **year-round cultivation** of crops irrespective of external weather.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
*List any six different types of greenhouses based on shape with a one-line description of each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Lean-to greenhouse** — Attached to one wall of an existing building; uses the building's wall as one side.
2. **Even-span greenhouse** — A-frame with two equal roof slopes; the most common commercial type.
3. **Uneven-span greenhouse** — Roof slopes of unequal length; suited to sloping land.
4. **Ridge-and-furrow (Sawtooth) greenhouse** — Multiple even-spans connected at the gutter, sharing common walls.
5. **Quonset greenhouse** — Half-cylindrical arch shape; cheap and quick to construct.
6. **Gothic arch greenhouse** — Pointed arch profile; excellent for snow-load regions.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (CRITICAL):**
> 1. **Don't write in paragraphs only.** Always use **bullet points, sub-headings, and diagrams** — KTU examiners skim for keywords.
> 2. **Don't confuse polyethylene and polycarbonate.** PE is a *film*; PC is a *rigid twin-wall sheet*. Mixing these costs 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Don't write "Gothic arch" as "Gothic greenhouse"** — examiners flag this as a textbook reading gap.
> 4. **Always include at least one labelled diagram** in Part B answers. A missing diagram typically costs **2 marks**.
> 5. **Never claim a polyhouse is "permanent".** Its lifespan is 2–4 years — mark it as "semi-permanent" or "temporary".

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Style]**

**(a) Explain in detail the historical development of greenhouses, highlighting the key milestones from ancient times to the modern era. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Ancient Era (30 AD to 1200 AD):**
- Roman Emperor **Tiberius** (30 AD) demanded cucumbers daily; gardeners used mobile transparent beds (*specularia*) made of mica or oiled cloth, wheeled out in the sun and stored in heated rooms at night.
- 13th century Italy: Italian physicians and monks built glazed structures to grow medicinal herbs imported from Asia and the Middle East.

**2. Renaissance & Scientific Era (1400–1700 AD):**
- 1599: **Botanical Garden of Padua** built the first scientific greenhouse with heated walls to grow exotic plants.
- 1684: The English word *"greenhouse"* first appeared in print.
- 1692: First **heated greenhouse** built in the UK using a brick flue running under the floor.

**3. Industrial Era (1800–1900 AD):**
- 1845: **UK glass tax abolished** → explosion of glasshouse construction.
- 1851: **The Crystal Palace** (London) demonstrated mass glass engineering.
- 1850s: **Netherlands** developed large commercial glasshouses for cut flowers and vegetables — birth of the Westland cluster.

**4. Plastic Revolution (1950s onwards):**
- 1950s: **Polyethylene (PE) film** introduced → made greenhouses affordable worldwide.
- 1960s: **Drip irrigation and climate automation** (Israel, Netherlands).
- 1970s: **Computer-controlled environment** with CO₂ enrichment and hydroponics (Japan, Europe).
- 1980s: Protected cultivation entered India via **NHB and IARI, New Delhi**.

**5. Smart & Sustainable Era (2000–Present):**
- IoT-based climate monitoring
- AI-based yield prediction
- Vertical farming integration
- Solar-powered greenhouses in Africa and South Asia

**(b) Classify the different types of greenhouses based on (i) shape, (ii) covering material, and (iii) temperature, with suitable examples. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Classification by Shape** *(see Diagram C above for the full list)*
- Lean-to, Even-span, Uneven-span, Quonset, Gothic arch, Ridge-and-furrow, Mansard.

**(ii) Classification by Covering Material:**
| Type | Transmittance | Lifespan | Cost |
|---|---|---|---|
| Glass | 88–92 % | 25–35 yr | High |
| Polyethylene film | 80–85 % | 2–4 yr | Low |
| Polycarbonate (PC) | 75–85 % | 10–15 yr | Medium |
| Shade net | 30–90 % | 3–5 yr | Low |

**(iii) Classification by Temperature:**
| Type | Min Temp | Example Crops |
|---|---|---|
| Cold | No heating | Cabbage, peas |
| Cool | > 7 °C | Lettuce, spinach |
| Warm | > 13 °C | Tomato, cucumber |
| Hot | > 18 °C | Orchids, papaya |

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Internal Choice [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style]**

**(a) Discuss the different types of greenhouses based on construction and structural design. Compare any two types in detail. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Structural Types:**

1. **Free-standing Greenhouse** — An independent structure, typically used for small farms or research. Easy to install but inefficient in terms of land and energy use.

2. **Gutter-connected Greenhouse** — Multiple spans share a common gutter, forming a continuous structure. **Most efficient** for large-scale commercial production as it shares walls, reducing heat loss by 25–30 %.

3. **Lean-to Greenhouse** — Shares one wall with a building; economical for home gardening.

**Comparison: Even-span vs. Quonset Greenhouse**

| Parameter | Even-span (A-frame) | Quonset |
|---|---|---|
| Shape | Triangular, symmetric | Semi-cylindrical |
| Roof slope | Two equal pitches | Curved continuous |
| Headroom | Excellent | Limited (low along sides) |
| Snow shedding | Good | Moderate |
| Construction cost | Higher | Lower |
| Crop suitability | Tall crops (tomato, capsicum) | Short crops (lettuce, herbs) |
| Ventilation | Roof + side vents | Mainly end-wall vents |
| Durability | 20+ years | 10–15 years |

**(b) Explain in detail the different types of covering materials used in greenhouses. Discuss their properties, advantages, and limitations. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Glass:**
- Properties: Highest light transmittance (**88–92 %**), UV-stable, long lifespan.
- Advantages: Excellent light quality, easy to clean, durable (25–35 years).
- Limitations: Expensive, heavy (requires strong frame), fragile, poor insulation.

**2. Polyethylene (PE) Film:**
- Properties: Flexible film, 80–85 % light transmittance, UV-stabilized.
- Advantages: Cheapest option, easy to install, good for polyhouses.
- Limitations: Short lifespan (2–4 years), susceptible to wind damage.

**3. Polycarbonate (PC) Sheets:**
- Properties: Rigid, twin-wall, 75–85 % transmittance, 10–15 year lifespan.
- Advantages: Excellent thermal insulation, impact-resistant, lightweight.
- Limitations: Moderate cost, yellows with age if not UV-coated.

**4. Acrylic:**
- Properties: UV-stable, transparent, high clarity.
- Advantages: Good light transmission, weather-resistant.
- Limitations: Expensive, flammable.

**5. Fiberglass Reinforced Plastic (FRP):**
- Properties: Translucent panel, 80–85 % transmission.
- Advantages: Durable, lightweight.
- Limitations: Yellows over time, lower clarity than glass.

**6. Shade Net:**
- Properties: Woven HDPE fabric, available in 30 %, 50 %, 75 %, 90 % shade.
- Advantages: Cheap, reduces heat load, ideal for tropical regions.
- Limitations: Not frost protection; not suitable for winter cultivation.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Points to Memorize Before the Exam:**

- [ ] **First greenhouse prototype:** Roman *specularia* (30 AD, Tiberius).
- [ ] **First scientific greenhouse:** Padua Botanical Garden, **1599**.
- [ ] **First heated greenhouse:** UK, **1692**.
- [ ] **Term "greenhouse" first used:** **1684** (UK).
- [ ] **Polyethylene film introduced:** **1950s** (USA) — the affordable-revolution moment.
- [ ] **India's polyhouse adoption:** **1980s** (NHB + IARI).
- [ ] **PAR range:** **400–700 nm**.
- [ ] **Optimum CO₂ for enrichment:** **800–1200 ppm**.
- [ ] **Optimum RH:** **60–80 %**.
- [ ] **Most common shape in India:** **Even-span polyhouse** (or Quonset).
- [ ] **Highest light transmittance covering:** **Glass** (88–92 %).
- [ ] **Cheapest covering:** **Polyethylene film**.
- [ ] **Best for snow-load regions:** **Gothic arch**.
- [ ] **Best for tropical regions:** **Sawtooth** (fixed natural ventilation).
- [ ] **Most energy-efficient large structure:** **Gutter-connected ridge & furrow**.
- [ ] **Key formula to remember:** $A_g = L \cdot W$, $V = A_g \cdot H$, $I = S \cdot \tau$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic — "SCTSF" for Classification Bases:**
> **S**hape | **C**overing | **T**emperature | **S**tructure | **F**unction
> (also known as the 5-axis classification — at least one of these appears in every KTU Module 1 question paper.)

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Importance, Function, and Features of Greenhouse
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEAGT721 - DESIGN AND MAINTENANCE OF GREEN HOUSE
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Importance, Function, and Features of Greenhouse

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Greenhouse (KTU 2024 Definition):**
> A greenhouse is a framed or inflated structure covered with a transparent or translucent material (such as glass, polyethylene film, or polycarbonate panels) in which environmental conditions — including temperature, humidity, light intensity, carbon dioxide concentration, and ventilation — can be regulated partially or fully to permit optimized cultivation of plants, vegetables, flowers, and fruits throughout the year, irrespective of the external climatic variations.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** context for *OEAGT721 – Design and Maintenance of Greenhouse*, the term is treated from an **engineering design perspective** rather than purely an agricultural one. The structure is a **controlled-environment agriculture (CEA) facility** where the design, material selection, structural loads, and HVAC parameters are systematically engineered.

> [!NOTE]
> **Synonyms used in KTU syllabus:** Glasshouse, Polyhouse, Controlled-Environment Structure, Protected Cultivation Unit.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **car parked in summer with its windows closed**. The inside of the car becomes dramatically hotter than the outside air. This happens because **short-wavelength solar radiation** enters through the glass, gets absorbed by the interior surfaces (seats, dashboard), and is re-radiated as **long-wavelength infrared radiation**, which cannot escape back through the glass. The trapped energy raises the internal temperature.

A **greenhouse works on exactly this same principle** — but instead of an uncomfortable car, it creates a **comfortable micro-climate for plants**. The transparent covering acts as a **selective filter** — letting sunlight in but trapping the warmth.

> [!NOTE]
> This phenomenon is called the **"Greenhouse Effect"** at the structure level. (Note: This is different from the atmospheric greenhouse effect causing global warming, though the underlying physics is similar.)

Another intuitive analogy: **Think of the greenhouse as a "sweater for plants."** Just as a sweater traps your body heat to keep you warm in cold weather, the greenhouse covering traps solar energy to keep plants warm — but the farmer (engineer) can open vents, switch on fans, or activate shade nets to **prevent it from getting too warm**, just like you would unzip a sweater if you got too hot.

---

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

The following key metrics are **bolded** as they are the standard reference values used across KTU questions and the protected-cultivation industry:

- **PAR (Photosynthetically Active Radiation):** **400 – 700 nm** wavelength range
- **Solar transmissivity of standard greenhouse glass:** **0.85 – 0.90 (85–90 %)**
- **Solar transmissivity of polyethylene film (200 µm):** **0.80 – 0.88 (80–88 %)**
- **Ideal inside temperature for tropical crops:** **20 – 30 °C**
- **Ideal Relative Humidity (RH) for plant growth:** **60 – 80 %**
- **CO₂ enrichment level:** **800 – 1000 ppm** (vs. ambient **~415 ppm**)
- **Stefan–Boltzmann constant (σ):** **5.67 × 10⁻⁸ W/m²K⁴**

---

### 1.4 Why Study This Topic? (KTU 2024 Context)

In the **2024 KTU NEP-aligned syllabus**, this opening topic of Module 1 sets the **conceptual foundation** for the entire course. Subsequent modules will deal with:
- **Module 2:** Design of greenhouse structures
- **Module 3:** Materials and covering technologies
- **Module 4:** Environmental control systems (HVAC, fertigation)
- **Module 5:** Maintenance and lifecycle management

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Without a clear understanding of *what a greenhouse does* (function) and *why it matters* (importance), students will struggle to grasp the **why behind the design choices** in later modules. This topic is the **anchor** of the course.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Importance of Greenhouse — Structured Analysis

The importance of greenhouses can be grouped into **six engineering-agronomic dimensions**, each of which is a potential KTU short-answer target.

#### 2.1.1 Year-Round / Off-Season Production
- Greenhouses **decouple crop production from seasonal constraints**.
- A tomato crop that normally grows only in winter in North India can be grown **anytime in a polyhouse** in Kerala.
- This stabilizes **farmer income** and ensures **continuous market supply**.

#### 2.1.2 Higher Yield per Unit Area
- Controlled environment increases **biomass accumulation rate**.
- Yield increase documented in literature: **2× to 10×** compared to open-field cultivation (depending on crop and technology).
- Hydroponic greenhouses (fully controlled) can yield **20×** more.

#### 2.1.3 Efficient Use of Inputs (Water, Fertilizer, Pesticide)
- Greenhouse cultivation uses **drip irrigation + fertigation**.
- Water savings: up to **70 %** vs. open-field flood irrigation.
- Pesticide use reduced (due to physical barrier) — supports **pesticide-free / organic** production.

#### 2.1.4 Protection from Abiotic and Biotic Stresses
- **Abiotic protection:** Wind, heavy rain, hail, frost, extreme heat.
- **Biotic protection:** Insects, birds, large animals.

#### 2.1.5 Quality and Uniformity of Produce
- Greenhouse produce has **uniform size, color, and grade**.
- Premium markets (export, supermarkets, hotels) demand such quality.
- Less **post-harvest loss**.

#### 2.1.6 Employment and Rural Economy
- Greenhouse farming is **labor-intensive** (pruning, training, harvesting, packing).
- Creates **rural employment**, especially for women and educated youth.
- Aligns with **KTU's Outcome-Based Education (OBE)** goal of socially-relevant engineering.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** In Part A (3-mark) questions, the most common question on this topic is *"List any six importance/advantages of greenhouse cultivation."* Memorize the **six points above**.

---

### 2.2 Functions of a Greenhouse

The **functions** describe *what a greenhouse is supposed to do* — these are engineering deliverables.

A greenhouse must perform the following **seven core functions**:

1. **Transmit photosynthetically active radiation (PAR)** — let the useful sunlight reach the plants.
2. **Trap long-wave infrared radiation** — to maintain a warmer internal micro-climate.
3. **Provide a controlled thermal environment** — heating in winter, cooling in summer.
4. **Provide a controlled humidity environment** — humidification or dehumidification.
5. **Maintain elevated CO₂ levels** — through enrichment systems.
6. **Allow controlled ventilation** — natural (vents) or mechanical (fans).
7. **Provide structural protection** — against wind, rain, snow, and pests.

---

### 2.3 Features of a Greenhouse

"Features" refer to the **design attributes** that make a greenhouse capable of performing the above functions. These are the *physical, structural, and environmental characteristics*.

| # | Feature Category | Specific Feature | Engineering / Functional Role |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Structural** | Frame material (GI pipe, aluminium, wood) | Provides load-bearing skeleton |
| 2 | **Structural** | Span width, gutter height, ridge height | Determines volume, light penetration |
| 3 | **Structural** | Shape (Quonset, Gothic, Gable, Sawtooth) | Affects snow/rain shedding, internal airflow |
| 4 | **Covering** | Glazing material (Glass / Polyethylene / Polycarbonate / Acrylic) | Controls light, heat, and UV transmission |
| 5 | **Covering** | Double-layer (inflated) poly film | Adds insulation, reduces heat loss |
| 6 | **Ventilation** | Roof vents, side vents | Enables natural cross-ventilation |
| 7 | **Ventilation** | Exhaust fans + cooling pads | Mechanical (forced) ventilation |
| 8 | **Thermal Control** | Heating system (tubular heaters, hot water pipes) | Maintains temperature in cold climates |
| 9 | **Thermal Control** | Shading nets / thermal screens | Reduces solar heat gain in summer |
| 10 | **Irrigation** | Drip / mist / fogger system | Delivers water and nutrients precisely |
| 11 | **Environmental Sensors** | Temperature, RH, CO₂, PAR sensors | Enables automated control |
| 12 | **Automation** | Climate controllers, fertigation units | Reduces labor, ensures precision |
| 13 | **Foundation** | Plinth / pillars / ground anchoring | Transfers structural loads to soil |
| 14 | **Drainage** | Gutter system, floor slope | Manages rainwater and prevents waterlogging |
| 15 | **Benches/Beds** | Raised platforms, ground beds | Organizes crop layout, ergonomics |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Tip:** In a 14-mark question, a structured table like this is worth **3–4 marks** by itself if well presented.

---

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table summarises all **standard formulas** required for problems on this topic. These appear in design and heat-transfer sub-modules but originate from the **fundamental principles** introduced in this opening topic.

| # | Formula / Concept | Equation | Variables & Units | Where Used |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Greenhouse Effect (Stefan–Boltzmann form)** | $$q = \varepsilon \sigma T^4$$ | q = radiated heat flux (W/m²), ε = emissivity, σ = 5.67×10⁻⁸ W/m²K⁴, T = surface temperature (K) | Heat transfer through covering |
| 2 | **Net Solar Energy Transmitted** | $$Q_{\text{trans}} = I \cdot \tau \cdot A$$ | I = incident solar radiation (W/m²), τ = transmissivity, A = area (m²) | Sizing heating/cooling loads |
| 3 | **Thermal Resistance (R-value)** | $$R = \frac{L}{k}$$ | L = thickness (m), k = thermal conductivity (W/m·K) | Heat loss through walls |
| 4 | **Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient (U)** | $$U = \frac{1}{R_{\text{total}}}$$ | R_total = sum of all resistances (m²K/W) | Heat loss calculation |
| 5 | **Heat Loss Through Covering** | $$Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T$$ | Q = heat loss (W), ΔT = inside–outside temp diff (K) | Sizing heater capacity |
| 6 | **Ventilation Rate (ACH)** | $$\text{ACH} = \frac{\dot{V}}{V_{\text{room}}}$$ | V̇ = volumetric flow (m³/h), V_room = greenhouse volume (m³) | Ventilation design |
| 7 | **Photosynthetic Photon Flux Density** | $$\text{PPFD} = \frac{I_{\text{PAR}} \cdot \tau}{h\nu}$$ | I_PAR = PAR irradiance (W/m²), hν = photon energy (J) | Lighting design |
| 8 | **Saturated Vapour Pressure (Antoine approx.)** | $$e_s(T) = 0.6108 \cdot e^{\left(\frac{17.27 T}{T + 237.3}\right)}$$ | e_s = sat. vapour pressure (kPa), T = air temp (°C) | Humidity control |
| 9 | **Relative Humidity** | $$\text{RH} = \frac{e}{e_s} \times 100\%$$ | e = actual vapour pressure (kPa) | Climate control |
| 10 | **Vapour Pressure Deficit (VPD)** | $$\text{VPD} = e_s \cdot \left(1 - \frac{\text{RH}}{100}\right)$$ | VPD (kPa) | Plant transpiration control |

> [!WARNING]
> **Formula Pitfall:** In the heat-loss equation, the **area A is the total surface area of the covering**, not the floor area. Students commonly lose 1–2 marks by using the wrong area.

---

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application |
|---|---|
| **Agriculture** | Year-round vegetable & flower production in Kerala's humid tropical climate |
| **Horticulture** | Cut-flower export (roses, gerberas) from polyhouses in Wayanad, Idukki |
| **Plant Tissue Culture** | Acclimatization of tissue-cultured plantlets in controlled greenhouse |
| **Research** | Phenotyping, climate-change impact studies at ICAR institutes |
| **Nurseries** | Commercial seedling production (vegetable, forestry, ornamental) |
| **Space / Vertical Farming** | Engineered CEA systems (advanced greenhouses) for urban agriculture |
| **Aquaponics / Hydroponics** | Greenhouse-integrated systems for fish + vegetable production |

> [!NOTE]
> The **Kerala State Horticulture Mission** actively promotes polyhouse farming with **50–90 % subsidy** under the *Rashtriya Krishi Vikas Yojana (RKVY)* and *MIDH (Mission for Integrated Development of Horticulture)* — a real-world fact students can quote in answers to earn appreciation marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 The Greenhouse Effect — Detailed Derivation

This is a **KTU-favourite derivation** worth 5–7 marks. Let us derive the **net energy balance** of a greenhouse as a function of incident solar radiation.

#### 3.1.1 Step 1: Define the System
Consider a simple greenhouse:
- Transparent covering with transmissivity **τ** (for shortwave solar) and emissivity **ε** (for longwave IR).
- Inside air at temperature **Tᵢ** (K).
- Outside air at temperature **T₀** (K).
- Inside ground/plant canopy absorbing solar radiation.
- Internal air volume acts as a heat reservoir.

#### 3.1.2 Step 2: Shortwave (Solar) Energy Balance
Solar irradiance **I** (W/m²) falls on the roof of area **A**.

The fraction transmitted inside is **τ × I**. Some is reflected (**(1−τ) × I**). The transmitted portion is **absorbed by the floor, plants, and internal surfaces**.

$$
Q_{\text{solar in}} = \tau \cdot I \cdot A
$$

#### 3.1.3 Step 3: Longwave (Infrared) Energy Balance
The warm inside surfaces radiate longwave IR:

$$
Q_{\text{IR out from inside}} = \varepsilon \sigma T_i^4 A
$$

The outside (cold sky) radiates inward (small, can be neglected in simple analysis):

$$
Q_{\text{IR in from outside}} \approx 0
$$

The covering itself absorbs and re-radiates — for a single-layer covering, the net heat lost by longwave radiation to the sky is:

$$
Q_{\text{IR lost}} = \varepsilon \sigma (T_i^4 - T_{\text{sky}}^4) A
$$

#### 3.1.4 Step 4: Convective Heat Loss
The internal air loses heat to the outside through the covering by convection:

$$
Q_{\text{conv}} = U_c A (T_i - T_o)
$$

where **U_c** is the convective heat transfer coefficient (W/m²K).

#### 3.1.5 Step 5: Steady-State Energy Balance
At steady state, **energy in = energy out**:

$$
\tau \cdot I \cdot A = \varepsilon \sigma (T_i^4 - T_{\text{sky}}^4) A + U_c A (T_i - T_o)
$$

Dividing throughout by **A**:

$$
\tau \cdot I = \varepsilon \sigma (T_i^4 - T_{\text{sky}}^4) + U_c (T_i - T_o)
$$

#### 3.1.6 Step 6: Solving for Internal Temperature
For typical daytime values (I = 600 W/m², τ = 0.85, ε = 0.1 for glass, U_c = 6 W/m²K, T_o = 298 K, T_sky = 280 K):

$$
\tau \cdot I = 0.85 \times 600 = 510 \text{ W/m}^2
$$

$$
\varepsilon \sigma (T_i^4 - T_{\text{sky}}^4) = 0.1 \times 5.67 \times 10^{-8} (T_i^4 - 280^4)
$$

$$
= 5.67 \times 10^{-9} (T_i^4 - 6.146 \times 10^9)
$$

Let us assume **Tᵢ = 308 K (35 °C)** as a trial:

$$
T_i^4 = 308^4 = 8.998 \times 10^9
$$

$$
\varepsilon \sigma (T_i^4 - T_{\text{sky}}^4) = 5.67 \times 10^{-9} (8.998 \times 10^9 - 6.146 \times 10^9)
$$

$$
= 5.67 \times 10^{-9} \times 2.852 \times 10^9
$$

$$
= 16.17 \text{ W/m}^2
$$

$$
U_c (T_i - T_o) = 6 \times (308 - 298) = 60 \text{ W/m}^2
$$

Total RHS = 16.17 + 60 = **76.17 W/m²**

Since **LHS = 510 W/m²**, the balance is not satisfied — meaning **Tᵢ is actually much higher** than 308 K at steady state. This shows that a real greenhouse reaches a much higher internal temperature, which is why **ventilation/cooling is essential**.

> [!NOTE]
> The above derivation explains **why greenhouses need cooling systems** — a natural link to Module 4 (Environmental Control).

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Why Features Matter (Engineering Logic)

This matrix links each **function** to the **feature that performs it**, and to the **physics behind it**. This is the type of high-density content KTU 14-mark questions expect.

| Required Function | Feature that Achieves It | Underlying Physics | Engineering Trade-off |
|---|---|---|---|
| Transmit sunlight | Transparent covering (glass, PE film) | Optical transmission in 400–700 nm range | Higher transmission = lower structural strength |
| Trap heat | Same covering | Longwave IR opacity (low ε for shortwave, high ε for longwave) | Single material rarely perfect — multi-layer helps |
| Maintain temperature | Heating pipes / exhaust fans | First Law of Thermodynamics | Energy cost vs. crop value |
| Control humidity | Foggers / ventilation | Psychrometrics (VPD control) | High RH → fungal diseases; Low RH → stress |
| CO₂ enrichment | CO₂ burners / tanks + distribution | Photosynthesis stoichiometry | Leaks reduce efficiency; safety at >5000 ppm |
| Structural safety | GI pipe frame, anchor bolts | Strength of materials, wind/snow load codes (IS 875) | Cost vs. durability |
| Pest protection | Insect-proof nets on vents | Physical barrier | Reduces ventilation — increases heat |
| Automation | Sensors + PLC / climate controller | Feedback control theory | Capital cost vs. precision |

---

### 3.3 Derivation — Air Exchange Rate for Ventilation

The **ACH (Air Changes per Hour)** formula is derived as follows:

#### Step 1: Total inside volume

$$
V_{\text{room}} = L \times W \times H
$$

where **L** = length, **W** = width, **H** = average height (all in metres).

#### Step 2: Required ventilation flow

To remove excess heat **Q_excess** (W):

$$
\dot{V} = \frac{Q_{\text{excess}}}{\rho \cdot c_p \cdot (T_i - T_o)}
$$

where **ρ** = air density (**1.2 kg/m³**), **c_p** = specific heat (**1005 J/kg·K**).

#### Step 3: ACH

$$
\text{ACH} = \frac{\dot{V}}{V_{\text{room}}} \times 3600
$$

For a typical **500 m² polyhouse** with **Q_excess = 50 kW**, **Tᵢ − Tₒ = 5 K**:

$$
\dot{V} = \frac{50000}{1.2 \times 1005 \times 5} = \frac{50000}{6030} = 8.29 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
$$

$$
= 8.29 \times 3600 = 29,844 \text{ m}^3/\text{h}
$$

If V_room = 500 × 6 × 4 = **12,000 m³**:

$$
\text{ACH} = \frac{29844}{12000} \approx 2.49 \text{ per hour}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> For tropical greenhouses in Kerala, recommended ACH = **20–40 per hour** during peak summer. The example above shows that **natural ventilation alone is insufficient** during peak heat — **mechanical ventilation (fans + cooling pads) is essential**.

---

### 3.4 Worked Example — Sizing a Heating System (14-mark style)

**Problem:** A greenhouse has a floor area of **400 m²** and an average height of **4 m**. The covering is single-layer polyethylene film with **U = 6.5 W/m²K**. The total exposed surface area is **1200 m²**. The desired inside temperature is **18 °C** and the minimum outside temperature is **5 °C**. Estimate the heating load.

#### Solution:

**Step 1: Temperature difference**

$$
\Delta T = T_i - T_o = 18 - 5 = 13 \text{ K}
$$

**Step 2: Heat loss through covering**

$$
Q_{\text{loss}} = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T = 6.5 \times 1200 \times 13
$$

$$
= 6.5 \times 1200 \times 13
$$

$$
= 7800 \times 13
$$

$$
= 101,400 \text{ W} \approx 101.4 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 3: Add 10 % safety margin**

$$
Q_{\text{heater}} = 101.4 \times 1.1 = 111.54 \text{ kW}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This 4-step problem-solving structure is exactly what KTU board examiners expect. Always state: (1) Given data, (2) Formula, (3) Substitution, (4) Final answer with units.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Greenhouse Functional Architecture — Block Diagram

The following Mermaid block diagrams the **functional flow of a greenhouse** — how energy, mass, and information move through the system.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Sun[/Solar Radiation I W per m2/]
    OutsideAir[/Outside Air T0 RH0 CO2 0/]
    
    subgraph CoveringSystem["Covering System"]
        Roof[Transparent Roof tau 0.85]
        Walls[Side Walls]
        Vents[Vents and Fans]
    end
    
    subgraph InsideAir["Inside Air Volume"]
        TempSensor[Temperature Sensor]
        RHSensor[Humidity Sensor]
        CO2Sensor[CO2 Sensor]
        PARSensor[PAR Sensor]
    end
    
    subgraph ControlUnit["Climate Controller PLC"]
        HeaterCmd[Heater ON OFF]
        FanCmd[Fans ON OFF]
        VentCmd[Vents Open Close]
        CO2Cmd[CO2 Injector]
    end
    
    subgraph PlantSystem["Plant Canopy"]
        Plants[Crops and Beds]
        Drip[Drip Fertigation]
    end
    
    Sun --> Roof
    Roof --> Plants
    OutsideAir --> Walls
    Walls --> Plants
    Vents --> Plants
    
    Plants --> TempSensor
    Plants --> RHSensor
    Plants --> CO2Sensor
    Plants --> PARSensor
    
    TempSensor --> ControlUnit
    RHSensor --> ControlUnit
    CO2Sensor --> ControlUnit
    PARSensor --> ControlUnit
    
    ControlUnit --> HeaterCmd
    ControlUnit --> FanCmd
    ControlUnit --> VentCmd
    ControlUnit --> CO2Cmd
    
    HeaterCmd --> Plants
    FanCmd --> Vents
    VentCmd --> Vents
    CO2Cmd --> Plants
    Drip --> Plants
    
    style CoveringSystem fill:#e1f5e1,stroke:#2e7d32
    style InsideAir fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#1565c0
    style ControlUnit fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100
    style PlantSystem fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#6a1b9a
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this diagram:** Follow the arrows from left (inputs: Sun, Outside Air) → Covering (boundary) → Inside Air (sensors) → Control Unit (decisions) → back to plant system (actuators). This is a classic **closed-loop feedback control system** — a key engineering concept.

---

### 4.2 Greenhouse Types — Comparison Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Greenhouse Types]
    A --> B[By Shape]
    A --> C[By Covering]
    A --> D[By Structure]
    A --> E[By Technology Level]
    
    B --> B1[Quonset]
    B --> B2[Gothic Arch]
    B --> B3[Gable Roof]
    B --> B4[Sawtooth]
    B --> B5[Lean To]
    
    C --> C1[Glass Glazed]
    C --> C2[Polyethylene Film]
    C --> C3[Polycarbonate Panel]
    C --> C4[Acrylic Sheet]
    C --> C5[ETFE Film]
    
    D --> D1[Free Standing]
    D --> D2[Connected Gutter]
    D --> D3[High Tech Venlo]
    
    E --> E1[Low Tech Polyhouse]
    E --> E2[Medium Tech]
    E --> E3[High Tech Fully Automated]
    
    style A fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1
    style B fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
    style C fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17
    style D fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c
    style E fill:#f8bbd0,stroke:#880e4f
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** In 14-mark questions, a well-labelled classification diagram (like the one above) earns **2–3 marks** as a visual summary. Always close a long-answer with a **diagram or table**.

---

### 4.3 Heat Transfer Pathways — Block Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Inside["Inside Greenhouse"]
        Plants2[Plant Canopy Ti]
        Air2[Air Mass Ti]
    end
    
    subgraph Cover["Transparent Cover"]
        CoverLayer[Single or Double Layer]
    end
    
    subgraph Outside["Outside Environment"]
        Sky[T_sky approx 20 to 30 K below To]
        OutsideAir2[Ambient Air To]
    end
    
    Plants2 -- Longwave IR epsilon sigma T4 --> CoverLayer
    Air2 -- Convection h T i minus T cover --> CoverLayer
    CoverLayer -- Conduction k by L --> CoverLayer

    CoverLayer -- Longwave IR epsilon sigma T cover 4 --> Sky
    OutsideAir2 -- Wind Infiltration --> Air2
```

### 4.5 Importance–Function–Feature Logic Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Imp[Importance Why]
    Func[Function What it Does]
    Feat[Feature How it is Done]
    
    Imp --> I1[Year Round Production]

    Imp --> I4[Pest and Weather Protection]
    
    Func --> F1[Transmit PAR]
    Func --> F2[Trap Longwave IR]

    Feat --> FT2[Heating and Cooling]
    Feat --> FT3[Sensors and Automation]

```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorization Aid:** When answering a 14-mark question on this topic, **always start with the triangle: Importance → Function → Feature**. This is the **highest-scoring answer structure** in the KTU evaluation rubric.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer)

> **Q1.** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**
> Define a greenhouse. List any four functions of a greenhouse.

**Model Answer:**

A **greenhouse** is a framed or inflated structure covered with transparent or translucent material in which climatic parameters like temperature, humidity, light, and CO₂ can be controlled or partially regulated to permit plant growth under optimized conditions.

**Four functions:**
1. Transmit **photosynthetically active radiation (PAR)** to crops.
2. **Trap longwave infrared radiation** to maintain a warmer micro-climate (greenhouse effect).
3. **Provide controlled thermal environment** through heating and cooling.
4. **Protect crops** from wind, rain, pests, and extreme weather.

---

> **Q2.** **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Question]**
> List any six advantages / importance of greenhouse cultivation.

**Model Answer:**

1. **Year-round / off-season production** decoupled from climate.
2. **Higher yield per unit area** (2× to 10× open field).
3. **Water savings up to 70 %** through drip and fertigation.
4. **Reduced pesticide use** due to physical protection.
5. **Better quality and uniformity** of produce — premium market access.
6. **Employment generation** in rural and peri-urban areas.

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**
> **(a)** [7 Marks] Explain in detail the **importance of greenhouse cultivation** in modern agriculture. List and briefly describe **at least six points** with engineering relevance.
>
> **(b)** [7 Marks] With the help of a **neat energy-balance equation**, derive the relationship governing the **greenhouse effect** and explain how it leads to a higher internal temperature.

##### Model Answer — Part (a):

**Importance of greenhouse cultivation:**

1. **Off-season and year-round production:** Greenhouses enable cultivation outside the natural growing season, ensuring continuous market supply and stable income. (1 mark)
2. **Higher productivity:** Controlled environment optimises photosynthesis, often doubling or tripling yields compared to open-field farming. (1 mark)
3. **Water-use efficiency:** Combined with drip and fogger systems, greenhouses use up to **70 % less water** than flood irrigation. (1 mark)
4. **Pesticide-free / organic production:** The enclosed structure acts as a physical barrier, drastically reducing pest incidence and chemical use. (1 mark)
5. **Quality and export potential:** Uniform size, colour, and grade of produce make greenhouse output suitable for export, hospitality, and supermarket supply chains. (1 mark)
6. **Climate resilience:** Protection from extreme weather — heat waves, unseasonal rain, hail, frost — ensures crop survival. (1 mark)
7. **Employment and rural development:** Greenhouse operations are labor-intensive, supporting the rural economy, especially women-led enterprises. (1 mark)

##### Model Answer — Part (b):

**Derivation of the greenhouse effect energy balance:**

Consider a single-span greenhouse with transparent covering, internal air temperature **Tᵢ** and outside air temperature **Tₒ**. Let the incident solar radiation be **I** and the transmissivity be **τ**.

The **shortwave solar energy entering** the greenhouse per unit floor area:

$$
Q_{\text{solar}} = \tau \cdot I
$$

The **longwave IR energy re-radiated by the warm internal surfaces and lost through the cover**:

$$
Q_{\text{IR}} = \varepsilon \sigma (T_i^4 - T_{\text{sky}}^4)
$$

The **convective heat loss** through the cover to outside air:

$$
Q_{\text{conv}} = U_c (T_i - T_o)
$$

At **steady state**, energy in equals energy out:

$$
\tau I = \varepsilon \sigma (T_i^4 - T_{\text{sky}}^4) + U_c (T_i - T_o)
$$

Rearranging for Tᵢ:

$$
\tau I - U_c T_o = \varepsilon \sigma (T_i^4 - T_{\text{sky}}^4) + U_c T_i
$$

Since the **right-hand side increases with Tᵢ** while the left-hand side is fixed, the **only solution is Tᵢ > Tₒ** — i.e., the inside is warmer than outside. (3 marks for derivation, 1 mark for interpretation)

**Numerical example:** With I = 600 W/m², τ = 0.85, U_c = 6 W/m²K, Tₒ = 298 K, T_sky = 280 K, solving iteratively yields Tᵢ ≈ 330 K (57 °C) — far above plant tolerance, hence the need for **ventilation/cooling systems**. (2 marks for example)

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**
> **(a)** [7 Marks] Discuss the **features of a modern greenhouse** with respect to its **structure, covering, ventilation, and environmental control systems**. Use a comparative table.
>
> **(b)** [7 Marks] Explain the **functions of a greenhouse** in detail and describe how each function is achieved by a specific feature.

##### Model Answer — Part (a):

**Features of a modern greenhouse** — a structured comparison:

| Category | Feature | Engineering Role |
|---|---|---|
| **Structure** | Galvanized Iron (GI) pipe frame | Corrosion-resistant, strong, cost-effective for Kerala's humid climate. |
| **Structure** | Gothic or Gable roof | Better rain shedding, more uniform light distribution than flat roof. |
| **Covering** | 200 µm UV-stabilized polyethylene film | High PAR transmission (~85 %), low cost, easy to replace. |
| **Covering** | Double-layer inflated poly film | Better insulation (R-value improved by 40 %), reduces heating cost. |
| **Ventilation** | Roof and side vents (automated) | Natural ventilation in mild weather; rain-sensor controlled. |
| **Ventilation** | Exhaust fans + cellulose cooling pads | Mechanical ventilation; reduces inside temp by 8–10 °C. |
| **Control** | Climate controller + sensors | Maintains setpoint for T, RH, CO₂; data logging. |
| **Irrigation** | Drip + fertigation unit | Delivers water and nutrients at root zone, minimizes wastage. |

(1 mark each for 7 rows = 7 marks)

##### Model Answer — Part (b):

**Functions and corresponding features:**

1. **Transmit sunlight** → **Feature: Transparent covering** (glass / PE film). Achieved because covering materials have high transmissivity in the 400–700 nm PAR band. (1 mark)

2. **Trap longwave heat** → **Feature: Low-emissivity covering / double layer.** Achieved by IR-opaque material reducing longwave transmission. (1 mark)

3. **Provide heating in winter** → **Feature: Tubular heater / hot water piping system.** Thermostatically controlled. (1 mark)

4. **Provide cooling in summer** → **Feature: Roof vents + exhaust fans + evaporative cooling pad.** Combined natural and forced ventilation. (1 mark)

5. **Humidity control** → **Feature: Fogger / mist system + ventilation.** Maintains 60–80 % RH. (1 mark)

6. **CO₂ enrichment** → **Feature: CO₂ tank / generator + distribution tubes.** Raises ambient CO₂ from ~415 to ~1000 ppm. (1 mark)

7. **Plant protection** → **Feature: Insect-proof nets on vents + UV-stabilized covering.** Physical barrier. (1 mark)

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing "function" with "feature".** Function = *what* the greenhouse does; Feature = *how* it does it. Examiners specifically test this distinction.
> 2. **Forgetting units in numerical answers** — always write W, m², K, °C, kg/m³, etc.
> 3. **Not labelling Mermaid/diagram axes or blocks** — unlabelled diagrams get 0 marks.
> 4. **Writing only 4–5 importance points** when 6 are asked — partial marking is strict in KTU.
> 5. **Using "it traps heat" without explaining the *longwave IR opacity* mechanism** — vague answers score 0–1 mark instead of 2–3.
> 6. **Conflating greenhouse structural design with agronomic practices** — KTU is an engineering course, focus on design and systems, not just cultivation tips.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 1, Topic 1**

- [ ] **Definition:** Greenhouse = transparent-enclosed controlled-environment agriculture (CEA) structure.
- [ ] **Principle:** Shortwave solar radiation enters; longwave IR is trapped → internal temperature rises.
- [ ] **Six importance points:** Off-season production, higher yield, water/fertilizer efficiency, pest/weather protection, quality produce, rural employment.
- [ ] **Seven core functions:** PAR transmission, IR trapping, temperature control, humidity control, CO₂ enrichment, controlled ventilation, structural protection.
- [ ] **Fifteen key features** (memorize categories, not all 15 items): structure, covering, ventilation, thermal control, irrigation, sensors, automation, foundation, drainage, benches.
- [ ] **Key numbers to remember:** PAR = 400–700 nm; ideal T = 20–30 °C; ideal RH = 60–80 %; CO₂ enrichment = 800–1000 ppm; transmissivity of glass = 0.85–0.90; transmissivity of PE film = 0.80–0.88.
- [ ] **Formulas to know:** Stefan–Boltzmann ($q = \varepsilon \sigma T^4$), Net solar ($Q = I \tau A$), Heat loss ($Q = UA \Delta T$), ACH ($\dot{V}/V_{\text{room}}$), VPD formula.
- [ ] **Greenhouse effect energy balance:** $\tau I = \varepsilon \sigma (T_i^4 - T_{\text{sky}}^4) + U_c (T_i - T_o)$
- [ ] **Designate Tᵢ > Tₒ** — the only solution at steady state, which proves the heating effect.
- [ ] **Distinguish clearly:** Importance (WHY) vs. Function (WHAT) vs. Feature (HOW) — this triangle is the answer template.
- [ ] **Mention Kerala-specific examples** (Wayanad rose polyhouses, Idukki spice polyhouses) to score higher-order Bloom's marks (Application / Analysis level).
- [ ] **Mention government schemes** (RKVY, MIDH, NHM subsidies) when discussing importance for socio-economic impact.
- [ ] **Always close 14-mark answers with a labelled diagram or table** — this can be worth 2–3 marks by itself.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU Tip:** This topic is **fundamental** — every subsequent module builds on it. Take time to internalize the **Importance → Function → Feature** logic chain. Once mastered, answering any question on this topic becomes a structured exercise rather than rote memorization.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Scope and Development of Greenhouse Technology, Location, Planning, and Various Components of Greenhouse
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEAGT721 - DESIGN AND MAINTENANCE OF GREEN HOUSE
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Scope and Development of Greenhouse Technology, Location, Planning, and Various Components of Greenhouse

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 What is a Greenhouse? (KTU Formal Definition)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Greenhouse Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> A **greenhouse** (also called a *glasshouse*, *hothouse*, or *controlled environment agriculture (CEA) structure*) is a **transparent enclosed structure** designed to provide a **regulated micro-climatic environment** for the off-season or year-round cultivation of horticultural crops, by **trapping solar short-wave radiation**, converting it into **long-wave thermal radiation**, and using the **convection, conduction, and radiation** principles to maintain optimum temperature, humidity, light, and CO₂ levels for plant growth.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, the term **Protected Cultivation** is used as an umbrella that includes greenhouses, shade nets, poly-houses, and poly-tunnels.

---

## 1.2 The Conceptual Analogy — The "Car Dashboard Effect"

Imagine you leave your car parked outside on a sunny Kerala afternoon. You open the door after 1 hour, and the interior feels *much hotter* than the outside air. Why?

**Because sunlight enters through the glass, hits the dark dashboard (absorbing the energy), warms up, and tries to re-radiate as heat. But the glass is *opaque* to this long-wave (infrared) heat radiation — it cannot escape back. The car is acting exactly like a greenhouse.**

**Engineering Interpretation:**
| Car Component | Greenhouse Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Glass windows | Transparent cover (polyethylene / glass / polycarbonate) |
| Dashboard | Soil + plants + floor |
| Trapped hot air | Convective heat trapped inside |
| Cannot escape IR | Selective absorption of long-wave radiation |

So a greenhouse is essentially **"a transparent envelope that lets light IN but does not let heat OUT."**

---

## 1.3 Scope and Development of Greenhouse Technology

### 1.3.1 Global Development Timeline

> [!NOTE]
> **Historical Milestones (must remember for 3-mark KTU questions):**

| Era | Development |
|---|---|
| **Roman Era (14 AD)** | Tiberius Caesar grew cucumbers under transparent mica sheets |
| **13th Century** | Italians built structures called "Giardini Botanici" |
| **1599** | First practical greenhouse built in Holland for medicinal plants |
| **1800s** | Glass greenhouses became status symbols in Europe |
| **1949** | First polyethylene film discovered (transparent plastic revolution) |
| **1960s** | Modern poly-houses commercialized in Israel and Spain |
| **1980s** | Computer-controlled climate systems introduced |
| **2000s–Present** | AI-based climate control, vertical farms, fully automated hydroponic greenhouses |

### 1.3.2 Scope in India (KTU Kerala Context)

The Indian greenhouse market is projected to grow at a **CAGR of ~12.4%** (2024 estimate), with major clusters in:
- **Maharashtra, Karnataka, Tamil Nadu, Gujarat, Himachal Pradesh**
- **Kerala** is now emerging with high-tech climate-controlled greenhouses for **cut-flowers (rose, orchid, anthurium), strawberries, capsicum, tomatoes, lettuce, and exotic vegetables**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU-Specific Relevance:** Kerala's tropical-humid climate makes greenhouse technology ESSENTIAL because:
> - Heavy monsoon causes fungal diseases (controlled environment solves this)
> - High RH (Relative Humidity) reduces pollination → enclosed poly-houses help
> - High-value crops like **anthurium & orchids** fetch 5–10× field prices
> - Land scarcity → vertical/intensive CEA systems are viable

### 1.3.3 Major Applications

1. **Off-season cultivation** of vegetables (cucumber, capsicum in summer)
2. **Nursery raising** of vegetable seedlings and forest species
3. **Flower production** (rose, gerbera, carnation, orchid)
4. **Disease-free propagation** (Tissue-cultured banana, vanilla)
5. **Hydroponics / Soilless culture** (lettuce, spinach)
6. **Exotic fruit cultivation** (strawberry, kiwi, dragon fruit)
7. **Research and academic trials** under controlled environments
8. **Seed production** under isolated conditions

---

## 1.4 Why This Topic Matters in KTU Examinations

Based on KTU 2024 Scheme ESE (End Semester Evaluation) pattern:
- **Module 1** carries **~20–25%** weightage in the question paper.
- Direct questions on *components*, *site selection*, and *scope* are common in **Part A (3 marks)**.
- A combined **14-mark question** typically covers *site selection + components* OR *types of covering material + structural design*.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Physics of the Greenhouse Effect (Quantitative)

### 2.1.1 The Energy Balance Equation (Most Important Equation in Greenhouse Engineering)

For a **steady-state** greenhouse, the energy balance is given by:

$$Q_s = Q_c + Q_l + Q_v + Q_e + Q_s$$

Where every term has a strict definition (KTU examiner expects these units):

| Symbol | Meaning | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $Q_s$ | Total incoming solar radiation (heat gain) | W (Watts) |
| $Q_c$ | Conductive heat loss through cover & structure | W |
| $Q_l$ | Convective heat loss (infiltration + ventilation) | W |
| $Q_v$ | Latent heat loss due to evapotranspiration | W |
| $Q_e$ | Long-wave (thermal) radiation loss | W |
| $Q_s$ | Stored / utilized heat by plants and soil | W |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** If asked to *design for tropical Kerala*, note that $Q_s$ (solar gain) is much larger than $Q_c$ (conduction loss). So the **primary design problem is COOLING & VENTILATION, not HEATING** — the opposite of cold-climate greenhouse design.

---

## 2.2 Solar Geometry — The Foundation of Location & Orientation

### 2.2.1 Solar Declination Angle (δ)

The angle between the sun's rays and the equatorial plane varies throughout the year:

$$\delta = 23.45^\circ \times \sin\left[\frac{360}{365}(284 + n)\right]$$

Where $n$ = day number of the year (Jan 1 = 1, Dec 31 = 365).

| Date | $n$ | $\delta$ |
|---|---|---|
| Mar 21 (Vernal Equinox) | 80 | 0° |
| Jun 21 (Summer Solstice) | 172 | +23.45° |
| Sep 23 (Autumnal Equinox) | 266 | 0° |
| Dec 22 (Winter Solstice) | 356 | −23.45° |

### 2.2.2 Hour Angle (ω)

$$\omega = 15^\circ \times (t - 12)$$

Where $t$ = solar time in hours. At solar noon, $\omega = 0^\circ$.

### 2.2.3 Angle of Incidence on a Tilted Surface

For a greenhouse cover tilted at angle $\beta$ from horizontal, oriented at azimuth $\gamma$, at latitude $\phi$:

$$\cos\theta_i = \sin\delta\sin\phi\cos\beta - \sin\delta\cos\phi\sin\beta\cos\gamma + \cos\delta\cos\phi\cos\beta\cos\omega + \cos\delta\sin\phi\sin\beta\cos\gamma\cos\omega + \cos\delta\sin\beta\sin\gamma\sin\omega$$

> For a **symmetric greenhouse (gable roof, ridge running E-W)**, we typically use **β = latitude (φ) ± 10°** to capture maximum winter and summer sun.

### 2.2.4 Optimum Tilt Angle for Greenhouse Cover

For **year-round** maximum solar collection at latitude $\phi$:

$$\beta_{opt} = \phi + 15^\circ \quad \text{(winter biased)}$$

$$\beta_{opt} = \phi - 15^\circ \quad \text{(summer biased)}$$

$$\beta_{opt} = \phi \quad \text{(annual average)}$$

For Kerala (Kottayam, latitude $\phi \approx 9.6°$ N):
- Winter biased tilt $\approx 24.6°$
- Annual tilt $\approx 9.6°$ → greenhouse roofs are nearly flat in Kerala
- Summer biased tilt $\approx -5.4°$ → flat or slightly south-tilted

---

## 2.3 Heat Transfer Formulas (All KTU High-Yield)

### 2.3.1 Conductive Heat Loss

$$Q_c = U \times A \times \Delta T$$

Where:
- $U$ = Overall heat transfer coefficient of cover (W/m²·K)
- $A$ = Surface area (m²)
- $\Delta T$ = Temperature difference (K or °C)

> [!NOTE]
> **Typical U-values (must remember):**
> - **Single glass (4 mm):** $U \approx 6.4$ W/m²·K
> - **Double glass:** $U \approx 3.0$ W/m²·K
> - **Single polyethylene (0.2 mm):** $U \approx 6.8$ W/m²·K
> - **Double inflated PE film:** $U \approx 4.0$ W/m²·K
> - **8 mm Twin-wall polycarbonate:** $U \approx 3.2$ W/m²·K

### 2.3.2 Ventilation Heat Loss

$$Q_v = \dot{m} \times C_p \times \Delta T$$

Where $\dot{m} = \rho \times V \times A_v \times \text{ACH}$ (mass flow rate).

Or in simplified form:

$$Q_v = 1.2 \times V \times \text{ACH} \times \Delta T \quad \text{(W)}$$

Where $V$ = volume of greenhouse (m³), ACH = Air Changes per Hour.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Recommended ACH values (must remember):**
> - **Cold climates:** ACH = 0.5 – 1.0
> - **Tropical (Kerala):** ACH = 20 – 60 (heavy ventilation needed)
> - **Fan-and-pad cooled:** ACH = 60 – 100

### 2.3.3 Photosynthetically Active Radiation (PAR)

Plants use only **45–50%** of total solar radiation:

$$\text{PAR} = (0.45 \text{ to } 0.50) \times I_{global}$$

Where $I_{global}$ is total global solar radiation (W/m²).

> **PAR range required for most crops = 200 – 600 µmol/m²/s** (measured as PPFD — Photosynthetic Photon Flux Density)

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Exam-Ready Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD FORMULA TABLE — Memorize these for KTU ESE:**

| # | Formula | Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q_s = Q_c + Q_l + Q_v + Q_e + Q_s$ | Greenhouse heat balance |
| 2 | $\delta = 23.45° \sin[360/365(284+n)]$ | Solar declination |
| 3 | $\omega = 15°(t-12)$ | Hour angle |
| 4 | $\beta_{opt} = \phi \pm 15°$ | Cover tilt angle |
| 5 | $Q_c = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T$ | Conductive heat loss |
| 6 | $Q_v = 1.2 \cdot V \cdot \text{ACH} \cdot \Delta T$ | Ventilation heat loss |
| 7 | $\text{PAR} = 0.45 \cdot I_{global}$ | PAR estimation |
| 8 | $Q = A \times K \times I$ (K = 0.36 glass, 0.16 PE) | Solar heat gain through cover |
| 9 | $V = A_{floor} \times h$ | Greenhouse volume |
| 10 | $\text{ACH} = Q/V$ | Air changes per hour |

---

## 2.5 Location and Site Selection Criteria (Deep Dive)

### 2.5.1 Climatic Considerations

1. **Solar radiation availability** — minimum 4–6 kWh/m²/day average
2. **Wind speed and direction** — site must allow ventilation but protect from cyclonic winds
3. **Temperature range** — must be suitable for the crop; avoid frost pockets
4. **Rainfall and humidity** — high rainfall needs strong drainage; Kerala needs anti-fungal design
5. **Elevation** — affects UV intensity and temperature (every 1000 ft ≈ 1°C drop)

### 2.5.2 Physical & Infrastructure Considerations

| Criterion | KTU-Mentioned Threshold |
|---|---|
| **Land slope** | 1–2% gentle slope for drainage (max 5%) |
| **Water source** | Within 200 m; ≥ 5000 L/day for 1000 m² greenhouse |
| **Electricity** | 3-phase connection; stable voltage |
| **Road access** | Motorable road within 500 m for marketing |
| **Market distance** | Preferably < 50 km for perishables |
| **Labor availability** | Continuous labor for daily operations |
| **Soil bearing capacity** | > 100 kPa for foundation |
| **Distance from pollution** | > 1 km from industries (for clean CO₂ supply) |

### 2.5.3 Orientation Rules (Critical)

| Latitude | Ridge Orientation | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| **φ < 25° (Kerala, Tamil Nadu)** | **East-West (E-W)** ridge | Maximum winter sun perpendicular to ridge |
| **φ > 35° (North India)** | **North-South (N-S)** ridge | Uniform light distribution all day |

> For **Kerala (φ ≈ 10°N)** → greenhouse ridges should run **East-West**, with the **gable slope facing south** for maximum winter solar capture.

---

## 2.6 Various Components of Greenhouse (Deep Technical)

### 2.6.1 Structural Frame

| Material | Pros | Cons | Cost |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Galvanized Iron (GI) pipe** | Strong, durable, easy to fabricate | Conducts heat → bridges | Medium |
| **Aluminum alloy** | Light, rust-proof, no thermal bridging | Costly | High |
| **Wood (treated)** | Cheap, easy to shape | Rots, termite attack | Low |
| **Concrete + steel** | Permanent, for glasshouses | Not flexible | High |
| **Bamboo (low-cost)** | Cheap, rural areas | Short life (2–3 yr) | Very low |

### 2.6.2 Covering Material (Most Exam-Relevant)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU examiner almost always asks this in 14-mark Q:**

| Covering | Light Trans. | Lifespan | UV Stability | Cost/m² |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Float glass (4 mm)** | 88–90% | 25+ yr | Excellent | ₹400–600 |
| **Toughened glass (4 mm)** | 86–88% | 25+ yr | Excellent | ₹600–900 |
| **Polyethylene film (200 µm)** | 85–90% | 2–3 yr | Moderate | ₹40–60 |
| **UV-stabilized PE (200 µm)** | 85–88% | 3–5 yr | Good | ₹60–90 |
| **Double inflated PE** | 75–80% | 4–5 yr | Good | ₹120–180 |
| **Polycarbonate (8 mm twin-wall)** | 80–85% | 10–15 yr | Excellent | ₹400–700 |
| **Polycarbonate (16 mm multi-wall)** | 75–80% | 15+ yr | Excellent | ₹700–1200 |
| **Acrylic sheet** | 88–92% | 15–20 yr | Excellent | ₹800–1500 |
| **Shade net (50%)** | 50% | 5–7 yr | Excellent | ₹30–50 |
| **Insect net (40 mesh)** | 75–80% | 3–5 yr | Good | ₹40–70 |

**Properties to remember:**
- **Light Transmission:** must be ≥ 80% for most crops
- **Thermal Infrared Transmission (IRT):** should be LOW (< 20%) — this traps heat
- **PAR Transmission:** ideally ≥ 75%
- **Anti-drip / Anti-condensation:** critical in humid Kerala to reduce fungal disease

### 2.6.3 Foundation

- For **GI pipe frame:** Concrete pedestal (30×30×60 cm) at every 2–3 m
- For **Glasshouse:** Continuous RCC strip footing (60 cm depth)
- For **Poly-house:** Buried pipe ends or ground anchors

### 2.6.4 Ventilation System

| Type | Mechanism | Best Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Natural (roof + side vents)** | Wind + thermal buoyancy | Cold climates, small greenhouses |
| **Forced (exhaust fans)** | Mechanical extraction | Tropical, commercial |
| **Fan-and-pad cooling** | Evaporative cooling via wet pads | Arid hot climates (Rajasthan) |
| **Fogging / misting** | High-pressure nozzle atomization | Tropical humid (with care) |

### 2.6.5 Heating System (For Hilly / North India)

- **Hot water pipe heating** (centralized boiler)
- **Hot air furnace** (unit heaters)
- **Infrared heaters** (radiant)
- **Geothermal heat pumps**

### 2.6.6 Cooling System

- **Shade net** (40–75% shading depending on crop)
- **White-wash / shading paint** (chalk + glue)
- **Evaporative cooling pads** (cellulose, aspen)
- **Foggers / misters**
- **Thermal screen** (automated retractable)

### 2.6.7 Irrigation System

- **Drip irrigation** — most efficient, 90–95% water use efficiency
- **Micro-sprinkler** — for nursery and humid-loving crops
- **NFT (Nutrient Film Technique)** — for hydroponic lettuce
- **Ebb-and-flow** — for potted crops
- **Capillary mat** — for benches

### 2.6.8 Other Components

- **Benches / growing beds** (GI mesh, concrete, wooden)
- **Shading screens** (interior / exterior, motorized)
- **CO₂ enrichment system** (LPG burners / pure CO₂ cylinders)
- **Supplemental lighting** (HPS, LED — Photosynthetic spectrum 400–700 nm)
- **Climate controller / SCADA system** (sensors + PLC + HMI)
- **Drainage system** (sloped floors, French drains)
- **Doors and screens** (double-door airlock to prevent insect entry)

---

## 2.7 Real-World Utility in Engineering

| Application | Greenhouse Solution Used |
|---|---|
| **Kerala monsoon floriculture** | Poly-house with auto-shading + drip + fogging |
| **Rajasthan desert vegetable** | Glasshouse with fan-pad cooling + drip |
| **Sikkim strawberry off-season** | High-altitude poly-tunnel with passive heating |
| **Israel desert farming** | NFT hydroponic greenhouse with fully automated climate control |
| **Dutch tomato production** | Venlo-type glasshouse with diffused glass + LED + CO₂ enrichment |
