# SECTION 3 — STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Solar Declination & Optimum Tilt for Kerala

### Problem Statement (KTU-Style):
A farmer in **Thiruvananthapuram** (latitude $\phi = 8.5°$ N) wants to construct a greenhouse for year-round cucumber cultivation.
**(a)** Calculate the **solar declination angle** on **March 15** and **December 22**.
**(b)** Recommend the **optimum tilt angle** of the south-facing cover for **year-round** maximum solar collection.
**(c)** Determine the **solar altitude angle** at solar noon on **December 22**.

### Step-by-Step Solution:

#### Part (a): Solar Declination Angle

For **March 15**, $n = 31 + 28 + 15 = 74$ days (non-leap year).

$$\delta_{Mar15} = 23.45^\circ \times \sin\left[\frac{360}{365}(284 + 74)\right]$$

$$\delta_{Mar15} = 23.45^\circ \times \sin\left[\frac{360}{365} \times 358\right]$$

$$\delta_{Mar15} = 23.45^\circ \times \sin[353.10^\circ]$$

$$\delta_{Mar15} = 23.45^\circ \times \sin[-6.90^\circ]$$

$$\delta_{Mar15} = 23.45^\circ \times (-0.1201)$$

$$\boxed{\delta_{Mar15} \approx -2.82^\circ}$$

For **December 22**, $n = 356$ days.

$$\delta_{Dec22} = 23.45^\circ \times \sin\left[\frac{360}{365}(284 + 356)\right]$$

$$\delta_{Dec22} = 23.45^\circ \times \sin\left[\frac{360}{365} \times 640\right]$$

$$\delta_{Dec22} = 23.45^\circ \times \sin[631.23^\circ]$$

$$\delta_{Dec22} = 23.45^\circ \times \sin[-88.77^\circ]$$

$$\boxed{\delta_{Dec22} \approx -23.43^\circ \approx -23.45^\circ}$$

*(This confirms Dec 22 is the winter solstice — minimum declination in Northern Hemisphere.)*

#### Part (b): Optimum Tilt Angle

For **year-round average**, use $\beta_{opt} = \phi$:

$$\beta_{opt} = 8.5^\circ$$

For **winter-biased** (off-season cucumber in Dec–Jan):

$$\beta_{opt,winter} = \phi + 15^\circ = 8.5° + 15° = 23.5°$$

> **Recommendation:** Since Kerala cucumber is grown **October to March** (cooler months), the **winter-biased tilt of ~23°** is recommended.

#### Part (c): Solar Altitude Angle at Solar Noon (Dec 22)

The solar altitude angle ($\alpha_s$) at solar noon is:

$$\alpha_s = 90° - \phi + \delta$$

$$\alpha_s = 90° - 8.5° + (-23.45°)$$

$$\boxed{\alpha_s = 58.05^\circ}$$

**Interpretation:** Even in deepest winter, Thiruvananthapuram gets the sun at **58° above horizon at noon** — very high. This explains why greenhouses in Kerala do NOT need steep tilt angles and why cooling (not heating) is the primary concern.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Greenhouse Heat Loss Calculation

### Problem Statement:
A polyethylene greenhouse has dimensions **30 m × 8 m × 4 m** (height at ridge). Inside temperature is **30 °C** and outside is **22 °C**. The covering is **single PE film with U = 6.8 W/m²·K**. Surface area of the cover (walls + roof) is **approximately 1.5 × floor area** (for this shape).

**(a)** Calculate the **conductive heat loss** through the cover.
**(b)** If ventilation provides **20 ACH**, calculate **ventilation heat loss**.
**(c)** Estimate the **solar heat gain** if global radiation is **600 W/m²** and PE film transmits **85%**.
**(d)** Comment on whether additional cooling is needed.

### Step-by-Step Solution:

#### Part (a): Conductive Heat Loss

Floor area: $A_f = 30 \times 8 = 240 \text{ m}^2$
Cover surface area: $A_c \approx 1.5 \times 240 = 360 \text{ m}^2$
$\Delta T = 30 - 22 = 8 \text{ °C} = 8 \text{ K}$

$$Q_c = U \times A_c \times \Delta T$$

$$Q_c = 6.8 \times 360 \times 8$$

$$\boxed{Q_c = 19{,}584 \text{ W} \approx 19.58 \text{ kW}}$$

#### Part (b): Ventilation Heat Loss

Volume: $V = 30 \times 8 \times 4 = 960 \text{ m}^3$

$$Q_v = 1.2 \times V \times \text{ACH} \times \Delta T$$

$$Q_v = 1.2 \times 960 \times 20 \times 8$$

$$\boxed{Q_v = 184{,}320 \text{ W} \approx 184.3 \text{ kW}}$$

#### Part (c): Solar Heat Gain

Effective transmitted radiation: $I_{eff} = 600 \times 0.85 = 510 \text{ W/m}^2$

Using heat gain coefficient $K = 0.36$ for PE film (transmittance × absorption):

$$Q_{solar} = A_f \times K \times I_{global} = 240 \times 0.36 \times 600$$

$$\boxed{Q_{solar} = 51{,}840 \text{ W} \approx 51.84 \text{ kW}}$$

#### Part (d): Net Heat Balance & Cooling Comment

$$\text{Net Heat Inside} = Q_{solar} - Q_c - Q_v$$

$$\text{Net Heat Inside} = 51{,}840 - 19{,}584 - 184{,}320$$

$$\boxed{\text{Net Heat} = -152{,}064 \text{ W (heat removed by ventilation)}}$$

**Comment:**
> **Ventilation alone is removing ~3× the solar heat gain.** However, in **peak afternoon** (when global radiation can reach 900 W/m²) and **low wind speeds** (low ACH), the temperature can climb dangerously. Therefore:
> 1. **Shade net (50%)** is recommended for summer
> 2. **Fan-and-pad cooling** should be installed for critical crops
> 3. **Foggers** can provide supplementary evaporative cooling
> 4. **White-wash shading** during peak summer (March–May in Kerala)

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Air Change / Fan Capacity Sizing

### Problem Statement:
Design the **exhaust fan capacity** for a greenhouse of **20 m × 6 m × 3.5 m** in **Thrissur** (humid tropical). Required ACH for tropical greenhouse = **30**.

### Step-by-Step Solution:

Volume:

$$V = 20 \times 6 \times 3.5 = 420 \text{ m}^3$$

Required airflow:

$$Q_{air} = \frac{V \times \text{ACH}}{60} \quad \text{(in m}^3\text{/s)}$$

$$Q_{air} = \frac{420 \times 30}{60} = 210 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

Converting to CMM (Cubic Meter per Minute):

$$Q_{air,CMM} = 210 \times 60 = 12{,}600 \text{ CMM}$$

> **Design:** Use **4 fans each of 3,200 CMM capacity** (a small safety margin of ~2%) OR **6 fans of 2,100 CMM** for distributed airflow.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often forget to **convert hours to minutes** when computing fan capacity. Always recheck units!

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 4 — PAR Estimation for Crop Lighting

### Problem Statement:
A greenhouse receives **800 W/m²** of total global solar radiation. If PAR fraction is 0.47, what is the **PAR in µmol/m²/s**?

### Step-by-Step Solution:

PAR energy:

$$\text{PAR}_{energy} = 0.47 \times 800 = 376 \text{ W/m}^2$$

Conversion from energy (W) to photon flux (µmol/m²/s) using conversion factor **4.57 µmol/J** for sunlight:

$$\text{PPFD} = \text{PAR}_{energy} \times 4.57$$

$$\text{PPFD} = 376 \times 4.57$$

$$\boxed{\text{PPFD} \approx 1718 \text{ µmol/m}^2/\text{s}}$$

> **Interpretation:** This is well above the light saturation point of most C3 crops (~600 µmol/m²/s for tomato), meaning **shading may be required** in midday to prevent photoinhibition.

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q:** Define a greenhouse. List any **four** advantages of protected cultivation over open-field farming.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1.5 marks):** A greenhouse is a **transparent enclosed structure** that traps solar short-wave radiation and converts it into usable thermal energy for plant growth, providing a **controlled microclimate** (temperature, humidity, light, CO₂) for year-round or off-season cultivation of horticultural crops.

> **Four Advantages (1.5 marks — any 4 of the following):**
> 1. **Off-season cultivation** possible → premium market price
> 2. **Higher yield** (3–10× open field due to optimal conditions)
> 3. **Pest and disease control** via insect-proof nets and enclosed environment
> 4. **Water saving** through drip irrigation (90–95% efficiency)
> 5. **Quality produce** (uniform size, color, blemish-free)
> 6. **Protection from extreme weather** (rain, wind, hail, frost)

### **Question B-A** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]`

**(14 Marks) — Attempt any ONE from (i) and (ii)**

**(i) [7 Marks]** Discuss in detail the **factors governing site selection** for establishing a greenhouse in Kerala.

### **MODEL ANSWER — Question B-A (i) [7 Marks]**

**Site Selection Factors for Greenhouse in Kerala:**

**1. Climatic Factors (2 marks):**
- **Solar radiation:** Minimum 4–6 kWh/m²/day; Kerala gets 4.5–5.5 kWh/m²/day (sufficient)
- **Wind speed:** Avoid sites with > 40 km/h wind; provide windbreak if needed
- **Humidity:** Kerala's RH is 75–95% → must design for anti-fungal ventilation
- **Temperature:** Range 22–35 °C ideal; avoid low-lying frost pockets

**2. Topographical Factors (1.5 marks):**
- **Slope:** 1–2% gentle slope for natural drainage (avoid flat → waterlogging)
- **Elevation:** Slightly elevated to avoid flooding during monsoon
- **Orientation:** East-West ridge for Kerala (latitude < 25°)
- **Soil bearing capacity:** > 100 kPa; rocky / laterite soils common in Kerala

**3. Infrastructure Factors (2 marks):**
- **Water source:** Bore well / open well within 200 m; ≥ 5000 L/day for 1000 m²
- **Power supply:** 3-phase connection; backup generator essential
- **Road access:** Motorable road within 500 m for transporting produce
- **Market proximity:** Within 50 km for perishable flowers and vegetables

**4. Socio-Economic Factors (1 mark):**
- **Skilled labor availability**
- **Distance from polluting industries** (> 1 km)
- **Government subsidy availability** (NHM, MIDH schemes)
- **Land cost and ownership clarity**

**5. Special Considerations for Kerala (0.5 marks):**
- **Flood-prone areas must be avoided**
- **Anti-cyclone structural design** for coastal districts
- **Soil sterilization facility** for repeated cropping

### **Question B-B (Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper]`

**(14 Marks) — Attempt any ONE from (i) and (ii)**

**(i) [7 Marks]** Explain the **principle of greenhouse effect** with a neat energy balance diagram. Derive the **heat balance equation** of a greenhouse and explain each term.

**(ii) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **scope of greenhouse technology in India** with special reference to **Kerala**. List the major crops grown under protected cultivation.

---

### **MODEL ANSWER — Question B-B (i) [7 Marks]**

**Principle of Greenhouse Effect (3 marks):**

Sun emits **short-wave solar radiation** (wavelength 0.3–3 µm) which **passes freely** through the transparent greenhouse cover (glass, PE, PC). This radiation is absorbed by **plants, soil, and benches**, which become warm and **re-emit long-wave infrared radiation** (wavelength 3–100 µm). The transparent cover is **opaque to this long-wave radiation**, so the heat is **trapped inside** — this is the **greenhouse effect**.

> *Analogy:* Just like a one-way mirror — light goes IN, heat cannot get OUT.

**Energy Balance Equation (3 marks):**

$$Q_s = Q_c + Q_l + Q_v + Q_e + Q_s$$

**Description of Each Term (1 mark):**

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| $Q_s$ | Solar radiation entering greenhouse (W) |
| $Q_c$ | Conductive heat loss through walls, roof, floor (W) |
| $Q_l$ | Convective heat loss due to air infiltration (W) |
| $Q_v$ | Latent heat loss via transpiration (W) |
| $Q_e$ | Long-wave thermal radiation emitted by cover (W) |
| $Q_s$ | Heat stored in soil, plants, structure (W) |

For **steady-state**, the heat in = heat out. For **daytime in Kerala**, $Q_s$ is very large → design focuses on **cooling**. For **winter night in North India**, $Q_c$ and $Q_l$ dominate → design focuses on **heating**.

---

### **MODEL ANSWER — Question B-B (ii) [7 Marks]**

**Scope of Greenhouse Technology in India:**

**1. Global Scenario (1.5 marks):**
- World greenhouse area ≈ **500,000 hectares** (largest in China, then Spain, Korea, Japan, Turkey, Netherlands)
- India has only **~50,000 hectares** under protected cultivation → huge growth potential

**2. Indian Scenario (2 marks):**
- Major states: **Maharashtra, Karnataka, Gujarat, Himachal Pradesh, Tamil Nadu, West Bengal**
- Govt. schemes: **MIDH (Mission for Integrated Development of Horticulture)**, NHM, PMKSY
- Subsidy: **50–90%** for small farmers under various schemes
- Major players: **Ninjacart, Clover, WayCool** (supply chain integration)

**3. Kerala-Specific Scope (2.5 marks):**

| Crop | Type of Structure | Area (approx.) |
|---|---|---|
| **Anthurium** | Shade net + poly-house | 100+ ha |
| **Orchids** | Shade net house (75% shade) | 500+ ha |
| **Rose** | Poly-house | 50+ ha |
| **Capsicum (colored)** | Naturally ventilated poly-house | Growing |
| **Strawberry** | Poly-tunnel | 20+ ha (Wayanad, Munnar) |
| **Tomato (hybrid)** | Insect-proof net house | 30+ ha |
| **Lettuce & salad veg** | Hydroponic greenhouse | New ventures |

**Special advantages for Kerala:**
- High-value crops fetch **5–10× open-field prices**
- Monsoon protection for cut-flowers
- Year-round production possible
- Export potential to Middle East via Cochin port

**4. Challenges (1 mark):**
- High initial cost (₹400–1500/m² for poly-house, ₹3000–5000/m² for glasshouse)
- Skilled labor shortage
- Power reliability issues
- High humidity needs special anti-fungal design

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Pitfall & Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ COMMON MISTAKES THAT COST MARKS IN KTU EXAMS:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "PE film U-value" with "glass U-value"** — They differ by ~10%. PE = 6.8, Glass = 6.4. Memorize both.
>
> 2. **Writing $\phi$ for latitude as "latitude angle"** without specifying it's for the **location** — examiners deduct for ambiguity.
>
> 3. **Forgetting units in energy balance** — Always write **W (Watts)** or **kW** — never just a number.
>
> 4. **Confusing vent orientation with ridge orientation** — In Kerala, the **RIDGE runs E-W**, but **vents can be on the roof (ridge vent) AND sides** — be clear.
>
> 5. **Not mentioning "anti-condensation / anti-drip coating"** when discussing PE film for humid Kerala — this is a **favourite 1-mark follow-up** by KTU examiners.
>
> 6. **Calculating $\delta$ using wrong $n$** — Remember Jan 1 = 1, not 0.
>
> 7. **Forgetting the **fan-and-pad cooling** is INEFFECTIVE in Kerala's high humidity** — only mention it for arid regions.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember (Last-Minute Revision)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🎯 KTU EXAM NIGHT REVISION CHECKLIST — MODULE 1:**

### ✅ Definitions to Memorize
- ☐ Greenhouse definition (with "transparent enclosed structure + controlled microclimate")
- ☐ Greenhouse effect principle (short-wave IN, long-wave OUT blocked)
- ☐ Protected cultivation definition

### ✅ Historical Timeline
- ☐ 14 AD Romans (Tiberius cucumber)
- ☐ 1599 Holland (first practical greenhouse)
- ☐ 1949 polyethylene film invention
- ☐ 1980s computer-controlled climate

### ✅ Key Numbers to Remember
- ☐ Solar declination range: **−23.45° to +23.45°**
- ☐ Hour angle: **15° per hour from solar noon**
- ☐ PE U-value: **6.8 W/m²·K** | Glass: **6.4 W/m²·K** | PC 8mm: **3.2 W/m²·K**
- ☐ PAR fraction: **45–50%** of global radiation
- ☐ Kerala latitude: **~8–12° N** → **E-W ridge orientation**
- ☐ Required ACH (Kerala): **20–60**
- ☐ Recommended CO₂: **800–1000 ppm** (vs ambient 350 ppm)
- ☐ Light transmission minimum: **≥ 80%**
- ☐ Optimal temperature for most crops: **20–28 °C**

### ✅ Covering Material Comparison
- ☐ Glass: highest light, longest life, highest cost
- ☐ PE film: cheapest, short life, high light
- ☐ Polycarbonate: best durability, mid-cost, diffused light
- ☐ Anti-drip + UV-stabilized coatings are essential for Kerala

### ✅ Components Checklist (14 components)
- ☐ Frame, Foundation, Cover, Gutter, Vents (roof + side), Heating, Cooling, Shading, Irrigation, Fertigation, Climate controller, CO₂ system, Lighting, Benches, Drainage

### ✅ Site Selection (8 factors)
- ☐ Solar, Wind, Water, Power, Road, Market, Labor, Soil bearing capacity, Pollution distance

### ✅ Energy Balance Equation
- ☐ $Q_s = Q_c + Q_l + Q_v + Q_e + Q_s$ — write ALL 6 terms with units

### ✅ Critical Concepts
- ☐ Kerala = COOLING-dominant design (not heating)
- ☐ E-W ridge at latitudes < 25° N
- ☐ Fan-pad cooling ineffective in humid Kerala
- ☐ Drip irrigation is the universal best practice
- ☐ NFT/hydroponics for high-value leafy greens
- ☐ Insect-proof net is critical to reduce pesticide cost

> **Good luck with your KTU exams! 🌱**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design Criteria and Calculation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEAGT721 - DESIGN AND MAINTENANCE OF GREEN HOUSE
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Design Criteria and Calculation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Greenhouse Design Criteria** refers to the systematic set of engineering parameters, environmental thresholds, structural load specifications, and functional requirements that must be satisfied to ensure a greenhouse structure is safe, durable, climate-controlled, and economically viable for protected cultivation of horticultural crops.

According to KTU 2024 Scheme (OEAGT721 - Module 1), design criteria encompass **five primary domains**:

1. **Climatic Design Criteria** – Solar radiation, wind, temperature, humidity, and rainfall.
2. **Structural Design Criteria** – Loads (dead, live, wind, snow, crop), member sizing, and foundation.
3. **Functional Design Criteria** – Ventilation, heating, cooling, lighting, and irrigation capacity.
4. **Site Selection Criteria** – Topography, soil bearing, accessibility, and market proximity.
5. **Economic Design Criteria** – Capital cost, payback period, and lifecycle analysis.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy – "The Greenhouse is Like a Custom-Built Suit":**
> Think of a greenhouse as a tailored suit for plants. Just as a suit is designed considering the wearer's body shape (load), climate (insulation), occasion (purpose), and budget (economics), a greenhouse is engineered considering the local climate (wind, sun, rain), the crop (live load), the structural skeleton (frame), and the farmer's budget. A poor design = an ill-fitting suit = unhappy wearer (stunted plants, collapsed structure, wasted money).

### 1.3 Core Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Reference Values Used in Greenhouse Design:**
> - **Density of air (ρ)** = **1.225 kg/m³** (at 15°C, sea level)
> - **Acceleration due to gravity (g)** = **9.81 m/s²**
> - **Solar constant (Isc)** = **1367 W/m²** (above atmosphere)
> - **Standard design wind speed (Vb)** = **33–47 m/s** (as per IS 875 Part 3 for Kerala)
> - **Thermal conductivity of glass** ≈ **1.05 W/m·K**
> - **Thermal conductivity of PE film** ≈ **0.33 W/m·K**

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Climatic Design Criteria – Step-by-Step Logic

| Step | Parameter | Why It Matters | Typical Value |
|------|-----------|----------------|---------------|
| 1 | Solar Radiation (MJ/m²/day) | Drives photosynthesis and heating | 10–25 MJ/m²/day |
| 2 | Outside Design Temperature | Sets heating/cooling load | 5°C (winter) / 40°C (summer) |
| 3 | Wind Speed & Direction | Affects structural load and ventilation | Basic wind speed 39 m/s (Kerala) |
| 4 | Relative Humidity | Affects transpiration and disease | 50–90% |
| 5 | Rainfall Intensity | Affects drainage and cover drainage | 100–300 mm/hr peak |

### 2.2 Structural Design Loads – The Five Pillars

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Five Loads?** A greenhouse is a lightweight flexible structure. Unlike a concrete building where dead load dominates, here **wind, snow, and crop loads** can exceed the structure's own weight. All loads must be combined using IS 875 / ASCE 7 load combinations.

1. **Dead Load (DL):** Self-weight of frame + glazing.
2. **Live Load (LL):** Maintenance personnel, suspended equipment.
3. **Wind Load (WL):** Most critical in coastal Kerala.
4. **Snow Load (SL):** Relevant only in high-altitude regions; usually **negligible in Kerala** but mandatory per code.
5. **Crop Load (CL):** Weight of hanging vines (tomato, cucumber) – typically **15–30 kg/m²**.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $V_z = V_b \cdot k_1 \cdot k_2 \cdot k_3$ | Design wind speed at height z | m/s |
| 2 | $p_z = 0.5 \cdot \rho \cdot V_z^2$ | Wind pressure (dynamic) | N/m² |
| 3 | $F = C_f \cdot A_e \cdot p_z$ | Wind force on structure | N |
| 4 | $P_{roof} = C_a - C_i) \cdot p_z$ | Net pressure on roof | N/m² |
| 5 | $Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T$ | Heat transfer through cover (W) | W |
| 6 | $R = \frac{T_i - T_o}{I_s + I_r}$ | Ventilation air exchange ratio | - |
| 7 | $V_{req} = \frac{Q_{total}}{\rho \cdot C_p \cdot \Delta T}$ | Required ventilation airflow | m³/s |
| 8 | $\sigma = \frac{M \cdot y}{I}$ | Bending stress in member | N/mm² |
| 9 | $P_{cr} = \frac{\pi^2 \cdot E \cdot I}{L_e^2}$ | Euler's buckling load | N |
| 10 | $A_{vent} = \frac{V_{req}}{v_{vent}}$ | Required vent area | m² |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These formulas are universal across agricultural, civil, and architectural engineering. A student mastering these can design not only greenhouses but also poly-houses, net-houses, cold storage, and even poultry sheds.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Wind Pressure and Force on a Greenhouse

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Starting Principle:** Wind exerts dynamic pressure on any structure that obstructs its flow. This pressure is proportional to the square of velocity (Bernoulli's principle applied to wind engineering).

**Step 1: Basic Wind Speed (Vb)**
The basic wind speed is obtained from IS 875 (Part 3) for the given zone. For Kerala coastal zone:
$$V_b = 39 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2: Design Wind Speed at Height z (Vz)**
Three factors modify Vb: risk level (k1), terrain roughness (k2), topography (k3).
$$V_z = V_b \cdot k_1 \cdot k_2 \cdot k_3$$
For a 4 m high greenhouse, Category 2, flat terrain:
$$V_z = 39 \cdot 1.0 \cdot 1.0 \cdot 1.0 = 39 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 3: Design Wind Pressure (pz)**
Apply the dynamic pressure equation from fluid mechanics:
$$p_z = 0.5 \cdot \rho \cdot V_z^2$$
$$p_z = 0.5 \cdot 1.225 \cdot (39)^2$$
$$p_z = 0.6125 \cdot 1521$$
$$p_z = 931.6 \text{ N/m}^2 \approx 932 \text{ Pa}$$

**Step 4: Wind Force on the Greenhouse (F)**
$$F = C_f \cdot A_e \cdot p_z$$
where Cf = 1.2 (force coefficient for arched greenhouse), Ae = projected area.
For a 10 m × 30 m greenhouse (Ae = 300 m²):
$$F = 1.2 \cdot 300 \cdot 931.6$$
$$F = 335,376 \text{ N} \approx 335.4 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 5: Distributed Load (w) Along the Ridge**
$$w = \frac{F}{L} = \frac{335,376}{30} = 11,179 \text{ N/m} \approx 11.18 \text{ kN/m}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight:** The 932 Pa pressure is roughly equivalent to a 95 kg/m² distributed load — explaining why lightweight greenhouses collapse in cyclones if not properly designed.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Heat Transfer Through Greenhouse Cover

**Step 1: Identify the Temperature Difference**
$$\Delta T = T_i - T_o$$
For winter design in Kerala: Ti = 25°C (inside), To = 15°C (outside).
$$\Delta T = 25 - 15 = 10 \text{ °C} = 10 \text{ K}$$

**Step 2: Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient (U)**
For a single-layer polyethylene film greenhouse:
$$U = 6.5 \text{ W/m}^2\text{·K}$$

**Step 3: Heat Loss Through Cover (Q)**
$$Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T$$
For 10 m × 30 m greenhouse (A = 300 m², considering only roof):
$$Q = 6.5 \cdot 300 \cdot 10$$
$$Q = 19,500 \text{ W} = 19.5 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 4: Total Heat Loss Including Infiltration**
Add 30% for air infiltration:
$$Q_{total} = 1.3 \cdot 19.5 = 25.35 \text{ kW}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight:** This 25.35 kW is the heater capacity a farmer must install. A double-layer PE film would reduce U to ~4.0 W/m²·K, cutting heating cost by nearly 40%.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Required Ventilation Area Calculation

**Step 1: Required Airflow Rate (Vreq)**
To maintain a 5°C temperature rise inside using only ventilation:
$$V_{req} = \frac{Q_{solar}}{\rho \cdot C_p \cdot \Delta T}$$
where Qsolar = 800 W/m² peak, Cp = 1005 J/kg·K.
$$V_{req} = \frac{800 \cdot 300}{1.225 \cdot 1005 \cdot 5}$$
$$V_{req} = \frac{240,000}{6155.6} = 39 \text{ m}^3\text{/s}$$

**Step 2: Vent Cross-Sectional Area**
If wind-driven vent velocity = 2 m/s:
$$A_{vent} = \frac{V_{req}}{v_{vent}} = \frac{39}{2} = 19.5 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 3: Vent Sizing (Roof Vents)**
If each vent is 1 m × 1 m, number of vents required:
$$n = \frac{19.5}{1} = 20 \text{ vents}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Tip:** Distribute vents evenly — 10 on each long side or along the ridge for natural stack effect ventilation.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix – Load Combination Table (KTU Standard)

| Combination | DL | LL | WL | CL | SL | Use Case |
|-------------|----|----|----|----|----|----------|
| 1 | 1.5 | 1.5 | – | 1.5 | – | No-wind, full crop |
| 2 | 1.2 | 1.2 | 1.2 | 1.2 | – | Operating wind |
| 3 | 1.2 | 1.2 | – | 1.2 | 1.2 | Snow + crop |
| 4 | 0.9 | – | 1.5 | – | – | Uplift check (empty) |
| 5 | 1.2 | – | 1.5 | 0.9 | – | Wind dominant |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Greenhouse Design Process Flow (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Project Definition] --> B[Site Selection & Survey]
    B --> C[Climatic Data Collection]
    C --> D[Load Determination]
    D --> D1[Dead Load]
    D --> D2[Live Load]
    D --> D3[Wind Load]
    D --> D4[Snow Load]
    D --> D5[Crop Load]
    D1 --> E[Structural Analysis]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    D4 --> E
    D5 --> E
    E --> F[Member Sizing]
    F --> G[Foundation Design]
    G --> H[Cover Material Selection]
    H --> I[Ventilation System Design]
    I --> J[Heating Cooling Load Calc]
    J --> K[Economic Analysis]
    K --> L{Final Approval}
    L -->|Yes| M[Construction Drawings]
    L -->|No| C
    M --> N[End]
```

### 4.2 Load Distribution on Arched Greenhouse Frame

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Windward Side: Positive Pressure Cp=+0.8] --> B[Arch Frame Member]
    B --> C[Roof: Negative Pressure Suction Cp=-0.7]
    C --> D[Leeward Side: Negative Cp=-0.5]
    D --> E[Foundation: Anchors Resist Uplift]
    E --> F[Base Reactions to Soil]
```

### 4.3 Functional Block Architecture – Integrated Climate Control

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    INPUT[Climate Inputs: Solar Wind RH Temp] --> SENSORS[Sensors and Data Logger]
    SENSORS --> CONTROL[Central Controller]
    CONTROL --> VENT[Roof and Side Vent Motors]
    CONTROL --> HEAT[Heater Unit]
    CONTROL --> FAN[Exhaust Fan and Pad System]
    CONTROL --> SHADE[Shade Net Motor]
    VENT --> GREENHOUSE[Greenhouse Interior]
    HEAT --> GREENHOUSE
    FAN --> GREENHOUSE
    SHADE --> GREENHOUSE
    GREENHOUSE --> CROP[Crop Environment]
    CROP --> OUTPUT[Yield and Quality Data]
    OUTPUT --> FEEDBACK[Feedback to Controller]
    FEEDBACK --> CONTROL
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** In KTU exams, block diagrams are accepted in lieu of detailed drawings. Use the arrows to show **flow of forces, energy, or data** — never physical geometry details which are better drawn by hand.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
> **List any six climatic factors that govern the design of a greenhouse.**

**Model Answer (6 × 0.5 = 3 marks):**
1. Solar radiation intensity and duration
2. Ambient air temperature (max/min)
3. Wind speed and direction
4. Relative humidity
5. Rainfall intensity and pattern
6. Atmospheric dust and cloud cover

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> **Define the term 'design wind speed'. State its significance in greenhouse design.**

**Model Answer:**
- **Definition:** Design wind speed is the basic wind speed (Vb) modified by factors k1 (risk), k2 (terrain), and k3 (topography) for the height of the structure, as per IS 875 (Part 3). $V_z = V_b \cdot k_1 \cdot k_2 \cdot k_3$
- **Significance:** It determines the wind pressure and hence the structural member sizes, foundation design, and overall stability of the greenhouse. (1½ + 1½ = 3 marks)

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each – Internal Choice)

> **Q3. [KTU University Exam – June 2024] – Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Explain in detail the various **structural design loads** to be considered for a greenhouse with neat sketches. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A gutter-connected greenhouse of size **10 m × 30 m × 4 m height** is to be constructed in coastal Kerala. Calculate the **design wind pressure and total wind force** on the structure. Assume basic wind speed Vb = 39 m/s, k1 = k2 = k3 = 1.0, force coefficient Cf = 1.2, and air density ρ = 1.225 kg/m³. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Structural Design Loads (7 marks)**

1. **Dead Load (DL):** Self-weight of frame (GI pipes, trusses) and glazing (glass/film). Usually 5–15 kg/m². **(1 mark)**
2. **Live Load (LL):** Maintenance personnel, suspended irrigation/heating pipes. Typically 75 kg/m² on accessible areas. **(1 mark)**
3. **Wind Load (WL):** Most critical in Kerala. Calculated using $p_z = 0.5 \cdot \rho \cdot V_z^2$. Acts as pressure on windward side and suction on leeward. **(2 marks)**
4. **Snow Load (SL):** Although rare in Kerala, included as per code. $S = \mu_i \cdot C_e \cdot C_t \cdot I \cdot S_g$. **(1 mark)**
5. **Crop Load (CL):** Hanging crops (tomato, cucumber, capsicum) – 15–30 kg/m². **(1 mark)**
6. **Special Loads:** Seismic (Zone III in Kerala), temperature-induced expansion, accidental loads. **(1 mark)**

**(b) Wind Pressure & Force Calculation (7 marks)**

**Given:**
- Vb = 39 m/s, k1 = k2 = k3 = 1.0
- Cf = 1.2, ρ = 1.225 kg/m³
- Size: 10 m × 30 m, height 4 m

**Step 1:** Design wind speed
$$V_z = 39 \cdot 1 \cdot 1 \cdot 1 = 39 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2:** Design wind pressure
$$p_z = 0.5 \cdot 1.225 \cdot (39)^2 = 0.6125 \cdot 1521 = 931.6 \text{ N/m}^2$$

**Step 3:** Projected area (Ae) on windward face
$$A_e = \text{width} \times \text{height} = 10 \cdot 4 = 40 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 4:** Total wind force
$$F = C_f \cdot A_e \cdot p_z = 1.2 \cdot 40 \cdot 931.6$$
$$F = 44,716.8 \text{ N} \approx 44.72 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 5:** Distributed load per unit length
$$w = \frac{44.72}{30} = 1.49 \text{ kN/m}$$

> **Answer:** Design wind pressure = **931.6 N/m²**, Total wind force = **44.72 kN**, Distributed load = **1.49 kN/m**

---

> **Q4. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] – Question B (14 Marks) [Internal Choice Alternative]**

**(a)** Discuss the **site selection criteria** for establishing a greenhouse in Kerala. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A polyhouse of size **8 m × 24 m** with single-layer polyethylene cover has inside temperature maintained at **28°C** while outside is **18°C**. Calculate the **total heat loss per hour** through the roof. Take U = 6.5 W/m²·K and add 25% for infiltration losses. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Site Selection Criteria (7 marks)**

1. **Topography:** Flat or gently sloping land (1–3% slope) for drainage. **(1 mark)**
2. **Solar Exposure:** South-facing orientation, free from shading by trees/buildings. **(1 mark)**
3. **Wind Protection:** Sheltered from strong cyclonic winds, but with adequate ventilation. **(1 mark)**
4. **Water Availability:** Continuous supply of good-quality irrigation water. **(1 mark)**
5. **Soil Quality:** Well-drained loamy soil with pH 6.0–7.0 and bearing capacity > 100 kN/m². **(1 mark)**
6. **Accessibility:** Near motorable road, market, and electricity grid. **(1 mark)**
7. **Legal/Social:** Away from flood-prone zones, with clear land title. **(1 mark)**

**(b) Heat Loss Calculation (7 marks)**

**Given:**
- A = 8 × 24 = 192 m² (roof area)
- Ti = 28°C, To = 18°C, ΔT = 10 K
- U = 6.5 W/m²·K
- Infiltration factor = 1.25

**Step 1:** Heat loss by conduction
$$Q_c = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T = 6.5 \cdot 192 \cdot 10$$
$$Q_c = 12,480 \text{ W} = 12.48 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 2:** Total heat loss including infiltration
$$Q_{total} = 1.25 \cdot 12,480 = 15,600 \text{ W} = 15.6 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 3:** Heat loss per hour
$$Q_{hr} = 15.6 \cdot 3600 = 56,160 \text{ kJ/hr} \approx 15.6 \text{ kWh/hr}$$

> **Answer:** Total heat loss = **15.6 kW** or **15.6 kWh per hour**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit Mismatch:** Students often mix up N/m² and kN/m² — always check final units. Losing **1–2 marks** is common.
> 2. **Forgetting to multiply Cf:** Many write wind force = A × pz, skipping Cf. This is a **3-mark deduction**.
> 3. **Infiltration Factor:** Always state it clearly. If omitted, lose 1 mark.
> 4. **Load Combination Missing:** Listing loads without combinations is incomplete; examiner expects at least the governing combination.
> 5. **Kerala-Specific Wind:** Using Vb = 44 or 47 m/s (North India values) is wrong for Kerala — stick to **33–39 m/s** as per IS 875.
> 6. **No Sketch in Part (a):** Even a simple block diagram fetches **1–2 marks extra**.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist – Design Criteria and Calculation:**

- ☐ **Five Domains of Design:** Climatic, Structural, Functional, Site, Economic.
- ☐ **Climatic Factors:** Solar radiation, temperature, wind, humidity, rainfall — all must be collected for at least 10 years.
- ☐ **Five Structural Loads:** DL, LL, WL, CL, SL — each with standard factors of safety.
- ☐ **Wind Pressure Formula:** $p_z = 0.5 \rho V_z^2$ — memorize it cold.
- ☐ **Wind Force Formula:** $F = C_f \cdot A_e \cdot p_z$ — never forget Cf.
- ☐ **Kerala Basic Wind Speed:** 39 m/s for coastal areas.
- ☐ **Heat Loss Formula:** $Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T$ — add 25–30% for infiltration.
- ☐ **U-value:** Glass = 5.7, PE film = 6.5, Double PE = 4.0 W/m²·K.
- ☐ **Ventilation Rule of Thumb:** Roof vent area = **15–20%** of floor area.
- ☐ **Crop Load:** 15–30 kg/m² for hanging vine crops.
- ☐ **Orientation:** Ridge oriented **East-West** for maximum winter light in tropics.
- ☐ **Foundation Depth:** Minimum 60 cm below ground for Kerala conditions.
- ☐ **Always Use IS 875 (Part 3)** for wind and **IS 875 (Part 4)** for dead loads.
- ☐ **Sketch in Every Answer:** Even a small diagram fetches easy marks.
- ☐ **Show All Steps in Numerical:** KTU awards step marks generously — never skip algebra.
- ☐ **Final Unit Check:** Convert W to kW, N to kN, Pa to kPa for clarity.
- ☐ **Key Real-World Insight:** A 1°C reduction in heating demand saves ~6% energy — making insulation design economically critical.

---

> **End of Module 1 Notes — Design Criteria and Calculation**
> *Next Recommended Topic: Greenhouse Covering Materials and Their Properties*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Constructional Material and Methods of Construction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEAGT721 - DESIGN AND MAINTENANCE OF GREEN HOUSE
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Constructional Material and Methods of Construction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Greenhouse Constructional Materials — Formal Definition

In the context of KTU 2024 Scheme (Course OEAGT721), **constructional materials** of a greenhouse refer to the complete set of structural, transparent (glazing), and auxiliary components used to physically erect the controlled-environment enclosure. They are broadly classified into:

1. **Glazing / Covering Materials** — the light-transmitting skin that admits solar radiation while retaining heat (e.g., glass, polyethylene film, polycarbonate, acrylic, ETFE).
2. **Framing / Structural Materials** — the load-bearing skeleton (e.g., galvanized steel, aluminum, wood, bamboo, PVC, concrete).
3. **Foundation & Ancillary Materials** — base materials, fasteners, sealants, gaskets, and shading/insect nets.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU Module 1):** A student must be able to *identify, compare, and justify* the selection of constructional materials for different greenhouse types (low-cost, medium, and high-tech) used across Indian and global horticulture.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "House as a Human Body"

Imagine a greenhouse is a **living organism**:
- The **glazing** is the **skin** — it lets in light (food), controls temperature, and keeps rain out.
- The **frame** is the **skeleton** — it gives shape, holds the skin, and resists wind and snow loads.
- The **foundation** is the **feet** — it anchors the structure to the ground and prevents sinking or tilting.

Just as a thin cotton shirt (polyethylene film) is cheap but tears easily, while a thick woolen coat (polycarbonate panel) is durable but costly, **each glazing material represents a trade-off between light transmission, durability, and cost**.

## 1.3 Classification of Glazing Materials

| Category | Examples | Typical Service Life | Cost Index |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Rigid Glass** | Annealed, tempered, horticultural float glass | 20–30 years | High |
| **Rigid Plastic** | Polycarbonate (PC), Acrylic (PMMA), FRP | 10–20 years | Medium–High |
| **Flexible Film** | LDPE, LLDPE, EVA, ETFE | 1–4 years (films); 20+ yrs (ETFE) | Low–Medium |
| **Mesh / Netting** | HDPE shade net, insect net | 5–8 years | Low |

## 1.4 Classification of Framing Materials

| Material | Key Property | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Galvanized Iron (GI) Steel** | High strength, corrosion resistant | Commercial polyhouses & glasshouses |
| **Aluminum Alloys** | Lightweight, rust-proof, no painting needed | High-tech Dutch Venlo greenhouses |
| **Wood (Teak, Sal, Pine)** | Cheap, easy to shape, biodegradable | Low-cost naturally ventilated polyhouses |
| **Bamboo (treated)** | Low cost, renewable, low strength | Small nursery / shade net houses |
| **PVC / GI Pipes** | Easy to assemble, low load capacity | Low tunnels, walk-in tunnels |
| **RCC / Concrete** | Permanent foundation only | Permanent foundations |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric to Remember:** The global benchmark for a *high-performance* greenhouse uses **tempered glass with 89–92% PAR transmission** and an aluminum frame. The benchmark for *low-cost Indian polyhouse* is **200-micron UV-stabilized LDPE film** over a **GI pipe frame**.

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Material Selection Logic — The "Five-Pillar Test"

Every candidate material must be evaluated on five engineering pillars (this is a KTU-favourite framework):

1. **Optical Property** — Light transmittance ($\tau$), PAR transmission ($\tau_{PAR}$), and diffusion ratio.
2. **Thermal Property** — Overall heat transfer coefficient ($U$-value) and emissivity ($\varepsilon$).
3. **Mechanical Property** — Tensile strength, impact resistance, and Young's modulus.
4. **Durability** — UV stability, service life, and degradation rate per year.
5. **Economic Property** — Cost per m² and life-cycle cost.

## 2.2 Optical Behavior of Glazing

The total light transmission is governed by the **Fresnel reflection law**, summed over both glass–air interfaces. For a single transparent sheet of refractive index $n$ in air ($n_0 = 1$):

$$R_{interface} = \left(\frac{n - 1}{n + 1}\right)^{2}$$

The transmittance through a single sheet (ignoring absorption) is:

$$\tau = (1 - R)^{2} = \left[\frac{2n}{n+1}\right]^{2} \cdot \left[\frac{2}{n+1}\right]^{2}$$

When materials are layered (as in twin-wall PC), the transmittance reduces multiplicatively: $\tau_{total} = \prod_{i=1}^{k} \tau_{i}$.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $R = \left(\frac{n-1}{n+1}\right)^{2}$ | Fresnel reflectance at one interface | dimensionless |
| 2 | $\tau = (1-R)^{2}$ | Single sheet transmittance (no absorption) | dimensionless (0–1) |
| 3 | $U = \frac{1}{R_{i} + \sum \frac{x_{j}}{k_{j}} + R_{o}}$ | Overall heat transfer coefficient (R = thermal resistance) | $\text{W/m}^{2}\text{K}$ |
| 4 | $q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T$ | Heat loss through covering | W |
| 5 | $\tau_{PAR} \approx 0.45 \cdot \tau_{visible}$ | PAR fraction of visible light | dimensionless |
| 6 | $\alpha = \frac{\Delta L}{L_{0} \cdot \Delta T}$ | Linear thermal expansion coefficient | $\text{K}^{-1}$ or $^\circ\text{C}^{-1}$ |
| 7 | $F_{snow} = C_{s} \cdot \rho_{snow} \cdot g \cdot t$ | Snow load on roof | $\text{N/m}^{2}$ |
| 8 | $F_{wind} = 0.5 \cdot \rho_{air} \cdot v^{2} \cdot C_{d}$ | Wind pressure (drag) | $\text{Pa}$ |
| 9 | $Q_{cond} = U \cdot A \cdot (T_{i} - T_{o})$ | Conductive heat loss | W |
| 10 | $T_{inside} = T_{out} + \frac{I \cdot \tau}{U}$ | Greenhouse temperature rise (steady state) | $^\circ\text{C}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** These formulas are used to (a) select the right glazing thickness to retain heat, (b) size the heating system, and (c) design the frame to resist wind and snow — directly answering Module 1 and Module 4 design questions.

## 2.4 Decision Tree for Choosing Glazing (KTU Reasoning Style)

- **Budget < ₹400 / m² AND climate is mild?** → LDPE 200-micron film
- **Service life > 10 years AND hail-prone?** → Twin-wall Polycarbonate (8–10 mm)
- **Premium Dutch-style greenhouse?** → Tempered 4 mm float glass on aluminum
- **Sports stadium / botanical atrium?** → ETFE foil (single or multi-layer cushion)
- **Partial shade / tropical fruit nursery?** → 50% HDPE shade net
- **Insect-protected vegetable production?** → 50-mesh HDPE insect net + UV film

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Application

In the **Dutch Westland region**, growers use 4 mm **diffuse glass** on aluminum Venlo structures to scatter sunlight evenly, eliminating leaf scorch and boosting yields by 8–10% over clear glass. In **Indian polyhouses of Karnataka and Maharashtra**, growers use **200-micron UV-stabilized LDPE** (5-layer co-extruded) over a **GI Class-B pipe frame** because the climate is mild and capital must be recovered in 3–4 crop cycles.

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Single-Sheet Light Transmittance

We derive the transmittance of a single transparent sheet (refractive index $n$) immersed in air ($n_0 = 1$), assuming **zero absorption** inside the sheet.

**Step 1 — Snell's Law at first interface (air → glass).**
The angle of refraction $\theta_{r}$ inside the glass is governed by $n_0 \sin\theta_{i} = n \sin\theta_{r}$, but for **normal incidence** the angular term collapses and reflectance becomes purely a function of $n$.

**Step 2 — Fresnel Equation for Normal Incidence.**
At normal incidence, the reflectance at one interface is:

$$R_{01} = \left(\frac{n_{0} - n}{n_{0} + n}\right)^{2} = \left(\frac{1 - n}{1 + n}\right)^{2} = \left(\frac{n - 1}{n + 1}\right)^{2}$$

**Step 3 — Transmittance at One Interface.**
By conservation of energy (no absorption): $T_{01} = 1 - R_{01}$.

**Step 4 — Two Interfaces (Front and Back).**
Light passes through the front interface (transmittance $T_{01}$), travels through the glass with no loss, then exits through the back interface (transmittance $T_{10}$). By Snell's reversibility, $T_{10} = T_{01}$.

**Step 5 — Total Transmittance of Single Sheet.**

$$\tau_{single} = T_{01} \cdot T_{10} = (1 - R_{01})^{2}$$

**Step 6 — Substitute $R_{01}$.**

$$\tau_{single} = \left[1 - \left(\frac{n-1}{n+1}\right)^{2}\right]^{2}$$

**Step 7 — Numerical Check for Glass ($n = 1.5$).**

$$R_{01} = \left(\frac{0.5}{2.5}\right)^{2} = (0.2)^{2} = 0.04$$

$$\tau_{single} = (1 - 0.04)^{2} = (0.96)^{2} = 0.9216 \approx 92.2\%$$

This matches the **measured horticultural glass transmittance of 89–92%** (the 1–3% gap is due to iron-oxide absorption in the glass).

## 3.2 Derivation: Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient (U-Value)

For a multi-layer covering (e.g., double-film inflated polyhouse or twin-wall PC), the total thermal resistance is the **sum of individual layer resistances** plus the inner and outer surface convective resistances.

**Step 1 — Define Thermal Resistance.**
For a homogeneous layer of thickness $x$ and thermal conductivity $k$:

$$R_{layer} = \frac{x}{k} \quad [\text{m}^{2}\text{K/W}]$$

**Step 2 — Surface Resistances.**
Inner surface resistance: $R_{i} = 1/h_{i}$ (typical $h_{i} = 8\,\text{W/m}^{2}\text{K}$).  
Outer surface resistance: $R_{o} = 1/h_{o}$ (typical $h_{o} = 23\,\text{W/m}^{2}\text{K}$ for wind).

**Step 3 — Total Resistance.**

$$R_{total} = R_{i} + \sum_{j=1}^{m} \frac{x_{j}}{k_{j}} + R_{o}$$

**Step 4 — Overall U-value.**

$$U = \frac{1}{R_{total}} \quad [\text{W/m}^{2}\text{K}]$$

**Step 5 — Sample Calculation: Single 4 mm Glass.**
Glass conductivity: $k = 1.05\,\text{W/mK}$, $x = 0.004\,\text{m}$.

$$\frac{x}{k} = \frac{0.004}{1.05} = 0.00381 \;\text{m}^{2}\text{K/W}$$

$$R_{i} = 0.125,\quad R_{o} = 0.043,\quad R_{total} = 0.125 + 0.00381 + 0.043 = 0.172$$

$$U_{glass} = \frac{1}{0.172} = 5.81\;\text{W/m}^{2}\text{K}$$

**Step 6 — Sample Calculation: Double Polyethylene Film (200 µm + 200 µm inflated).**
Two film layers each with $R = 0.012$, air gap contributes $R = 0.16$:

$$R_{total} = 0.125 + 0.012 + 0.16 + 0.012 + 0.043 = 0.352$$

$$U_{double-film} = \frac{1}{0.352} = 2.84\;\text{W/m}^{2}\text{K}$$

**Conclusion:** Double film reduces heat loss by **~51%** vs single glass — a classic KTU exam insight.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Material vs Property

| Material | $\tau$ (light) | $U$ (W/m²K) | Service Life | Cost ₹/m² | Suitability |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 4 mm Float Glass | 0.89 | 5.8 | 25 yr | 600–800 | High-tech |
| 4 mm Tempered Glass | 0.91 | 5.8 | 30 yr | 900–1200 | Premium |
| 8 mm Twin-wall PC | 0.80 | 2.7 | 15 yr | 700–1000 | Medium-tech |
| 200 µm LDPE (single) | 0.87 | 6.5 | 2 yr | 30–50 | Low-cost |
| 200 µm LDPE (double) | 0.78 | 2.8 | 2 yr | 60–100 | Low-cost |
| ETFE (single foil) | 0.95 | 5.6 | 20+ yr | 2000+ | Stadiums |
| 50% HDPE Shade Net | 0.50 | – | 7 yr | 25–40 | Shading only |

> [!NOTE]
> **Higher $\tau$ = More light = Better photosynthesis**, but **Lower $U$ = Less heat loss = Lower heating cost**. The **optimum** is a material with $\tau > 0.85$ **and** $U < 3.0\,\text{W/m}^{2}\text{K}$ — which is why **double-film inflated polyethylene** and **twin-wall PC** dominate commercial polyhouses.

## 3.4 Derivation: Greenhouse Steady-State Temperature Rise

Under steady state, the **incoming solar gain equals the heat lost by conduction** through the covering.

**Step 1 — Solar Gain.**
$I$ = incident solar radiation (W/m²), $\tau$ = covering transmittance.  
Heat gained: $Q_{gain} = I \cdot \tau \cdot A$ (W).

**Step 2 — Conductive Loss.**

$$Q_{loss} = U \cdot A \cdot (T_{i} - T_{o})$$

**Step 3 — Equate at Steady State.**

$$I \cdot \tau \cdot A = U \cdot A \cdot (T_{i} - T_{o})$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $T_{i}$.**

$$T_{i} = T_{o} + \frac{I \cdot \tau}{U}$$

**Step 5 — Numerical Check.**
For $\tau = 0.87$, $U = 2.84$, $I = 600\,\text{W/m}^{2}$, $T_{o} = 25^\circ\text{C}$:

$$T_{i} = 25 + \frac{600 \cdot 0.87}{2.84} = 25 + 183.8 \approx 209^\circ\text{C}$$

The unrealistic value reveals that **steady state is never reached** — actual $T_{i}$ is much lower because of **ventilation losses**, **infiltration**, and **transient heat storage** in soil and plants. This is the key reason why **ventilation design (Module 3)** is critical.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**  
*List any six properties that a material should possess to be used as a covering (glazing) material for a greenhouse.*

**Model Answer (6 points × 0.5 = 3 marks):**

1. **High light transmittance** — at least 85% in PAR range (400–700 nm).
2. **Low thermal conductivity** — so that heat loss during night is minimized (low $U$-value).
3. **High mechanical strength** — to resist wind, hail, and handling damage.
4. **Long service life** — resistance to UV degradation for 5–25 years.
5. **Light weight** — reduces frame cost and dead load.
6. **Low cost per square meter** — economically viable for farmers.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Students often write *"good quality"* or *"durable"* without quantifying. KTU examiners award full marks only when *numbers or units* are mentioned (e.g., "transmittance > 85%", "$U < 3\,\text{W/m}^{2}\text{K}$"). Avoid vague phrases.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Sample 2023]**  
*Differentiate between a Quonset-type greenhouse and a Gable-type greenhouse. Mention one advantage of each.*

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Quonset Type | Gable Type |
|---|---|---|
| **Shape** | Continuous semi-circular arch | Triangular roof with vertical walls |
| **Frame Material** | Bent GI pipes (single piece) | Welded trusses with columns |
| **Glazing** | Mostly polyethylene film | Glass / Polycarbonate |
| **Headroom** | Limited at sides | Uniform throughout |
| **Snow Shedding** | Excellent (curved roof) | Moderate (depends on slope) |
| **Advantage** | Cheaper, easier to construct | Higher headroom, suitable for tall crops |

> [!NOTE]
> Examiners in KTU 2024 expect a **table or bullet comparison**, not paragraph form, for "differentiate" questions.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Sub-parts (a) and (b)

**[KTU University Exam — Module 1, 2024 Scheme Pattern]**

**Q.A (a)** *Explain in detail the different types of covering (glazing) materials used in greenhouses. Compare their properties with respect to light transmission, thermal conductivity, service life, and cost.* **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

The major categories of covering materials are:

**(i) Glass** — Traditional material. Types: annealed, tempered, low-iron horticultural, and diffuse glass.
- Light transmittance: **89–92%** (PAR).
- $U$-value: **5.8 W/m²K** (4 mm single).
- Service life: **25–30 years**.
- Cost: **₹600–1200/m²**.
- Pros: highest clarity, scratch-resistant, long life. Cons: heavy, brittle, expensive.

**(ii) Polyethylene Film (PE)** — Most popular in India. Types: LDPE, LLDPE, EVA, 5-layer co-extruded, UV-stabilized.
- Light transmittance: **85–90%** (single), **78%** (double inflated).
- $U$-value: **6.5 W/m²K** (single), **2.8 W/m²K** (double).
- Service life: **1–2 years** (UV-stabilized), up to **4 years** (EVA / IR films).
- Cost: **₹30–100/m²**.
- Pros: cheap, easy to install, available. Cons: short life, wind damage.

**(iii) Polycarbonate (PC)** — Rigid, twin-wall / multi-wall panel.
- Light transmittance: **78–82%** (8 mm twin-wall).
- $U$-value: **2.4–2.7 W/m²K** (8 mm twin-wall).
- Service life: **10–15 years**.
- Cost: **₹700–1000/m²**.
- Pros: impact-resistant, good insulation. Cons: yellowing over time, dust accumulation in flutes.

**(iv) Acrylic (PMMA)** — Clear rigid sheet.
- Light transmittance: **88–92%**.
- $U$-value: **5.0 W/m²K** (3 mm).
- Service life: **15–20 years**.
- Cost: **₹500–800/m²**.
- Pros: very clear, lightweight. Cons: brittle, expensive.

**(v) ETFE (Ethylene Tetrafluoroethylene)** — Fluoropolymer foil, used in premium projects.
- Light transmittance: **>95%** (single layer).
- $U$-value: **5.6 W/m²K** (single), **2.0 W/m²K** (triple cushion).
- Service life: **20+ years**.
- Cost: **₹2000+/m²**.
- Pros: extremely lightweight (1% of glass weight), self-cleaning. Cons: very high cost.

**(vi) HDPE Shade Net / Insect Net** — Used for shading and insect exclusion.
- Light transmittance: **30–90%** (as per shade percentage).
- $U$-value: not a primary thermal barrier.
- Service life: **5–8 years**.
- Cost: **₹25–60/m²**.
- Pros: cheap, easy. Cons: not a true cladding.

**Summary Table for Examiner:**

| Material | $\tau$ | $U$ (W/m²K) | Life (yr) | ₹/m² |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Glass | 0.89 | 5.8 | 25 | 800 |
| LDPE | 0.87 | 6.5 | 2 | 50 |
| Twin-wall PC | 0.80 | 2.7 | 15 | 800 |
| ETFE | 0.95 | 5.6 | 25 | 2000 |

---

**Q.A (b)** *Describe the step-by-step procedure for the construction of a low-cost naturally ventilated polyhouse. List the materials required for its construction with specifications.* **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Site Selection (1 mark)**
- Level, well-drained land with access to road and electricity.
- East–West orientation of the long axis (for low latitudes like India).

**Step 2 — Marking and Layout (0.5 mark)**
- Mark center line, column positions at 4 m × 2 m spacing.
- Use 100 m string, ranging rod, and lime powder.

**Step 3 — Foundation (1 mark)**
- For GI pipe columns: dig pits **45 cm × 45 cm × 60 cm deep**.
- Place 10 cm PCC (1:4:8) at the bottom.
- Insert GI pipe (Class B, 42.4 mm OD, 2 mm thick) **60 cm deep** into the pit.
- Pour 1:2:4 RCC pedestal around pipe up to ground level.
- Cure for 7 days.

**Step 4 — Column and Truss Erection (1 mark)**
- Erect vertical columns **3.0 m above ground**.
- Connect top of columns with **trusses / arches** of GI pipe (33 mm OD).
- Fix **purlins** (25 mm GI pipe) at 1 m spacing across the truss.

**Step 5 — Covering (1.5 marks)**
- Use **200 micron UV-stabilized LDPE film**, 5-layer co-extruded.
- Fix film on roof and side walls using **aluminium profile locks** and **zig-zag wire** to the purlins.
- Ensure proper tightening to avoid flapping.
- A second inner film creates an **inflated double-layer** (air inflated by a small blower of 0.25 HP).

**Step 6 — Doors and Side Ventilation (0.5 mark)**
- **Slide door** (1 m × 2 m) at one end.
- **Roll-up side vents** (1 m height) on both long sides for natural ventilation.

**Step 7 — Shade Net and Insect Net (0.5 mark)**
- Fix **50% HDPE green shade net** as a retractable curtain inside.
- Fix **40-mesh insect net** on all openings.

**Step 8 — Testing and Commissioning (1 mark)**
- Check inflation pressure of double film: maintain 6–8 mm water column.
- Run the blower continuously 24×7.
- Verify all fasteners and locks.

**Bill of Materials (Specifications):**
- GI pipe 42.4 mm OD, 2 mm thick (columns) — Class B, IS 1239.
- GI pipe 33 mm OD, 2 mm thick (truss / purlin).
- 200 micron LDPE UV-stabilized film (5-layer).
- Aluminium profile lock with EPDM gasket.
- Zig-zag spring wire.
- 0.25 HP air blower.
- HDPE 50% shade net, 40-mesh insect net.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Sub-parts (a) and (b)

**Q.B (a)** *Compare the properties of galvanized iron, aluminum, wood, and bamboo as structural framing materials for greenhouses. Which one would you recommend for a high-tech Venlo-type greenhouse in Kerala? Justify.* **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

| Property | GI Steel | Aluminum | Wood (Teak) | Bamboo (Treated) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Density (kg/m³)** | 7850 | 2700 | 700 | 600 |
| **Tensile Strength (MPa)** | 350–500 | 200–300 | 80–100 | 30–40 |
| **Corrosion Resistance** | High (galvanized) | Excellent | Poor (rots) | Moderate |
| **Maintenance** | Low | Very Low | High | Moderate |
| **Cost ₹/m (typical)** | 90–120 | 250–400 | 60–100 | 15–30 |
| **Service Life (yr)** | 25+ | 30+ | 8–10 | 4–6 |
| **Sustainability** | Recyclable | Highly recyclable | Renewable | Highly renewable |
| **Workability** | Welding needed | Extruded profiles | Easy carpentry | Limited joinery |
| **Thermal Expansion (×10⁻⁶ /°C)** | 12 | 23 | 5–10 | 4 |

**Recommendation: Aluminum** — for a high-tech Venlo greenhouse in Kerala.

**Justification (1 mark):**
1. Kerala's tropical humid climate causes rapid rusting of GI steel; aluminum's natural oxide layer gives **excellent corrosion resistance**.
2. Aluminum can be extruded into complex profiles (gutters, glazing bars, ridge connectors) needed for the **Venlo design** — GI pipe cannot form these.
3. **Lighter weight** (1/3 of steel) reduces foundation cost and earthquake load — important in **Kerala's seismic zone III**.
4. **No painting required** — lower maintenance cost.
5. **30+ year service life** matches the 25-year glass cladding life.
6. Disadvantage: **higher initial cost** (3–4× GI), so recommended only for high-tech, capital-intensive projects.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Pitfall:** Students often pick **GI steel** for Kerala without acknowledging the **corrosion problem from high humidity and coastal salinity**. Examiners deduct up to 2 marks for missing this climatic justification. Always tie the material choice to **the local climate**.

---

**Q.B (b)** *What are the different foundation types used in greenhouse construction? Explain with neat sketches. How is the foundation depth decided for a polyhouse?* **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Foundation Types (1.5 marks each, total ~5 marks):**

1. **RCC Isolated Footing** — Most common for medium to large greenhouses.
   - Square or rectangular concrete pedestal below each column.
   - Typical size: 45 cm × 45 cm × 60 cm deep for low-tech polyhouses; 60 cm × 60 cm × 90 cm for high-tech glasshouses.
   - Reinforcement: 4 nos. 10 mm dia longitudinal bars with 8 mm @ 150 mm c/c stirrups.

2. **Strip / Continuous Footing** — Used along the perimeter wall of permanent greenhouses.
   - Depth: 60–90 cm, width: 30–45 cm.
   - Used when there is a brick masonry dwarf wall up to 60–90 cm height.

3. **Pile / Pipe Foundation** — For very soft soil or where excavation is difficult.
   - GI / MS pipe driven 1–2 m into the ground.
   - Surrounding concrete cap at top.

4. **Ground Anchors / Earth Augers** — For low-cost net houses and tunnels.
   - Helical screw anchor or simple bent pipe inserted 45–60 cm deep.
   - No concrete; rapid installation.

5. **Raft / Mat Foundation** — For very high-tech Venlo greenhouses on weak soil.
   - Full concrete slab over entire floor area.
   - Most expensive, used for permanent structures.

**Decision of Foundation Depth (2 marks):**

The depth of foundation $D$ is decided based on:

$$D = D_{frost} + D_{bearing} + D_{anchor}$$

Where:
- $D_{frost}$ = depth below frost line (not relevant in Kerala, but 30 cm minimum in north India).
- $D_{bearing}$ = depth to reach firm bearing soil (typically **45–60 cm** in alluvial soil).
- $D_{anchor}$ = embedment length of column to resist **uplift from wind** (minimum **45 cm** for polyhouse, **60 cm** for glasshouse).

For Kerala's laterite / red soil, the minimum depth is **45 cm** for a polyhouse and **60 cm** for a glasshouse, considering:
1. **Wind uplift** (Kerala experiences cyclonic winds up to 150 km/hr).
2. **Soil bearing capacity** (typically 100–150 kN/m² for laterite).
3. **Anti-rusting** of the embedded portion.
4. **Lateral stability** of the column.

**Typical formula for column embedment depth to resist uplift:**

$$D_{anchor} = \frac{F_{u}}{P_{s} \cdot \pi \cdot d}$$

Where $F_{u}$ = uplift force, $P_{s}$ = passive soil pressure (~10 kN/m² for laterite), $d$ = column diameter.

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mark-Loss Traps in Module 1:**
> 1. **No diagrams / no sketches** in construction questions → **−2 marks**.
> 2. **No numerical comparison** (just qualitative "glass is better") → **−2 marks**.
> 3. **Forgetting orientation** (East-West for low latitudes) in polyhouse construction → **−1 mark**.
> 4. **No mention of local climate** when justifying material choice → **−1 to 2 marks**.
> 5. **Writing "UV-stabilized" without micron specification** for LDPE film → **−1 mark**.
> 6. **Confusing Quonset and Gothic arch** shapes in sketches → **−1 mark**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [ ] **Glazing materials** are classified into **glass, rigid plastics (PC, PMMA, FRP), flexible films (LDPE, LLDPE, EVA, ETFE), and nets**.
- [ ] **Glass** has the highest clarity (89–92% transmittance) and longest life (25+ years) but is heavy and expensive.
- [ ] **Polyethylene film** is the cheapest (₹30–100/m²) and most common in Indian polyhouses, with a short life of 1–2 years (UV-stabilized).
- [ ] **Twin-wall polycarbonate** offers the **best thermal insulation** ($U \approx 2.7\,\text{W/m}^{2}\text{K}$) with 15-year life.
- [ ] **ETFE** is the premium option (>95% transmittance, very light, 20+ years) — used in Eden Project, National Stadium Beijing.
- [ ] **GI steel** is the universal framing choice for low/medium-tech polyhouses in India; **aluminum** is preferred for high-tech Venlo glasshouses.
- [ ] **Bamboo and wood** are limited to low-cost shade net houses and naturally ventilated polyhouses.
- [ ] **Single sheet transmittance** follows the Fresnel equation: $\tau = (1-R)^{2}$ where $R = ((n-1)/(n+1))^{2}$.
- [ ] **U-value** is the inverse of total thermal resistance: $U = 1/R_{total}$.
- [ ] **Double-film inflated polyhouse** reduces heat loss by **~50%** vs single film.
- [ ] **Foundation depth** = frost depth + bearing depth + column embedment (min 45 cm for polyhouse, 60 cm for glasshouse).
- [ ] **Orientation** in India: long axis **East–West** to maximize winter solar gain and avoid summer overheating.
- [ ] **Greenhouse types**: Quonset, Gable, Gothic, Sawtooth, Venlo, Shade net house.
- [ ] Always quote **numbers and units** (transmittance, $U$-value, ₹/m²) in KTU answers to score full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Covering Materials and its Characteristics, Solar Heat Transfer, Solar Fraction for Greenhouse, Steady State Analysis of Greenhouse
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEAGT721 - DESIGN AND MAINTENANCE OF GREEN HOUSE
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Covering Materials and its Characteristics, Solar Heat Transfer, Solar Fraction for Greenhouse, Steady State Analysis of Greenhouse

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Greenhouse Covering Materials

### Formal Academic Definition
A **greenhouse covering material** is the transparent or semi-transparent envelope that encloses the crop-growing environment, allowing controlled transmission of photosynthetically active radiation (PAR, 400–700 nm) while providing structural integrity and modulating the internal microclimate. According to KTU 2024 syllabus, covering materials are classified based on their optical, thermal, mechanical, and durability properties and govern the **solar heat gain**, **long-wave radiation retention**, and the overall **energy balance** of the greenhouse.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight – Module 1:**
> The covering material is the *single most important design decision* in greenhouse engineering because it directly determines the transmissivity (τ), reflectivity (ρ), and absorptivity (α) coefficients that drive every subsequent energy-balance calculation.

### Classification of Covering Materials

| Family | Common Examples | Typical Service Life | Light Transmissivity |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Rigid Glasses** | Float glass, tempered glass, low-iron glass | 25–30 years | 0.88 – 0.92 |
| **Flexible Films** | Polyethylene (PE), EVA, PVC | 2–4 years (single), 4–6 years (thermal) | 0.80 – 0.90 |
| **Rigid Plastics** | Acrylic (PMMA), Polycarbonate (PC) twin/multi-wall | 10–20 years | 0.80 – 0.88 |
| **Specialty Films** | ETFE (fluoropolymer), F-Clean vinyl | 20+ years | 0.90 – 0.95 |
| **Composite Panels** | FRP (Fiberglass Reinforced Plastic) | 10–15 years | 0.75 – 0.85 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Energy Conservation Rule (Kirchhoff + Fresnel):**
> For any covering material,
> $$\tau + \rho + \alpha = 1$$
> where $\tau$ is solar transmissivity, $\rho$ is solar reflectivity, and $\alpha$ is solar absorptivity. Each coefficient must always be reported for **normal incidence (θ = 0°)** unless otherwise stated, because transmissivity drops steeply with increasing angle of incidence (especially beyond 60°).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Greenhouse as a Selective Window"

Imagine a **car parked in summer sun**. The closed windows act like a greenhouse cover: short-wave solar radiation enters (visible light passes through glass), heats the dark interior surfaces (seats, dashboard), and that heat is re-emitted as **long-wave infrared (LWIR, 5–50 µm)**, which the glass cannot transmit back out — it traps it. This **selective wavelength behavior** is the *exact* mechanism that makes a greenhouse warm. The covering material is therefore a *wavelength-selective filter*, not just a simple transparent sheet.

## 1.3 Solar Heat Transfer — Intuitive Overview

**Solar heat transfer through a greenhouse cover** is the process by which incident short-wave solar radiation (0.3 – 3.0 µm) interacts with the covering via three competing mechanisms: **transmission (τ·I)**, **reflection (ρ·I)**, and **absorption (α·I)**. Of the absorbed portion, energy is re-radiated both inward and outward as long-wave radiation, and convected to the outside air.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants Used Throughout Module 1:**
> - **Stefan–Boltzmann constant:** $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \, \text{W/m}^2\text{K}^4$
> - **Solar constant (extraterrestrial):** $I_0 = 1367 \, \text{W/m}^2$
> - **Peak ground-level AM1.5:** $I_{sc} = 1000 \, \text{W/m}^2$
> - **Air specific heat:** $c_p = 1.005 \, \text{kJ/kg·K}$
> - **Air density at STP:** $\rho_{air} = 1.20 \, \text{kg/m}^3$

## 1.4 Solar Fraction — Quick Definition

The **Solar Fraction (SF)** is the fraction of the total heating (or cooling) load of a greenhouse that is met by *incident solar radiation alone*, without supplemental heating or active cooling. It is a dimensionless design parameter ranging from **0 (no solar contribution)** to **1 (100% solar contribution)**.

## 1.5 Steady State Analysis — Intuitive Overview

**Steady state** means the greenhouse *internal temperature has stabilized* — energy gained equals energy lost at every instant. Mathematically:

$$Q_{\text{gain}} = Q_{\text{loss}}$$

Think of a water tank with an inlet and outlet pipe: when inflow rate equals outflow rate, the water level stays constant. The greenhouse under steady state is exactly this — a thermal reservoir at equilibrium.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Detailed Characteristics of Covering Materials

### 2.1.1 Optical Properties
1. **Solar Transmissivity (τ):** The fraction of incident solar radiation that physically passes through the material. For a single 3 mm horticultural float glass, $\tau \approx 0.89$. Polyethylene typically achieves $\tau \approx 0.85$ initially, dropping to 0.70–0.75 after 2 years due to UV degradation.
2. **Reflectivity (ρ):** Determined by Fresnel equations at each air-glass interface. For normal incidence on a single pane:
$$\rho = \left(\frac{n_{\text{material}} - n_{\text{air}}}{n_{\text{material}} + n_{\text{air}}}\right)^2$$
For glass ($n = 1.52$), $\rho \approx 0.043$ per surface, so a single pane has $\rho_{\text{total}} \approx 0.08$ (≈4% per surface).
3. **Absorptivity (α):** The fraction of solar radiation absorbed within the bulk material. Depends on pigment, iron content (low-iron glass has lower α), and thickness.

### 2.1.2 Thermal Properties
4. **Emissivity (ε):** A *surface property* describing how efficiently a material emits long-wave radiation. Glass has $\varepsilon \approx 0.84$ for both surfaces; polyethylene has $\varepsilon \approx 0.50$–$0.70$; ETFE has $\varepsilon \approx 0.10$–$0.20$ (highly LWIR-transparent).
5. **Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient (U-value):** Measured in $\text{W/m}^2\text{K}$. Includes convection (both sides) and radiation. For single glass, $U \approx 6.0 \, \text{W/m}^2\text{K}$; for double PE film with air gap, $U \approx 4.0 \, \text{W/m}^2\text{K}$.

### 2.1.3 Mechanical & Durability Properties
6. **Tensile Strength & Impact Resistance**
7. **UV Degradation Rate (yellowing index)**
8. **Anti-Drip / Anti-Fog Coatings (condensation control)**
9. **Diffusion (light scattering for uniform canopy illumination)**

## 2.2 The Solar Heat Transfer Mechanism — Layer-by-Layer

For a **single covering layer** receiving solar irradiance $I$ ($\text{W/m}^2$), the incident beam is partitioned as:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Reflected outward:} \quad & Q_r = \rho \cdot I \cdot A \\
\text{Absorbed in layer:} \quad & Q_a = \alpha \cdot I \cdot A \\
\text{Transmitted inward:} \quad & Q_t = \tau \cdot I \cdot A
\end{aligned}
$$

The absorbed energy $Q_a$ is then re-distributed by **conduction-convection** and **long-wave radiation** — typically ~50% inward and ~50% outward under no-wind conditions (governed by surface temperatures and convection coefficients).

### 2.2.1 Net Solar Heat Gain
For a greenhouse with $N$ identical covering layers, the **net solar heat gain entering the interior** is:

$$
Q_{\text{solar,net}} = \tau_N \cdot \alpha_{\text{floor}} \cdot I \cdot A_g + \tfrac{1}{2}\,\alpha_{\text{layer}} \cdot I \cdot A_g
$$

where $\tau_N$ is the *combined* transmissivity of $N$ layers (each layer roughly multiplies transmissivity, $\tau_N \approx \tau^N$ for identical non-absorbing layers, but lower in practice due to absorption).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is the second term "½ × absorbed by layer"?**
> Because for a thin, well-coupled cover, *half the absorbed heat is conducted/radiated inward* to the plants and *half outward* to the sky. This 50/50 assumption is standard in KTU-level textbook treatments and ASAE standards.

## 2.3 Solar Fraction — Formal Definition and Use

The **Solar Fraction** is defined as:

$$
\text{SF} = \frac{Q_{\text{solar gain}}}{Q_{\text{total required heating (or cooling) load}}}
$$

In heating mode (cold climates, winter):

$$
\text{SF}_{\text{heat}} = \frac{Q_{\text{solar gain}}}{Q_{\text{solar gain}} + Q_{\text{supplemental heater}}}
$$

In cooling mode (hot climates, summer):

$$
\text{SF}_{\text{cool}} = \frac{Q_{\text{solar avoided}}}{Q_{\text{total cooling load}}}
$$

## 2.4 Steady State Heat Balance Equation of a Greenhouse

Under steady-state, no condensation, no plant transpiration simplification, the energy balance reduces to:

$$
\boxed{Q_{\text{solar}} = Q_{\text{conduction}} + Q_{\text{ventilation}} + Q_{\text{infiltration}}}
$$

In expanded form:

$$
\tau \cdot I \cdot A_g = U_g \cdot A_g \cdot (T_i - T_o) + \dot{m} \cdot c_p \cdot (T_i - T_o) + \dot{V} \cdot \rho_{air} \cdot c_p \cdot (T_i - T_o)
$$

Where:
- $U_g$ = overall heat transfer coefficient of cover ($\text{W/m}^2\text{K}$)
- $A_g$ = floor/ground area or cover area ($\text{m}^2$)
- $\dot{m}$ = ventilation mass flow rate ($\text{kg/s}$)
- $\dot{V}$ = infiltration volumetric flow rate ($\text{m}^3/\text{s}$)

### 2.4.1 Solving for Inside Temperature $T_i$

Rearranging the steady-state balance:

$$
T_i = T_o + \frac{\tau \cdot I \cdot A_g}{U_g \cdot A_g + \dot{m} \cdot c_p + \dot{V} \cdot \rho_{air} \cdot c_p}
$$

This is the **KTU-favorite formula** — it lets you compute the inside temperature purely from solar input and design parameters.

## 2.5 KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Typical Units | Used For |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\tau + \rho + \alpha = 1$ | Energy partitioning at cover | dimensionless | Verifying optical coefficients |
| 2 | $\rho = \left(\frac{n_2 - n_1}{n_2 + n_1}\right)^2$ | Fresnel reflection at one interface | dimensionless | Single-surface reflection |
| 3 | $q_{\text{rad}} = \varepsilon \cdot \sigma \cdot T^4$ | Long-wave radiation emission | $\text{W/m}^2$ | Cover-to-sky LWIR heat loss |
| 4 | $Q_{\text{solar,net}} = \tau I A_g + \tfrac{1}{2}\alpha I A_g$ | Net useful solar gain | W | Heating load computation |
| 5 | $Q_{\text{cond}} = U_g \cdot A_g \cdot \Delta T$ | Conductive loss through cover | W | Heat loss term |
| 6 | $Q_{\text{vent}} = \rho_{air} \cdot c_p \cdot \dot{V} \cdot \Delta T$ | Sensible ventilation loss | W | Heat loss term |
| 7 | $\text{SF} = \dfrac{Q_{\text{solar}}}{Q_{\text{total load}}}$ | Solar fraction | dimensionless | Design sizing of backup heater |
| 8 | $T_i = T_o + \dfrac{\tau I A_g}{U_g A_g + \rho_{air} c_p \dot{V}}$ | Steady-state inside temperature | K or °C | Climate-control verification |
| 9 | $\text{ACH} = \dfrac{\dot{V}}{V_{\text{gh}}}$ | Air changes per hour | $\text{hr}^{-1}$ | Infiltration specification (0.5–2 typical) |
| 10 | $I = I_{\text{sc}} \cdot k_t \cdot \cos\theta_z$ | Irradiance on tilted cover | $\text{W/m}^2$ | Real-time incident solar input |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Reality Check:** All KTU textbook problems assume **steady, clear-sky noon conditions** unless otherwise specified. If "monthly average" or "annual" is mentioned, a clearness index $k_t \in [0.4, 0.7]$ must be applied.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation of Net Solar Heat Gain Through a Single Cover

### Starting Point
Incident solar irradiance $I$ ($\text{W/m}^2$) strikes a transparent cover of area $A_g$. We define:
- $\rho$ = solar reflectivity (fraction)
- $\alpha$ = solar absorptivity (fraction)
- $\tau$ = solar transmissivity (fraction)

By **conservation of energy at the cover surface** (no internal energy storage in steady state):

$$
\begin{aligned}
I \cdot A_g &= \rho \cdot I \cdot A_g + \alpha \cdot I \cdot A_g + \tau \cdot I \cdot A_g \\
\Rightarrow \quad \rho + \alpha + \tau &= 1
\end{aligned}
$$

The absorbed energy $\alpha I A_g$ is split equally between inward and outward hemispheres for a thin symmetric cover:

$$
\begin{aligned}
q_{\text{absorbed, in}} &= \tfrac{1}{2} \, \alpha \cdot I \cdot A_g \\
q_{\text{absorbed, out}} &= \tfrac{1}{2} \, \alpha \cdot I \cdot A_g
\end{aligned}
$$

The transmitted portion $\tau I A_g$ entirely enters the greenhouse. Therefore the **net solar heat gain** delivered to the greenhouse interior is:

$$
\boxed{Q_{\text{solar,net}} = \tau \cdot I \cdot A_g + \tfrac{1}{2}\,\alpha \cdot I \cdot A_g = \left(\tau + \tfrac{\alpha}{2}\right) I A_g}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation of the Steady-State Inside Temperature $T_i$

### Assumptions
1. Steady state: $dT_i/dt = 0$.
2. No plant transpiration, no latent heat.
3. Single-pane cover with overall heat transfer coefficient $U_g$.
4. Ventilation provides mass flow rate $\dot{m} = \rho_{air} \dot{V}$ ($\text{kg/s}$).
5. Inside air is well-mixed (uniform $T_i$).

### Energy Balance
Energy In = Energy Out:

$$
Q_{\text{solar,net}} = Q_{\text{cond}} + Q_{\text{vent}}
$$

Substituting each term:

$$
\left(\tau + \tfrac{\alpha}{2}\right) I A_g = U_g A_g (T_i - T_o) + \dot{m}\, c_p (T_i - T_o)
$$

Factor the right-hand side:

$$
\left(\tau + \tfrac{\alpha}{2}\right) I A_g = (T_i - T_o)\left[U_g A_g + \dot{m}\, c_p\right]
$$

Solve for $T_i$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_i - T_o &= \frac{\left(\tau + \tfrac{\alpha}{2}\right) I A_g}{U_g A_g + \dot{m}\, c_p} \\
T_i &= T_o + \frac{\left(\tau + \tfrac{\alpha}{2}\right) I A_g}{U_g A_g + \dot{m}\, c_p}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation of the Result:**
> - Numerator = driving force (solar energy captured).
> - Denominator = sum of all thermal resistances converted to a conductance.
> - If $\dot{m} \to \infty$ (infinite ventilation), $T_i \to T_o$ (no heating).
> - If $\dot{m} = 0$ (sealed greenhouse), $T_i$ rises by $\dfrac{\tau I}{U_g}$ — the **classic greenhouse temperature rise**.

## 3.3 Numerical Example — Solved KTU-Style Problem

**Problem Statement (KTU Model):**
A greenhouse has a single glass cover with $\tau = 0.89$, $\alpha = 0.06$, $\rho = 0.05$. Cover area $A_g = 50 \, \text{m}^2$. Solar irradiance $I = 800 \, \text{W/m}^2$. Overall heat loss coefficient $U_g = 6.0 \, \text{W/m}^2\text{K}$. Outside air temperature $T_o = 15°\text{C}$. Air density $1.20 \, \text{kg/m}^3$, specific heat $1.005 \, \text{kJ/kg·K}$. Ventilation rate $\dot{V} = 0.5 \, \text{m}^3/\text{s}$.
Find: (a) Net solar heat gain. (b) Steady-state inside temperature. (c) Solar fraction if the total heating load is 25 kW.

### Part (a): Net Solar Heat Gain

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{\text{solar,net}} &= \left(\tau + \tfrac{\alpha}{2}\right) I A_g \\
&= \left(0.89 + \tfrac{0.06}{2}\right) \cdot 800 \cdot 50 \\
&= (0.89 + 0.03) \cdot 40000 \\
&= 0.92 \cdot 40000 \\
&= 36{,}800 \, \text{W} = 36.8 \, \text{kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Part (b): Steady-State Inside Temperature

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{m} &= \rho_{air} \cdot \dot{V} = 1.20 \cdot 0.5 = 0.60 \, \text{kg/s} \\
\dot{m} \cdot c_p &= 0.60 \cdot 1005 = 603 \, \text{W/K} \\
U_g A_g &= 6.0 \cdot 50 = 300 \, \text{W/K} \\
\text{Denominator} &= 300 + 603 = 903 \, \text{W/K} \\
T_i - T_o &= \frac{36{,}800}{903} = 40.75 \, \text{K} \\
T_i &= 15 + 40.75 = 55.75°\text{C}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Part (c): Solar Fraction

$$
\text{SF} = \frac{Q_{\text{solar,net}}}{Q_{\text{total load}}} = \frac{36.8 \, \text{kW}}{25 \, \text{kW}} = 1.47
$$

Since SF > 1, this means **solar alone exceeds the design heating load**, so no supplemental heating is needed during this solar period — typical of a high-sun winter day in Kerala. **SF is capped at 1.0** when designing for the worst case.

## 3.4 Worked-Out Two-Layer Cover Derivation (Double Polyethylene)

For two layers of identical PE film (each $\tau_1, \alpha_1, \rho_1$), the **combined** transmissivity for *non-diffuse* light is approximately:

$$
\tau_{\text{combined}} = \frac{\tau_1^2}{1 - \rho_1^2}
$$

For PE: $\tau_1 = 0.85, \rho_1 = 0.08$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau_{\text{combined}} &= \frac{(0.85)^2}{1 - (0.08)^2} = \frac{0.7225}{1 - 0.0064} = \frac{0.7225}{0.9936} \approx 0.727
\end{aligned}
$$

The energy loss coefficient drops from $U_g = 6.0$ (single) to $U_g \approx 4.0 \, \text{W/m}^2\text{K}$ (double PE), illustrating the **trade-off**: lower light transmission, but reduced heating bill.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Parameter Effect on Greenhouse Performance

| Design Parameter | Increase → Effect on $T_i$ | Effect on SF | Engineering Trade-off |
|---|---|---|---|
| Cover Transmissivity $\tau$ | $\uparrow T_i$ | $\uparrow$ SF | Use low-iron glass or anti-UV film |
| Cover Emissivity $\varepsilon$ | $\downarrow T_i$ at night | no direct effect | Low-$\varepsilon$ films retain heat |
| Number of Layers $N$ | $\uparrow T_i$ at night, $\downarrow$ day | complicated | Double layer saves fuel but loses light |
| Ventilation $\dot{V}$ | $\downarrow T_i$ (closer to ambient) | $\downarrow$ SF | Natural ventilation is free cooling |
| Infiltration Rate | $\downarrow T_i$ | $\downarrow$ SF | Seal gaps; use double-door vestibules |
| Solar Irradiance $I$ | $\uparrow T_i$ | $\uparrow$ SF | Latitude and season matter |
| Outside Temp $T_o$ | Affects $\Delta T$ | affects denominator | Climate is uncontrollable |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Solar Radiation Partition at a Greenhouse Cover

The following functional flow diagram maps how incident solar radiation $I$ is partitioned at a single transparent cover and how the absorbed portion is re-radiated and convected.

```mermaid
graph TD
    SunA[Incident Solar Radiation I at W/m^2] --> RefA[Reflected Outward rho times I]
    SunA --> AbsA[Absorbed in Cover alpha times I]
    SunA --> TransA[Transmitted Inward tau times I]
    AbsA --> RadIn[Re radiated Long wave Inward]
    AbsA --> RadOut[Re radiated Long wave Outward]
    AbsA --> ConvIn[Convected to Interior Air]
    AbsA --> ConvOut[Convected to Outside Air]
    TransA --> PlantsA[Reaches Floor Plants Soil]
    PlantsA --> SoilAbs[Absorbed by Canopy and Floor]
    SoilAbs --> EvapA[Transpiration Latent Heat]
    SoilAbs --> ConvA[Convective Heat to Air]
    RadIn --> ConvA
    ConvA --> InternalAirA[Greenhouse Internal Air T_i]
    InternalAirA --> CondA[Conductive Loss Through Cover U_g]
    InternalAirA --> VentA[Ventilation Loss rho cp V delta T]
    CondA --> ExternalAirA[Outside Air T_o]
    VentA --> ExternalAirA
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Steady-State Energy Balance Architecture

The following diagram illustrates how the steady-state energy balance is constructed module by module, mapping each heat transfer pathway to a mathematical term.

```mermaid
graph TD
    InputSolar[Solar Irradiance I] --> CalcA[Cover Optics Module]
    CalcA -->|tau plus half alpha| SolarGain[Net Solar Heat Gain Q_solar]
```
