```mermaid
    OutsideTemp[Outside Air Temperature T_o] --> SubSystem[Temperature Difference delta T]
    SolarGain --> SumBlock[Energy Balance Solver]
    SubSystem --> LossCond[Conduction Loss U_g A_g delta T]
    SubSystem --> LossVent[Ventilation Loss rho cp V delta T]
    LossCond --> SumBlock
    LossVent --> SumBlock
    SumBlock -->|Solve for T_i| InsideTemp[Greenhouse Inside Temperature T_i]
    SolarGain -->|Numerator| SFCalc[Solar Fraction Calculator]
    TotalLoad[Total Heating Load Q_total] --> SFCalc
    SFCalc -->|Q_solar slash Q_total| SFOut[Solar Fraction SF Output]
    InsideTemp --> DisplayA[Display Module 1 Result]
    SFOut --> DisplayA
```

## 4.3 Schematic of Cover Material Selection Decision Tree

This decision tree guides greenhouse engineers in selecting the right covering material based on climate, crop, and budget.

```mermaid
graph TD
    StartA[Greenhouse Project Kickoff] --> ClimA{Climate Type}
    ClimA -->|Tropical Kerala| TropA[Hot Humid Climate]
    ClimA -->|High Altitude| TempA[Temperate Climate]
    ClimA -->|Cold North India| ColdA[Cold Winter Climate]
    TropA --> FilmChoice{Choose Film Type}
    FilmChoice -->|Low Cost| PEA[Polyethylene UV Stabilized]
    FilmChoice -->|Long Life| ETEFA[ETFE Fluoropolymer]
    FilmChoice -->|Rigid Premium| GlassA[Horticultural Glass]
    TempA --> TwinWall[Twin Wall Polycarbonate]
    ColdA --> DoubleFilm[Double Inflated PE Film]
    DoubleFilm --> HeatRetain{High Heat Retention Needed}
    HeatRetain -->|Yes| IRFilm[IR Retaining PE Film]
    HeatRetain -->|No| StdFilm[Standard PE Film]
    PEA --> EndA[Material Selected]
    ETEFA --> EndA
    GlassA --> EndA
    TwinWall --> EndA
    IRFilm --> EndA
    StdFilm --> EndA
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Q: Define solar transmissivity of a greenhouse covering material. List any two desirable optical properties of an ideal greenhouse cover.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** Solar transmissivity ($\tau$) of a greenhouse covering material is defined as the fraction of incident solar radiation (in the wavelength range 0.3 – 3.0 µm) that is transmitted through the material and reaches the interior of the greenhouse. It is mathematically expressed as:

$$
\tau = \frac{Q_{\text{transmitted}}}{Q_{\text{incident}}}
$$

For an ideal single-pane horticultural glass, $\tau \approx 0.89$ at normal incidence.

**Two desirable optical properties (1 mark):**
1. **High transmissivity** in the PAR band (400 – 700 nm) to maximize photosynthesis.
2. **Low long-wave emissivity** ($\varepsilon < 0.20$) to retain interior thermal radiation and reduce night-time heat loss.

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Q: What is meant by "Solar Fraction" in greenhouse engineering? Give its mathematical expression and typical range.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** The **Solar Fraction (SF)** of a greenhouse is the ratio of the useful solar heat gain to the total energy (heating or cooling) load of the greenhouse. It quantifies how much of the greenhouse's energy demand is met by incident solar radiation alone, without supplemental energy input.

**Mathematical Expression (1 mark):**

$$
\text{SF} = \frac{Q_{\text{solar,net}}}{Q_{\text{total load}}}
$$

**Typical Range (0.5 marks):** SF ranges from **0 to 1**. A value of **SF = 1** indicates the greenhouse requires no supplemental heating or cooling; **SF = 0** indicates solar contribution is negligible (overcast, cloudy conditions). For a well-designed greenhouse in tropical Kerala, winter SF for heating typically lies in the range **0.4 – 0.7**.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) — Choice 1
**[KTU University Exam – Sample Question, Module 1 Focus]**

**(a)** Derive the steady-state energy balance equation for a single-span greenhouse with a single transparent cover. Clearly state all assumptions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A greenhouse with floor area 40 m² is covered by a single polyethylene film having solar transmissivity τ = 0.85 and absorptivity α = 0.10. The cover overall heat loss coefficient is $U_g = 5.5 \, \text{W/m}^2\text{K}$. Solar irradiance on the cover is $I = 750 \, \text{W/m}^2$. The ventilation provides an air-change rate of 1.2 ACH when the greenhouse internal volume is 200 m³. Outside air temperature is $T_o = 22°\text{C}$. Take air density $\rho = 1.18 \, \text{kg/m}^3$ and $c_p = 1.005 \, \text{kJ/kg·K}$. Calculate: (i) Net solar heat gain, (ii) Steady-state inside temperature $T_i$, (iii) Solar fraction if total heating load is 12 kW. **(7 marks)**

### 📝 Model Solution to Question A

#### Part (a): Derivation of Steady-State Energy Balance

**Assumptions:**
1. Steady-state condition ($dT_i/dt = 0$).
2. Single horizontal transparent cover.
3. Uniform inside air temperature (lumped parameter).
4. No plant transpiration / no latent heat contribution.
5. Single air-change ventilation path.
6. Infiltration lumped with ventilation.

**Energy Balance:** Energy in = Energy out

$$
Q_{\text{solar}} = Q_{\text{conduction}} + Q_{\text{ventilation}}
$$

Substituting each term:

$$
\left(\tau + \tfrac{\alpha}{2}\right) I A_g = U_g A_g (T_i - T_o) + \rho_{air} c_p \dot{V} (T_i - T_o)
$$

Solving for $T_i$:

$$
\boxed{T_i = T_o + \frac{\left(\tau + \dfrac{\alpha}{2}\right) I A_g}{U_g A_g + \rho_{air} c_p \dot{V}}}
$$

#### Part (b): Numerical Solution

**Given:**
- $A_g = 40 \, \text{m}^2$ (using cover/floor area equivalence for simplification)
- $\tau = 0.85$, $\alpha = 0.10$
- $U_g = 5.5 \, \text{W/m}^2\text{K}$
- $I = 750 \, \text{W/m}^2$
- $\text{ACH} = 1.2 \, \text{hr}^{-1}$, $V_{\text{gh}} = 200 \, \text{m}^3$
- $T_o = 22°\text{C}$
- $\rho_{air} = 1.18 \, \text{kg/m}^3$, $c_p = 1005 \, \text{J/kg·K}$

**(i) Net Solar Heat Gain:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{\text{solar,net}} &= \left(\tau + \tfrac{\alpha}{2}\right) I A_g \\
&= \left(0.85 + \tfrac{0.10}{2}\right) \cdot 750 \cdot 40 \\
&= (0.85 + 0.05) \cdot 30000 \\
&= 0.90 \cdot 30000 \\
&= 27{,}000 \, \text{W} = 27.0 \, \text{kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Steady-State Inside Temperature:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{V} &= \frac{\text{ACH} \cdot V_{\text{gh}}}{3600} = \frac{1.2 \cdot 200}{3600} = \frac{240}{3600} = 0.0667 \, \text{m}^3/\text{s} \\
\dot{m} \cdot c_p &= \rho_{air} \cdot \dot{V} \cdot c_p = 1.18 \cdot 0.0667 \cdot 1005 \\
&= 79.0 \, \text{W/K} \\
U_g A_g &= 5.5 \cdot 40 = 220 \, \text{W/K} \\
\text{Denominator} &= 220 + 79.0 = 299 \, \text{W/K} \\
T_i - T_o &= \frac{27{,}000}{299} = 90.30 \, \text{K} \\
T_i &= 22 + 90.30 = 112.3°\text{C}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** The very high computed $T_i$ signals *excessive solar gain* and *insufficient ventilation*. In real-world design this is **physically impossible** — plants die above 45°C. The student must comment that this means: (a) ventilation must be increased to $\dot{V} \geq 0.5 \, \text{m}^3/\text{s}$ OR (b) shading net (50%) must be applied OR (c) roof vents must be opened. **Failing to interpret results loses 1–2 marks.**

**(iii) Solar Fraction:**

$$
\text{SF} = \frac{27.0 \, \text{kW}}{12 \, \text{kW}} = 2.25
$$

Since SF > 1, the design heating load is *fully met* by solar — SF is capped at **SF = 1.0** for design purposes (no supplemental heater required during solar hours).

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — Choice 2 (Internal Choice Alternative)

**[KTU University Exam – Sample Question, Module 1 Focus]**

**(a)** Explain with neat sketches (i) the various types of greenhouse covering materials, and (ii) their important optical and thermal characteristics. Compare glass, polyethylene film, and polycarbonate on at least four parameters. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A single-span greenhouse in Kerala has a glass cover with $\tau = 0.88$, $\alpha = 0.07$. On a typical December day, the average solar irradiance on the cover is $I = 600 \, \text{W/m}^2$ for 8 hours. Cover area = 60 m². Overall heat loss coefficient $U_g = 6.5 \, \text{W/m}^2\text{K}$. Required inside temperature $T_i = 28°\text{C}$. Average outside temperature $T_o = 18°\text{C}$. Compute: (i) Daily solar heat gain in kWh, (ii) Daily heat loss in kWh assuming 24-hour operation, (iii) Solar fraction. Comment on the result. **(7 marks)**

### 📝 Model Solution to Question B

#### Part (a): Covering Materials — Types and Characteristics

**Types (3 marks):**

1. **Glass** — Rigid, brittle, long-life (25+ years), high light transmission (τ ≈ 0.89), heavy.
2. **Polyethylene (PE) Films** — Flexible, low-cost, short life (2–4 years), τ ≈ 0.80–0.85, light.
3. **Polycarbonate (PC)** — Rigid twin-wall or multi-wall panels, light weight, τ ≈ 0.80–0.85, life 10–15 years.
4. **Acrylic (PMMA)** — Rigid, τ ≈ 0.85, UV-degrading, life 8–10 years.
5. **ETFE Films** — Premium, τ ≈ 0.95, very long life, very low ε.

**Characteristics to be explained:** Transmissivity τ, absorptivity α, reflectivity ρ, emissivity ε, U-value, durability.

**Comparison Table (4 marks):**

| Parameter | Glass | Polyethylene | Polycarbonate |
|---|---|---|---|
| Transmissivity τ | 0.88 – 0.92 | 0.80 – 0.85 | 0.80 – 0.85 |
| Long-wave Emissivity ε | 0.84 | 0.50 – 0.70 | 0.65 – 0.75 |
| U-value (single) $\text{W/m}^2\text{K}$ | 6.0 | 6.5 | 5.5 (twin-wall 3.5) |
| Service Life (years) | 25+ | 2 – 4 | 10 – 15 |
| Weight (kg/m²) | 10 (3 mm) | 0.1 | 1.0 – 1.5 |
| Cost (₹/m² approx) | 350 – 500 | 25 – 40 | 150 – 250 |

#### Part (b): Daily Energy Computations

**Given:** $A_g = 60 \, \text{m}^2$, $I = 600 \, \text{W/m}^2$, $T_i = 28°\text{C}$, $T_o = 18°\text{C}$, $U_g = 6.5$, $\tau = 0.88$, $\alpha = 0.07$.

**(i) Daily Solar Heat Gain:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{\text{solar,net}} &= \left(\tau + \tfrac{\alpha}{2}\right) I A_g = \left(0.88 + 0.035\right) \cdot 600 \cdot 60 \\
&= 0.915 \cdot 36000 = 32{,}940 \, \text{W} \\
\text{Daily solar input} &= 32.94 \, \text{kW} \cdot 8 \, \text{hr} = 263.5 \, \text{kWh}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Daily Heat Loss (24 hours):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{\text{loss}} &= U_g A_g (T_i - T_o) \\
&= 6.5 \cdot 60 \cdot (28 - 18) = 6.5 \cdot 60 \cdot 10 = 3900 \, \text{W} = 3.9 \, \text{kW} \\
\text{Daily heat loss} &= 3.9 \cdot 24 = 93.6 \, \text{kWh}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Solar Fraction:**

$$
\text{SF} = \frac{263.5 \, \text{kWh}}{93.6 \, \text{kWh}} = 2.81
$$

Capped at **SF = 1.0**, the greenhouse requires **no supplemental heating** during the 8 solar hours. **However**, for the remaining 16 night-time hours, a backup heater of $3.9 \, \text{kW}$ capacity is essential to maintain $T_i = 28°\text{C}$. **The student should conclude that the design is energy-positive during the day but requires 62.4 kWh of night-time auxiliary heating.**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Loss of Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to multiply by 8 hours** — solar is *not* 24-hour; KTU specifically tests "daily" vs. "hourly" comprehension.
> 2. **Forgetting to cap SF at 1.0** — physically impossible to exceed 1.0 in design.
> 3. **Missing the night-time heating load** — many students ignore that steady-state heating still needs a backup heater at night.
> 4. **Units mismatch** — kW·hr vs kWh; consistently convert and *state the unit at the end of every numerical answer*.
> 5. **No engineering comment** — KTU 2024 scheme rewards students who *interpret* numbers, not just compute them.

---

## 🔁 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

✅ **Covering Materials — Key Facts:**
- Glass: $\tau \approx 0.89$, $\varepsilon \approx 0.84$, life 25+ years, heaviest, costliest.
- Polyethylene: $\tau \approx 0.85$, $\varepsilon \approx 0.60$, life 2–4 years, cheapest.
- Polycarbonate: twin-wall, $\tau \approx 0.85$, $U \approx 3.5$, life 10–15 years.
- ETFE: $\tau \approx 0.95$, $\varepsilon \approx 0.15$, premium choice for energy-efficient designs.

✅ **Optical Energy Balance (must memorize):**
$$\rho + \alpha + \tau = 1$$

✅ **Net Solar Heat Gain Formula:**
$$Q_{\text{solar,net}} = \left(\tau + \tfrac{\alpha}{2}\right) I A_g$$

✅ **Steady-State Energy Balance:**
$$Q_{\text{solar}} = Q_{\text{cond}} + Q_{\text{vent}}$$

✅ **Inside Temperature Equation:**
$$T_i = T_o + \frac{Q_{\text{solar,net}}}{U_g A_g + \rho c_p \dot{V}}$$

✅ **Solar Fraction:**
$$\text{SF} = \frac{Q_{\text{solar,net}}}{Q_{\text{total load}}}, \quad 0 \leq \text{SF} \leq 1$$

✅ **Design Heuristics to Remember:**
- Single glass: $U_g \approx 6.0$, double PE: $U_g \approx 4.0$.
- Tropics: high ventilation dominates; Kerala needs $0.5$–$1.0 \, \text{m}^3/\text{s}$ per 100 m².
- Night-time heating load = $U_g A_g (T_i - T_o) \times 16 \, \text{hr}$.
- Day-time solar gain = (transmissivity factor) × $I \times A_g \times \text{sun-hours}$.
- Always include **engineering comment** in numerical answers (KTU awards interpretation marks).

✅ **Common Pitfalls (KTU Examiner Perspective):**
- Confusing transmissivity τ with transmittance (angle-averaged).
- Forgetting the ½·α·I·A_g inward-re-radiation term.
- Mixing kW and kWh units.
- Reporting SF > 1 without capping it.
- Ignoring infiltration as a separate loss term.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Greenhouse Heating, Cooling, Shedding and Ventilation Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEAGT721 - DESIGN AND MAINTENANCE OF GREEN HOUSE
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Greenhouse Heating, Cooling, Shedding and Ventilation Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Greenhouse Climate Control – The Fundamental Definition

Greenhouse climate control is the **integrated engineering discipline** of regulating the four cardinal microclimatic variables inside an enclosed crop production structure: **temperature, humidity, light intensity, and carbon-dioxide concentration**. The four sub-systems addressed in this module — **Heating, Cooling, Shading, and Ventilation** — are interdependent and must be designed as a single coupled system rather than isolated units.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Reference):**
> *Greenhouse environmental control systems include the engineering methods and equipment used to maintain the inside air temperature, relative humidity, light intensity, and gas composition within biologically optimal limits for the cultivated crop, irrespective of the external atmospheric conditions.*

### 1.2 The Four Coupled Subsystems

| Subsystem | Primary Function | Controlled Variable |
|---|---|---|
| **Heating** | Adds thermal energy when inside temperature falls below set-point | Air & root-zone temperature (°C) |
| **Cooling** | Removes sensible + latent heat when inside temperature rises above set-point | Air temperature & RH (%) |
| **Shading** | Attenuates incoming solar radiation | Photosynthetic Photon Flux Density (PPFD, µmol·m⁻²·s⁻¹) |
| **Ventilation** | Exchanges inside air with outside air | CO₂, O₂, humidity, heat, pollutants |

### 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy — "The Greenhouse as a Human Body"

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Greenhouse is a Living Organism:**
> Imagine the greenhouse as a **human body** standing in extreme weather:
> - **Heating system** = the metabolic furnace burning food to keep the body warm in winter.
> - **Cooling system** = sweating (evaporative cooling) + the bloodstream carrying heat to the skin.
> - **Shading system** = a hat and sunglasses — controlling how much sunlight hits the skin.
> - **Ventilation system** = the lungs breathing in fresh oxygen and exhaling stale CO₂.
>
> Just as the body must coordinate these systems to stay alive in a desert or arctic environment, the greenhouse engineer must coordinate heating, cooling, shading, and ventilation to keep the **plant** in its narrow biological comfort zone (typically **18 °C to 27 °C** for most vegetables, **60 % to 80 % RH**, and **200–800 µmol·m⁻²·s⁻¹** PPFD).

### 1.4 Why These Systems Cannot Be Designed Independently

> [!WARNING]
> A common KTU exam pitfall: students treat heating and cooling as separate, opposing designs. In reality, **every heating kW installed requires a corresponding cooling kW to remove it during summer**, and **every shade screen reduces winter solar gain by the same percentage**. The design must be **energy-balanced** across the year.

### 1.5 Standard Reference Metrics Used Throughout the Module

- **Outside Design Temperature (T_out):** the lowest (for heating) or highest (for cooling) ambient temperature used for sizing, typically based on a **2.5 % frequency of occurrence** (i.e., the value exceeded only 2.5 % of hours in a year).
- **Inside Set-Point Temperature (T_in):** crop-specific, usually **18 °C** (tomato, cucumber) to **24 °C** (capsicum).
- **U-value (Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient):** typically **5.0 – 6.5 W·m⁻²·K⁻¹** for single-glazed glass, **3.5 – 4.5 W·m⁻²·K⁻¹** for double polyethylene film.
- **Solar Radiation (I):** peak summer midday ≈ **900 – 1000 W·m⁻²** at Kerala latitudes (~8°–12° N).
- **Standard Air Density:** **ρ = 1.20 kg·m⁻³** at 20 °C, 101.325 kPa.
- **Specific Heat of Air:** **c_p = 1.005 kJ·kg⁻¹·K⁻¹**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Greenhouse Heating Systems

Heating is required when **T_out < T_in_set**. The heating load is the sum of all heat losses from the enclosed volume.

#### 2.1.1 Classification of Heating Systems

**1. Central Hot-Water / Steam Heating System**
- A boiler heats water (or generates steam) which is circulated through finned pipes mounted along greenhouse side-walls and under benches.
- Preferred for areas **> 500 m²** because of higher thermal efficiency (**70 – 85 %**).

**2. Hot-Air Unit Heater (Furnace Heater)**
- Direct-fired or indirect-fired air heater with a fan that blows hot air through a polyethylene sleeve (poly-tube) with outlet holes distributed along the length.
- Quick response, low capital cost, but **non-uniform temperature distribution**.

**3. Under-Floor / Root-Zone Heating**
- Hot water pipes (35 – 40 °C) embedded in the soil or growing benches.
- Promotes **root activity and reduces stem elongation**.

**4. Solar Passive Heating**
- Use of **north-wall thermal mass** (water drums, stone), double glazing, thermal screens.

**5. Geothermal Heat Pump**
- Uses stable ground temperature (~20 – 25 °C at 5 m depth in Kerala) as a heat source/sink; COP (Coefficient of Performance) typically **3 – 4**.

**6. Biogas / Biomass Heating**
- Combustion of agricultural residue (coconut coir, rice husk) — particularly relevant in Kerala.

#### 2.1.2 Transmission Heat Loss (Q_trans)

Heat lost through the building envelope (cover, walls, floor):

$$Q_{trans} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} U_i \cdot A_i \cdot \Delta T$$

Where each component *i* is the roof, gable wall, side wall, floor, etc.

#### 2.1.3 Ventilation / Infiltration Heat Loss (Q_vent)

Sensible heat lost when outside air enters and inside air leaves:

$$Q_{vent} = \rho \cdot c_p \cdot V \cdot \Delta T$$

Substituting standard air properties:

$$Q_{vent} = 1.20 \cdot 1.005 \cdot V \cdot \Delta T \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; Q_{vent} = 1.206 \cdot V \cdot \Delta T \;\;[\text{W}]$$

(when V is in m³·s⁻¹). For engineering convenience, if V is in **m³·h⁻¹**, then:

$$Q_{vent} \;[\text{W}] = 0.335 \cdot V \;[\text{m}^3/\text{h}] \cdot \Delta T \;[\text{K}]$$

#### 2.1.4 Total Heating Load

$$\boxed{Q_{heat} = Q_{trans} + Q_{vent} + Q_{floor} - Q_{solar}}$$

where Q_solar is the useful solar gain during daytime (subtracted from the load).

---

### 2.2 Greenhouse Cooling Systems

Cooling is required when **T_out > T_in_set** or when solar load pushes internal temperature above set-point. Cooling methods are classified by the **mechanism of heat removal**.

#### 2.2.1 Classification

**A. Natural / Passive Cooling**
- Roof and side vents (buoyancy-driven stack effect).
- Shade nets / whitewash.

**B. Mechanical (Forced) Cooling**
- Exhaust fans + side inlet shutters.

**C. Evaporative Cooling**
- **Fan-and-pad system** (most common in commercial greenhouses).
- **Fog / mist system** (high-pressure, very fine droplets).

**D. Refrigeration / Heat-Pump Cooling**
- Used only in research / high-value crop greenhouses due to high capital cost.

#### 2.2.2 Evaporative Cooling Effectiveness

The ability of an evaporative cooler to reduce dry-bulb temperature is expressed by **saturating effectiveness (ε)**:

$$\varepsilon = \frac{T_{db,in} - T_{db,out}}{T_{db,in} - T_{wb,in}}$$

- Pad system efficiency: **70 – 85 %**.
- Fog system efficiency: **85 – 95 %**.

#### 2.2.3 Heat Removed by Evaporative Cooling

The required evaporation rate of water:

$$\dot{m}_{water} = \frac{Q_{cool}}{h_{fg}} = \frac{Q_{cool}}{2450 \;[\text{kJ/kg}]}$$

where **h_fg = 2450 kJ·kg⁻¹** is the latent heat of vaporization at 20 °C.

#### 2.2.4 Air Exchange for Cooling

Required ventilation flow rate to remove sensible heat:

$$V_{cool} = \frac{Q_{sensible}}{\rho \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T_{allow}}$$

---

### 2.3 Shading Systems

Shading reduces incoming solar radiation to prevent:
1. Excess leaf temperature (above **30 °C** photosynthesis drops sharply).
2. Scorching of fruits and flowers.
3. Excessive transpiration and wilting.

#### 2.3.1 Classification

| Type | Example | Shading Effect | Adjustable? |
|---|---|---|---|
| **External Fixed** | HDPE shade net 50 % | Reduces light by 50 % | No |
| **External Movable** | Motorized retractable net | Variable 30 – 70 % | Yes |
| **Internal Thermal Screen** | Aluminum / polyester screen | 20 – 60 % | Yes |
| **Liquid Shading (Whitewash)** | Lime + water sprayed on roof | 30 – 50 % | No (seasonal) |
| **Blackout Screen** | Opaque fabric for photoperiod control | 100 % | Yes |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Design Equation — Light Transmission through Shade Net:**
> $$I_{trans} = I_{in} \cdot (1 - S_F)$$
> where S_F is the **shade factor** (decimal, e.g., 0.50 for 50 % net).

---

### 2.4 Ventilation Systems

Ventilation is the **most economically important** control system because it simultaneously manages temperature, humidity, and CO₂.

#### 2.4.1 Natural Ventilation – Stack Effect

Hot air rises and exits through roof vents; cool air enters through side vents.

$$\Delta p_{stack} = \rho_0 \cdot g \cdot H \cdot \frac{T_{in} - T_{out}}{T_{in}}$$

- H = vertical distance between midpoints of inlet and outlet openings (m).
- ρ₀ = 1.20 kg·m⁻³ (outside air density reference).
- g = 9.81 m·s⁻².

#### 2.4.2 Volumetric Flow through an Opening (Orifice Equation)

$$\dot{V} = C_d \cdot A \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2 \cdot \Delta p}{\rho}}$$

- C_d = discharge coefficient ≈ **0.60 – 0.65** for greenhouse vents.

#### 2.4.3 Air Changes per Hour (ACH)

$$\text{ACH} = \frac{\dot{V}}{V_{greenhouse}} = \frac{\dot{V}}{L \cdot W \cdot H_{avg}}$$

Recommended minimum ventilation:
- Winter: **1 – 2 ACH** (humidity & CO₂ control).
- Summer: **20 – 40 ACH** (sensible heat removal).
- Tropical (Kerala): summer peak **up to 60 ACH**.

#### 2.4.4 Fan Sizing for Forced Ventilation

$$\dot{V}_{fan} \;[\text{m}^3/\text{h}] = \text{ACH} \cdot V_{greenhouse}$$

Static pressure drop through pad system typically **10 – 25 Pa**, requiring backward-curved or backward-inclined centrifugal fans.

---

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Physical Meaning | Typical Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Q_trans = Σ U·A·ΔT | Heat loss through envelope | W | Per-component summation |
| 2 | Q_vent = 0.335 · V · ΔT | Heat loss via air exchange | W | V in m³·h⁻¹ |
| 3 | Q_solar = τ · A · I | Useful solar gain | W | τ = cover transmissivity |
| 4 | ε = (T_db,in − T_db,out)/(T_db,in − T_wb,in) | Evaporative cooler efficiency | — | Range 0 – 1 |
| 5 | ṁ_water = Q_cool / h_fg | Water evaporation rate | kg·s⁻¹ | h_fg = 2450 kJ·kg⁻¹ |
| 6 | I_trans = I_in · (1 − S_F) | Light after shading | W·m⁻² | S_F = shade factor |
| 7 | Δp_stack = ρ₀ · g · H · (ΔT/T_in) | Stack-effect pressure | Pa | Drives natural ventilation |
| 8 | V̇ = C_d · A · √(2·Δp/ρ) | Flow through vent | m³·s⁻¹ | C_d ≈ 0.60 – 0.65 |
| 9 | ACH = V̇ / V_house | Air changes per hour | h⁻¹ | 1 – 60 depending on season |
| 10 | V̇_fan = ACH · V_house | Required fan capacity | m³·h⁻¹ | Add 10 % safety margin |
| 11 | COP = Q_useful / W_input | Heat pump efficiency | — | Geothermal = 3 – 4 |
| 12 | RH = (p_v / p_sat) · 100 | Relative humidity | % | Controls transpiration |

> [!NOTE]
> **Universal Gas-Constant Note:** In greenhouse psychrometrics, the relation **ρ = p/(R·T)** is occasionally used with R_air = **287 J·kg⁻¹·K⁻¹**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Total Greenhouse Heating Load

This is the **most frequently asked 14-mark derivation** in KTU examinations on this topic.

**Statement:** Show that the total heating load of a greenhouse is the algebraic sum of transmission, ventilation, and infiltration heat losses, minus the useful solar gain.

**Step 1 — Energy Balance on the Enclosed Volume**

At steady state, the rate of thermal energy entering the greenhouse equals the rate of thermal energy leaving it:

$$\dot{E}_{in} = \dot{E}_{out}$$

**Step 2 — Identify Energy Inflows**

The energy flowing INTO the greenhouse consists of:
1. Heat supplied by the heating system: $\dot{Q}_{heater}$
2. Useful solar radiation transmitted through the cover: $\dot{Q}_{solar} = \tau \cdot A_g \cdot I_g$
3. Heat brought in by incoming ventilation air: $\dot{Q}_{vent,in} = \rho \cdot c_p \cdot V \cdot T_{out}$
4. Metabolic heat from plants (small, often neglected in design).

**Step 3 — Identify Energy Outflows**

The energy flowing OUT consists of:
1. Heat conducted through the envelope: $\dot{Q}_{trans} = \sum U_i A_i (T_{in} - T_{out})$
2. Heat lost via outgoing ventilation air: $\dot{Q}_{vent,out} = \rho \cdot c_p \cdot V \cdot T_{in}$
3. Heat lost by long-wave radiation to the sky (small, included in U-value for design).
4. Latent heat lost by evapotranspiration (significant in tropical climates).

**Step 4 — Apply the Steady-State Energy Balance**

$$\dot{Q}_{heater} + \dot{Q}_{solar} + \rho c_p V T_{out} = \dot{Q}_{trans} + \rho c_p V T_{in} + \dot{Q}_{latent}$$

**Step 5 — Rearrange to Isolate the Heating Load**

Move all heating terms to the LHS:

$$\dot{Q}_{heater} = \dot{Q}_{trans} + \rho c_p V (T_{in} - T_{out}) + \dot{Q}_{latent} - \dot{Q}_{solar}$$

**Step 6 — Identify Each Term with Standard Nomenclature**

$$\boxed{\dot{Q}_{heater} = \underbrace{\sum_i U_i A_i \, \Delta T}_{\text{Transmission}} + \underbrace{\rho c_p V \, \Delta T}_{\text{Ventilation}} + \underbrace{\dot{Q}_{latent}}_{\text{Latent (E.T.)}} - \underbrace{\tau A_g I_g}_{\text{Solar Gain}}}$$

This is the **complete governing equation** used to size the boiler or heater.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Fan-and-Pad Cooling Equation

**Step 1 — Sensible Heat to be Removed**

The peak summer cooling load is dominated by solar radiation through the cover:

$$\dot{Q}_{cool} = \tau \cdot A_g \cdot I_{peak} + \dot{Q}_{infiltration}$$

For a typical Kerala summer day at noon, I_peak ≈ **900 W·m⁻²**, τ ≈ 0.70 for single glass, so solar load ≈ **630 W·m⁻²** of floor area.

**Step 2 — Allowable Temperature Rise of Air Across the House**

Design rule: ΔT across the greenhouse should not exceed **5 – 7 °C** (otherwise plant stress occurs at the fan end).

**Step 3 — Required Volumetric Air Flow**

$$\dot{V} = \frac{\dot{Q}_{cool}}{\rho \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T_{allow}}$$

Substituting numerical values:

$$\dot{V} = \frac{\dot{Q}_{cool}}{1.20 \cdot 1005 \cdot \Delta T_{allow}} = \frac{\dot{Q}_{cool}}{1206 \cdot \Delta T_{allow}} \;\;[\text{m}^3/\text{s}]$$

Convert to m³·h⁻¹:

$$\dot{V} \;[\text{m}^3/\text{h}] = \frac{\dot{Q}_{cool} \;[\text{W}]}{1206 \cdot \Delta T_{allow}} \cdot 3600 = \frac{3.0 \cdot \dot{Q}_{cool}}{\Delta T_{allow}}$$

**Step 4 — Air Changes per Hour**

$$\text{ACH} = \frac{\dot{V}}{L \cdot W \cdot H_{avg}}$$

where L, W, H_avg are length, width, and average height of the greenhouse.

**Step 5 — Pad Area from Air Velocity Across Pad**

For efficient evaporation, face velocity across cellulose pad must be **0.75 – 1.50 m·s⁻¹**.

$$\boxed{A_{pad} = \frac{\dot{V}}{v_{face}} = \frac{\dot{V} \;[\text{m}^3/\text{s}]}{v_{face} \;[\text{m/s}]}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Numerical Sketch (for exam practice):**
> Greenhouse 30 m × 8 m × 4 m, Q_cool = 60 kW, ΔT_allow = 6 K.
>
> $\dot{V} = 3.0 \cdot 60000 / 6 = 30000$ m³·h⁻¹.
>
> $V_{house} = 30 \cdot 8 \cdot 4 = 960$ m³.
>
> $\text{ACH} = 30000/960 \approx 31$ ACH — appropriate for summer cooling.

---

### 3.3 Derivation of the Stack-Effect Pressure for Natural Ventilation

**Step 1 — Hydrostatic Pressure Difference**

Consider two horizontal openings separated by vertical height H, the lower one admitting outside air at T_out, the upper one exhausting inside air at T_in > T_out.

The inside air column of height H has density:

$$\rho_{in} = \frac{p}{R \cdot T_{in}}$$

The outside air column has density:

$$\rho_{out} = \frac{p}{R \cdot T_{out}}$$

**Step 2 — Pressure Difference at the Lower Inlet**

At the inlet level, the inside pressure is:

$$p_{in,inlet} = p_0 + \rho_{in} \cdot g \cdot H$$

The outside pressure at the same level is:

$$p_{out,inlet} = p_0 + \rho_{out} \cdot g \cdot H$$

**Step 3 — Net Driving Pressure**

The pressure difference that drives air through the inlet is:

$$\Delta p = p_{out,inlet} - p_{in,inlet} = (\rho_{out} - \rho_{in}) \cdot g \cdot H$$

**Step 4 — Substitute Density Expressions Using Ideal Gas Law**

$$\Delta p = \frac{p_0 \cdot g \cdot H}{R} \left( \frac{1}{T_{out}} - \frac{1}{T_{in}} \right)$$

**Step 5 — Simplify Using Reference Density ρ₀ = p₀/(R·T_in)**

$$\boxed{\Delta p_{stack} = \rho_0 \cdot g \cdot H \cdot \frac{T_{in} - T_{out}}{T_{in}}}$$

This is the **governing equation** for natural ventilation by thermal buoyancy.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Design Choices for Kerala Climatic Zones

| Design Parameter | Cold Hill Zone (Wayanad, Idukki) | Coastal Zone (Thrissur, Ernakulam) | Hot Dry Zone (Palakkad) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Winter T_out (design) | **8 – 10 °C** | **18 – 20 °C** | **14 – 16 °C** |
| Summer T_out (design) | **28 – 30 °C** | **33 – 36 °C** | **38 – 42 °C** |
| Primary Heating | Hot-water + thermal screen | None / minimal | Solar + heat pump |
| Primary Cooling | Roof vents + fog | Fan-pad + roof vents | Fan-pad + external shade 50 % |
| Recommended Glazing | Double PE film | Single glass + screen | Single glass + 50 % external net |
| Humidity Control | Ventilation + heating | Critical — pad and fan | Less critical |
| Typical U-value (W·m⁻²·K⁻¹) | 3.5 (double film) | 5.5 (single glass) | 5.5 (single glass) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Integrated Greenhouse Climate Control Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Solar Radiation In] --> A2[Cover Glazing]
    A2 --> A3[Greenhouse Interior]
    A3 --> A4{Sensor Network}
    A4 --> A5[Temperature Sensor]
    A4 --> A6[Humidity Sensor]
    A4 --> A7[Light Sensor]
    A4 --> A8[CO2 Sensor]

    A5 --> A9[Controller PLC]
    A6 --> A9
    A7 --> A9
    A8 --> A9

    A9 --> B1[Heating Subsystem]
    A9 --> B2[Cooling Subsystem]
    A9 --> B3[Shading Subsystem]
    A9 --> B4[Ventilation Subsystem]

    B1 --> B1A[Hot Water Boiler]
    B1 --> B1B[Unit Heater]
    B1 --> B1C[Heat Pump]
    B1 --> B1D[Solar Collector]

    B2 --> B2A[Evaporative Pad]
    B2 --> B2B[Fog Nozzle]
    B2 --> B2C[Exhaust Fan]

    B3 --> B3A[External Shade Net]
    B3 --> B3B[Internal Thermal Screen]
    B3 --> B3C[Whitewash Coating]

    B4 --> B4A[Roof Vent Motor]
    B4 --> B4B[Side Vent Motor]
    B4 --> B4C[Inlet Shutter]

    B1A --> C1[Heated Air to Crops]
    B1B --> C1
    B1C --> C1
    B1D --> C1
    B2A --> C2[Cooled Humid Air]
    B2B --> C2
    B2C --> C2
    B3A --> C3[Filtered Light to Canopy]
    B3B --> C3
    B3C --> C3
    B4A --> C4[Fresh Air Exchange]
    B4B --> C4
    B4C --> C4

    C1 --> A3
    C2 --> A3
    C3 --> A3
    C4 --> A3
```

### 4.2 Schematic — Fan-and-Pad Evaporative Cooling System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Outside Hot Dry Air] --> A2[Corrosion Resistant Pad]
    A2 --> A3[Water Soaked Cellulose]
    A3 --> A4[Evaporation Removes Latent Heat]
    A4 --> A5[Cooled Humid Air Enters Greenhouse]
    A5 --> A6[Crop Canopy Absorbs Coolth]
    A6 --> A7[Air Heats Up by Solar Load]
    A7 --> A8[Exhaust Fan at Opposite End]
    A8 --> A9[Hot Stale Air Expelled Outside]

    A9 --> A10[Cycle Repeats Continuously]

    W1[Recirculating Water Pump] --> A3
    W1 --> W2[Water Reservoir]
    W2 --> W1
```

### 4.3 Schematic — Natural Ventilation Stack Effect

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    TOP1[Hot Air Rises Inside] --> TOP2[Roof Vent Opens]
    TOP2 --> TOP3[Stale Warm Air Exits]
    BOT1[Cool Outside Air] --> BOT2[Side Vent Inlet]
    BOT2 --> BOT3[Fresh Air Replaces Volume]
    TOP3 --> BOT3
    BOT3 --> TOP1
```

### 4.4 Block Diagram — Heating System Selection Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1[Greenhouse Floor Area] --> Q2{Larger than 500 m2?}
    Q2 -->|Yes| Q3[Use Central Hot Water Boiler]
    Q2 -->|No| Q4[Use Unit Heaters]
    Q3 --> Q5[Add Root Zone Heating Pipes]
    Q4 --> Q5
    Q5 --> R1{Crop Value High?}
    R1 -->|Yes| R2[Add Geothermal Heat Pump]
    R1 -->|No| R3[Biogas or Biomass Optional]
    R2 --> S1[Integrate with Thermal Screen]
    R3 --> S1
```

### 4.5 Schematic — Shading System Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    X1[Shading Required] --> X2{Adjustable Needed?}
    X2 -->|Yes| X3[Movable Retractable Net]
    X2 -->|No| X4[Fixed HDPE Net 35 to 75 percent]
    X3 --> X5{Mounting Position}
    X4 --> X5
    X5 -->|External| X6[Aluminium Profile Sliding System]
    X5 -->|Internal| X7[Truss Mounted Roller System]
    X6 --> X8[Photoperiodic Blackout Option]
    X7 --> X8
    X7 --> X9[Aluminized Thermal Screen]
    X9 --> X10[Reduces Heat Loss by 35 to 50 percent at Night]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1 (3 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Q: List any three natural ventilation methods used in greenhouses and state the principle behind each.**

**Model Answer (Stepwise, 3 marks):**

1. **Stack (Buoyancy) Effect Ventilation:** Hot air inside the greenhouse becomes less dense and rises, escaping through roof vents; cooler outside air enters through side vents. Pressure difference: Δp = ρ₀·g·H·(ΔT/T_in). *(1 mark)*

2. **Wind-Driven (Cross) Ventilation:** When external wind strikes the greenhouse, a positive pressure forms on the windward side and a negative pressure (suction) on the leeward side. Air enters the windward vent and exits the leeward vent. *(1 mark)*

3. **Combined Stack + Wind Ventilation:** In practice, both effects act together. The total driving pressure is the **algebraic sum** of stack-induced and wind-induced pressure differences. Vent sizing is based on the smaller of the two effects during design calm-wind conditions. *(1 mark)*

---

#### Question 2 (3 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Q: Differentiate between fan-and-pad cooling and fog cooling systems.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Fan-and-Pad Cooling | Fog Cooling |
|---|---|---|
| **Mechanism** | Air drawn through wet cellulose pad; water evaporates into the air stream. | High-pressure nozzles (~70 bar) inject micron-sized droplets directly into the air. *(1 mark)* |
| **Cooling Effectiveness (ε)** | 70 – 85 % | 85 – 95 %. *(1 mark)* |
| **Droplet Size** | Surface wet film on pad | 2 – 60 µm fog particles |
| **Wetting of Plants** | Risk of wetting near pad area | Negligible wetting if humidity-controlled |
| **Water Use** | Higher recirculating volume | Lower, but requires high-pressure pump |
| **Initial Cost** | Moderate | Higher (due to high-pressure plumbing) *(1 mark)* |

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style]**

**(a) (7 Marks)** With a neat sketch, describe the **fan-and-pad evaporative cooling system** for a greenhouse. Derive the expression for the required volumetric air flow rate.

**(b) (7 Marks)** A polyhouse of dimensions **40 m × 10 m × 4.5 m** (L × W × H_avg) is to be maintained at **28 °C** when the outside temperature is **38 °C** and outside wet-bulb is **26 °C**. The cover transmissivity is **0.70**, and the peak solar intensity is **900 W·m⁻²**. Determine:
  (i) The cooling load, (ii) The required fan capacity in m³·h⁻¹ for an allowable ΔT of 6 K, (iii) The pad area if face velocity is 1.0 m·s⁻¹.

---

### **Model Answer to Question A**

#### (a) Fan-and-Pad Evaporative Cooling System (7 marks)

**Working Principle:**
A cellulose pad (typically 100 mm thick, cross-fluted) is installed on one gable end of the greenhouse. An exhaust fan is mounted on the opposite gable end. Water is pumped over the pad and recirculated from a sump tank. *(2 marks)*

**Sketch (ASCII representation for the answer sheet):**

```
   EXHAUST FAN --> |==================|
   (Hot air out)   ||  Greenhouse     ||
                   ||   Interior      ||  <-- PAD <-- Water Pump
                   ||  40 m × 10 m    ||      |
                   ||                 ||      |
                   ||=================|| <-- Sump Tank
                       ▲
                  Cooled Humid
                  Air Inlet
```

**Operation:**
1. Exhaust fan creates negative pressure inside the greenhouse.
2. Outside air is drawn through the wet pad.
3. Water in the pad evaporates by absorbing latent heat from the air, cooling it adiabatically.
4. The cooled air travels across the house, absorbing sensible heat from the crop and structure.
5. Warm air is exhausted by the fan on the far side.

**Derivation of Required Air Flow Rate:** *(4 marks)*

Sensible heat balance: $\dot{Q}_{cool} = \dot{m} \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$

But $\dot{m} = \rho \cdot \dot{V}$, therefore:

$$\dot{V} = \frac{\dot{Q}_{cool}}{\rho \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T} = \frac{\dot{Q}_{cool}}{1.20 \cdot 1005 \cdot \Delta T} = \frac{\dot{Q}_{cool}}{1206 \cdot \Delta T} \;[\text{m}^3/\text{s}]$$

Converting to m³·h⁻¹ (multiply numerator by 3600):

$$\dot{V} \;[\text{m}^3/\text{h}] = \frac{\dot{Q}_{cool} \;[\text{W}] \cdot 3600}{1206 \cdot \Delta T} = \frac{3.0 \cdot \dot{Q}_{cool}}{\Delta T}$$

This is the **standard fan-sizing equation** for evaporative cooling.

---

#### (b) Numerical Solution (7 marks)

**Given:**
- Dimensions: 40 m × 10 m × 4.5 m
- T_in = 28 °C, T_out = 38 °C, T_wb_out = 26 °C
- τ = 0.70, I = 900 W·m⁻²
- ΔT_allow = 6 K, v_face = 1.0 m·s⁻¹

**Step 1 — Floor Area and Solar Cooling Load** *(2 marks)*

Floor area A_g = 40 × 10 = 400 m²

$$\dot{Q}_{solar} = \tau \cdot A_g \cdot I = 0.70 \cdot 400 \cdot 900 = 252{,}000 \;[\text{W}] = 252 \;[\text{kW}]$$

**Step 2 — Add Conduction and Infiltration Allowance (≈ 15 % extra)** *(1 mark)*

$$\dot{Q}_{cool} \approx 1.15 \cdot 252 = 289.8 \;[\text{kW}] \approx 290 \;[\text{kW}]$$

**Step 3 — Required Fan Capacity** *(2 marks)*

$$\dot{V} = \frac{3.0 \cdot \dot{Q}_{cool}}{\Delta T_{allow}} = \frac{3.0 \cdot 290{,}000}{6} = 145{,}000 \;[\text{m}^3/\text{h}]$$

**Step 4 — Air Changes per Hour (Verification)** *(1 mark)*

$$V_{house} = 40 \cdot 10 \cdot 4.5 = 1800 \;[\text{m}^3]$$

$$\text{ACH} = \frac{145{,}000}{1800} \approx 80 \;[\text{h}^{-1}]$$

This is on the **higher side**, indicating that the assumed ΔT_allow of 6 K is too low for the 38 °C ambient. In practice, ΔT_allow would be relaxed to 8 K, reducing ACH to ≈ 60.

**Step 5 — Pad Area** *(1 mark)*

Convert $\dot{V}$ to m³·s⁻¹: $\dot{V} = 145{,}000/3600 = 40.28 \;[\text{m}^3/\text{s}]$

$$A_{pad} = \frac{\dot{V}}{v_{face}} = \frac{40.28}{1.0} = 40.28 \;\text{m}^2$$

**Practical Pad Sizing:**
Two pads, each 2 m high × 10 m long → total 40 m². Height of 2 m is realistic. Length of 10 m is unusual — usually pads are installed on gable wall (max ~10 m here is OK since greenhouse is exactly 10 m wide).

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Valuation Warning:** Students frequently **forget to include the 10 – 15 % safety margin** for infiltration and conduction losses, and they commonly use the **floor area** in the solar-load formula instead of the **roof area** when the greenhouse has significant side-wall solar gain. Always state assumptions clearly.

---

### 5.3 PART B — Alternative Choice Question

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam – May 2023 Style]**

**(a) (7 Marks)** Explain the **principle of natural ventilation in greenhouses** with reference to the stack effect. Derive the expression for stack-effect driving pressure.

**(b) (7 Marks)** A greenhouse of size **50 m × 12 m × 5 m** has roof vents of total area **30 m²** at the ridge and side vents of total area **20 m²** at 1.5 m height. Inside temperature is **32 °C** and outside is **27 °C**. Calculate:
  (i) The driving pressure due to stack effect.
  (ii) The volumetric flow rate of air through the vents (assume C_d = 0.65).
  (iii) The resulting air changes per hour.

---

### **Model Answer to Question B**

#### (a) Natural Ventilation – Stack Effect Principle (7 marks)

**Principle (3 marks):**
When the inside air is warmer than the outside air, the air inside becomes lighter (less dense) and tends to rise. If roof vents are opened at the ridge, this hot air escapes through them. As hot air leaves, a slight negative pressure is created inside the greenhouse, which draws cooler outside air in through the lower side vents. This continuous buoyancy-driven air movement is called the **stack effect** (or chimney effect).

The pressure difference that drives this air movement depends on:
- The vertical height **H** between the centre of inlet and outlet vents.
- The temperature difference **ΔT = T_in − T_out**.
- The inside absolute temperature T_in (in Kelvin).

**Derivation (4 marks):**

At any horizontal level, the hydrostatic pressure inside the greenhouse is:

$$p_{in}(z) = p_{ref} + \rho_{in} \cdot g \cdot (H - z)$$

Outside the greenhouse at the same level:

$$p_{out}(z) = p_{ref} + \rho_{out} \cdot g \cdot (H - z)$$

The pressure difference at level z is:

$$\Delta p(z) = p_{in}(z) - p_{out}(z) = (\rho_{out} - \rho_{in}) \cdot g \cdot (H - z)$$

Using ideal gas law $\rho = p/(RT)$ with $\rho_0 = p/(R \cdot T_{in})$ as reference:

$$\rho_{out} - \rho_{in} = \rho_0 \cdot \left( \frac{T_{in}}{T_{out}} - 1 \right) = \rho_0 \cdot \frac{T_{in} - T_{out}}{T_{out}}$$

Combining and noting that the net driving pressure for the flow equals the **average pressure difference** between inlet and outlet:

$$\boxed{\Delta p_{stack} = \rho_0 \cdot g \cdot H \cdot \frac{T_{in} - T_{out}}{T_{in}}}$$

---

#### (b) Numerical Solution (7 marks)

**Given:**
- L = 50 m, W = 12 m, H_avg = 5 m
- A_roof = 30 m², A_side = 20 m²
- T_in = 32 °C = 305 K, T_out = 27 °C = 300 K
- g = 9.81 m·s⁻², ρ₀ = 1.20 kg·m⁻³, C_d = 0.65

**Vertical separation between vent centres:**
Roof vents at ridge: 5 m
Side vents at: 1.5 m
Therefore H = 5 − 1.5 = 3.5 m *(0.5 mark)*

**Step 1 — Stack-Effect Pressure** *(2 marks)*

$$\Delta p_{stack} = 1.20 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 3.5 \cdot \frac{305 - 300}{305}$$

$$\Delta p_{stack} = 1.20 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 3.5 \cdot 0.01639 = 0.675 \;[\text{Pa}]$$

**Step 2 — Volumetric Flow Rate** *(3 marks)*

Total effective vent area A = A_roof + A_side = 30 + 20 = 50 m² *(0.5 mark)*

Using the orifice equation:

$$\dot{V} = C_d \cdot A \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2 \cdot \Delta p}{\rho}}$$

$$\dot{V} = 0.65 \cdot 50 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2 \cdot 0.675}{1.20}}$$

$$\dot{V} = 32.5 \cdot \sqrt{1.125} = 32.5 \cdot 1.0607 = 34.47 \;[\text{m}^3/\text{s}]$$

Converting: $\dot{V} = 34.47 \cdot 3600 = 124{,}100 \;[\text{m}^3/\text{h}]$ *(0.5 mark)*

**Step 3 — Air Changes per Hour** *(1 mark)*

$$V_{house} = 50 \cdot 12 \cdot 5 = 3000 \;[\text{m}^3]$$

$$\text{ACH} = \frac{124{,}100}{3000} = 41.4 \;[\text{h}^{-1}]$$

This is **adequate for summer conditions** in Kerala. If the wind contribution were also added (typical combined value 1.5–2× stack alone), ACH could approach 60–80.

**Conclusion:** The greenhouse has **sufficient natural ventilation capacity** to handle the 5 K temperature differential using only stack effect.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Valuation Warning (Common Mistakes):**
> 1. Forgetting to convert °C → Kelvin (use 273.15, not 273).
> 2. Mixing units: ρ must be kg·m⁻³, g must be m·s⁻², Δp in Pa (= N·m⁻² = kg·m⁻¹·s⁻²).
> 3. Using absolute vent area instead of effective area (in practice effective area ≈ 0.8 × geometric area due to frame obstruction; KTU often ignores this in simple problems).
> 4. **Forgetting to multiply A_total in the orifice equation** — students often compute separate flows and forget to add them.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Module 2**

- [ ] **Heating load** = Transmission (ΣU·A·ΔT) + Ventilation (0.335·V·ΔT) − Solar gain.
- [ ] Standard values: **ρ = 1.20 kg·m⁻³**, **c_p = 1.005 kJ·kg⁻¹·K⁻¹**, **h_fg = 2450 kJ·kg⁻¹**.
- [ ] **Heat loss coefficient through glass cover** ≈ **5.5 W·m⁻²·K⁻¹**; through **double PE film** ≈ **3.8 W·m⁻²·K⁻¹**.
- [ ] **Evaporative cooling effectiveness** ε = (T_db,in − T_db,out)/(T_db,in − T_wb,in). Range **0.70 – 0.95**.
- [ ] **Pad face velocity** = **0.75 – 1.5 m·s⁻¹** for cellulose pads.
- [ ] **Shading factor** S_F = (shaded radiation)/(incoming radiation); light transmission = (1 − S_F).
- [ ] **Natural ventilation** governed by **stack effect** Δp = ρ₀·g·H·(ΔT/T_in).
- [ ] **Fan sizing** $\dot{V}$ [m³·h⁻¹] = **3.0 · Q_cool [W] / ΔT_allow**.
- [ ] **ACH** = $\dot{V}$ / V_house; tropical summer requires **30 – 60 ACH**.
- [ ] **Kerala-specific** note: humid climate means evaporation efficiency drops; combine fan-pad with **roof vents** for best results.
- [ ] **Geothermal heat pumps** have COP **3 – 4** — energy efficient for cold zones of Wayanad/Idukki.
- [ ] **C_d for greenhouse vents** = **0.60 – 0.65**.
- [ ] Always convert **°C to Kelvin** when using gas-law-derived formulas.

> [!NOTE]
> **Quick Mnemonic — "TVHS":** The four climate-control systems work together — **T**emperature (heating), **V**entilation (air exchange), **H**umidity (cooling + venting), **S**hading (radiation control). Remember to balance all four.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Carbon Dioxide Generation and Monitoring and Lighting Systems, Instrumentation & Computerized Environmental Control Systems
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 2: Carbon Dioxide Generation & Monitoring, Lighting Systems, Instrumentation & Computerized Environmental Control

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEAGT721 - DESIGN AND MAINTENANCE OF GREEN HOUSE
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Carbon Dioxide Generation and Monitoring and Lighting Systems, Instrumentation & Computerized Environmental Control Systems

---

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Carbon Dioxide (CO₂) Enrichment in Greenhouses

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology):**
> **CO₂ Enrichment** is the controlled supplementation of carbon dioxide concentration inside a greenhouse above the ambient atmospheric level (**~350–420 ppm**) to a target level of **800–1500 ppm** during the photoperiod, in order to enhance the rate of photosynthesis and thereby increase crop yield, biomass, and quality.

**Photosynthesis Reaction (Simplified):**
$$6\,CO_2 + 6\,H_2O \xrightarrow{\text{light}} C_6H_{12}O_6 + 6\,O_2$$

The rate of photosynthesis is limited by the **minimum available factor** (Liebig's Law of the Minimum). Inside a closed greenhouse, CO₂ can rapidly deplete below 200 ppm on bright sunny days due to active plant uptake, becoming the limiting factor even when light, water, and nutrients are abundant.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuitive Analogy — "The Restaurant Analogy":**
> Imagine a restaurant kitchen. Even with brilliant chefs (sunlight), fresh water (irrigation), and premium ingredients (nutrients), the kitchen can only produce a limited number of dishes if the supply of cooking gas (CO₂) runs low. CO₂ enrichment is like opening a second gas line — it removes the bottleneck, allowing the entire production line to operate at peak capacity.

### 1.2 CO₂ Monitoring

**Definition:**
> **CO₂ Monitoring** is the continuous, real-time measurement of carbon dioxide concentration within the greenhouse atmosphere using electronic sensors, providing feedback data to the climate control system for automatic regulation of CO₂ supply.

**Common CO₂ Sensing Technologies:**
- **NDIR (Non-Dispersive Infrared) Sensors** — most common, measures IR absorption by CO₂
- **EC (Electrochemical) Sensors** — lower cost, shorter life
- **Photoacoustic Sensors** — high precision laboratory use

### 1.3 Greenhouse Lighting Systems

**Definition:**
> **Greenhouse Lighting** refers to the artificial electromagnetic radiation supplied within a protected cultivation structure to supplement or replace natural sunlight, with the primary goal of driving **Photosynthetically Active Radiation (PAR)** in the **400–700 nm** waveband for crop growth.

**Key Lighting Terminology:**
- **PPFD** — Photosynthetic Photon Flux Density (μmol/m²/s)
- **DLI** — Daily Light Integral (mol/m²/day)
- **PAR** — Photosynthetically Active Radiation (400–700 nm)
- **Photoperiod** — Duration of light exposure (hours)

> [!TIP]
> **Intuitive Analogy — "The Plant Cafeteria":**
> Light is the energy currency of plants. PPFD is like the rate at which energy is served (μmol per second per square meter), while DLI is the *total* energy bill for the day. A plant needs both a sufficient "serving rate" and an adequate "daily total" to grow optimally.

### 1.4 Instrumentation

**Definition:**
> **Greenhouse Instrumentation** encompasses the entire suite of sensors, transducers, signal conditioners, data loggers, and actuators deployed to measure and manipulate the internal microclimate variables (temperature, RH, CO₂, light, soil moisture, pH, EC).

### 1.5 Computerized Environmental Control System (CECS)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:**
> A **Computerized Environmental Control System (CECS)** is an integrated digital automation architecture that uses sensors, microcontrollers/PLCs, and software algorithms to monitor greenhouse microclimate variables in real-time and automatically actuate HVAC, irrigation, CO₂ injectors, lighting, and shading systems to maintain setpoint conditions for optimal plant growth.

> [!NOTE]
> **"Smart Greenhouse" Analogy:**
> Think of CECS as the *autopilot* of an aircraft. The pilot (farmer) sets the destination (setpoints: 25°C, 70% RH, 1000 ppm CO₂), and the autopilot continuously reads instruments, compares them to the target, and adjusts engines, flaps, and rudder to stay on course — all without constant manual intervention.

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why CO₂ Enrichment Works — The Photosynthesis–CO₂ Curve

The photosynthesis rate vs. CO₂ concentration curve has three distinct regions:

1. **Limiting Region (0 – ~300 ppm):** Photosynthesis rises almost linearly with CO₂.
2. **Transition Region (300 – 1000 ppm):** Diminishing returns; stomatal conductance becomes the next limitation.
3. **Saturation Region (>1000–1500 ppm):** Curve plateaus; further CO₂ gives negligible benefit and may harm crops.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Compensation Point** is the CO₂ concentration at which photosynthesis = respiration (net CO₂ uptake = 0). For most C₃ crops, this is around **50–100 ppm**.

### 2.2 Sources of CO₂ Enrichment

| Method | Purity | Cost | Suitability |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Liquid CO₂ (bulk tank)** | ~99.9% | High (capital) | Commercial greenhouses |
| **Combustion of Natural Gas / Propane** | ~5–12% flue gas | Moderate | CO₂ + heat dual benefit |
| **Combustion of Pure Fuels (e.g., paraffin)** | High purity | Moderate | Small/medium houses |
| **Dry Ice (solid CO₂)** | Pure | High per kg | Research/emergency |
| **Fermentation / Biogas** | ~30–40% | Low | Experimental |
| **Chemical Reaction (NaHCO₃ + acid)** | Variable | High per kg | Lab use |

> [!WARNING]
> Combustion-based enrichment must be used with caution: incomplete combustion produces **ethylene (C₂H₄)** which is phytotoxic at concentrations as low as **0.05 ppm**. Always ensure clean burners and proper venting.

### 2.3 CO₂ Distribution & Ventilation Interaction

A critical operational rule:

> **CO₂ enrichment must be stopped when ventilators (roof/side vents) are open beyond a threshold (typically >10–20%), otherwise the enriched CO₂ is immediately vented out — wasting fuel and money.**

The **Air Exchange Rate (ACH)** equation:

$$ACH = \frac{Q}{V_{gh}}$$

where $Q$ is the volumetric airflow rate (m³/h) and $V_{gh}$ is the greenhouse interior volume (m³).

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Photosynthesis basic | $P = \frac{(C_i - \Gamma)}{r}$ | μmol/m²/s | CO₂ limited photosynthesis |
| 2 | CO₂ enrichment rate | $E_{CO_2} = \frac{V_{gh} \times \Delta C}{10^6} \times \rho$ | kg/h | CO₂ dosing |
| 3 | Air exchange rate | $ACH = Q / V_{gh}$ | h⁻¹ | Ventilation |
| 4 | PPFD from luminous flux | $PPFD = \frac{\Phi \cdot \eta_{conv}}{A}$ | μmol/m²/s | Lamp design |
| 5 | Daily Light Integral | $DLI = \int_{day} PPFD \cdot dt$ | mol/m²/day | Light sufficiency |
| 6 | Heat from CO₂ enrichment combustion | $Q = m_{fuel} \times HHV$ | kJ | Energy balance |
| 7 | Ventilation CO₂ loss rate | $L_{CO_2} = ACH \times V_{gh} \times C_{in}$ | m³/h × ppm | Mass balance |
| 8 | Lamp efficacy (μmol/J) | $\eta_{PAR} = \frac{\Phi_{PAR}}{P_{elec}}$ | μmol/J | Energy efficiency |
| 9 | Stefan–Boltzmann radiative heat | $Q_{rad} = \varepsilon \sigma A T^4$ | W | Cooling load |
| 10 | Dew point temperature | $T_d = \frac{b \cdot \gamma(T,RH)}{a - \gamma(T,RH)}$ | °C | Humidity control |

> [!NOTE]
> **Legend:** $C_i$ = internal CO₂; $\Gamma$ = compensation point; $r$ = resistance; $\rho$ = CO₂ density (**1.98 kg/m³** at STP); $HHV$ = higher heating value; $\varepsilon$ = emissivity; $\sigma$ = Stefan–Boltzmann constant (**5.67 × 10⁻⁸ W/m²K⁴**); $a=17.27$, $b=237.7$°C (Magnus formula constants).

### 2.5 Lighting Systems — Detailed Classification

| Lamp Type | Efficacy (μmol/J) | Spectrum | Lifespan (h) | Heat Output | Best Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **HPS (High Pressure Sodium)** | 1.0 – 1.7 | Red-heavy, some blue | 24,000 | High | Flowering, fruit set |
| **MH (Metal Halide)** | 0.8 – 1.4 | Blue-heavy | 15,000 | High | Vegetative growth |
| **LED (White/Full spectrum)** | 2.5 – 4.0 | Tunable | 50,000+ | Low | Modern greenhouses |
| **CFL (Compact Fluorescent)** | 0.6 – 0.9 | Broad | 10,000 | Moderate | Hobby / propagation |
| **Induction Lamps** | 1.2 – 1.6 | Broad | 100,000 | Moderate | Specialized |
| **Plasma Lamps** | 1.5 – 2.0 | Full spectrum | 30,000 | High | Research |

**Photoperiodic Lighting** (e.g., for chrysanthemum, poinsettia) requires *low-intensity* light to trigger flowering, typically **5–10 μmol/m²/s** for several hours at night — a different application from photosynthetic supplemental lighting.

### 2.6 Instrumentation — Sensor Subsystems

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board-Favorite Topic:** Students must remember the *transduction principle* for each sensor type — examiners routinely ask "Which principle does this sensor work on?"

| Measured Variable | Sensor | Principle | Typical Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| Air Temperature | Thermistor / RTD / Thermocouple | Resistance change / Seebeck | –20 to +60 °C |
| Relative Humidity | Capacitive polymer | Dielectric change | 0–100 % RH |
| CO₂ | NDIR | IR absorption at 4.26 μm | 0–5000 ppm |
| Light (PAR) | Quantum sensor | Photodiode + filter | 0–2500 μmol/m²/s |
| Solar radiation | Pyranometer | Thermopile | 0–1500 W/m² |
| Soil moisture | TDR / Capacitance | Dielectric permittivity | 0–100 % VWC |
| pH | Glass electrode | Electrochemical potential | 0–14 |
| EC (electrical conductivity) | 4-electrode cell | Conductivity | 0–10 mS/cm |
| Wind speed | Cup anemometer | Rotation count | 0–40 m/s |

### 2.7 CECS — Control Loop Architecture

A CECS implements a **closed-loop feedback control** based on the classical *measure → compare → actuate* cycle:

$$e(t) = SP(t) - PV(t)$$

where $e(t)$ = error signal, $SP$ = setpoint, $PV$ = process variable.

**Control Algorithms Used:**
- **On/Off (Bang-Bang) Control** — simple, cheap, causes oscillation
- **Proportional (P)** — reduces error proportionally
- **PI (Proportional + Integral)** — eliminates steady-state offset
- **PID (Proportional + Integral + Derivative)** — fast response, no overshoot

The **PID control law**:

$$u(t) = K_p \, e(t) + K_i \int_0^t e(\tau)\, d\tau + K_d \frac{de(t)}{dt}$$

where $K_p$, $K_i$, $K_d$ are the proportional, integral, and derivative gains respectively.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Required CO₂ Injection Rate

**Problem Setup:** A greenhouse of volume $V_{gh}$ has an internal CO₂ concentration $C_{in}$ (ppm). Outside concentration is $C_{out}$. Air exchange rate is $ACH$ (h⁻¹). The CO₂ setpoint is $C_{sp}$. The plant uptake rate is $U$ (μmol/m²/s) over canopy area $A_c$. Find the required CO₂ injection rate $E$ (g/h).

---

**Step 1: Identify the steady-state mass balance.**

At steady state: *Injection + Air ingress = Air egress + Plant uptake*

$$E + (ACH \cdot V_{gh} \cdot C_{out}) = (ACH \cdot V_{gh} \cdot C_{sp}) + U_{total}$$

**Step 2: Solve for E.**

$$E = ACH \cdot V_{gh} \cdot (C_{sp} - C_{out}) + U_{total}$$

*(all concentrations in ppm-volume)*

**Step 3: Convert plant uptake to volumetric-equivalent units.**

Plant uptake $U$ in μmol/m²/s → for canopy area $A_c$ in m²:

$$U_{total} = U \times A_c \;\;[\mu mol/s]$$

Convert μmol to mL at STP using ideal gas (1 mol = 22.4 L = 22.4 × 10⁶ μL):

$$U_{vol} = \frac{U \cdot A_c \cdot 22.4}{10^6} \;\;[\text{mL/s}]$$

Convert mL/s to m³/h:

$$U_{m^3/h} = U_{vol} \times 3.6 \times 10^{-3}$$

**Step 4: Convert ppm volumetric to m³ of pure CO₂ per hour.**

For a greenhouse air volume $V_{gh}$ in m³:

$$1\;\text{ppm} = \frac{V_{gh}}{10^6}\;\text{m}^3 \text{ of CO}_2$$

So the CO₂ loss due to ventilation:

$$L_{vent} = \frac{ACH \cdot V_{gh} \cdot (C_{sp} - C_{out})}{10^6}\;\;[\text{m}^3/\text{h}]$$

**Step 5: Convert volume to mass using CO₂ density at STP (1.98 kg/m³).**

$$\boxed{\,E_{mass} = \rho_{CO_2} \cdot \left[\,\frac{ACH \cdot V_{gh} \cdot (C_{sp} - C_{out})}{10^6}\, +\, \frac{U \cdot A_c \cdot 22.4 \cdot 3.6 \times 10^{-3}}{10^6}\,\right]\,}$$

units: kg/h of pure CO₂ to be injected.

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example

**Given:**
- Greenhouse volume $V_{gh}$ = **1000 m³**
- Canopy area $A_c$ = **200 m²**
- Outside CO₂ $C_{out}$ = **400 ppm**
- Setpoint $C_{sp}$ = **1000 ppm**
- Air exchange rate $ACH$ = **0.5 h⁻¹** (mostly closed house)
- Plant uptake $U$ = **10 μmol/m²/s**

**Step 1: Ventilation loss term.**

$$ACH \cdot V_{gh} \cdot (C_{sp} - C_{out}) = 0.5 \times 1000 \times (1000 - 400) = 300{,}000$$

Divide by $10^6$ → 0.3 m³/h of CO₂ lost.

**Step 2: Plant uptake term.**

$$U \cdot A_c = 10 \times 200 = 2000\;\mu mol/s$$

Convert to m³/h:

$$\frac{2000 \times 22.4 \times 3.6 \times 10^{-3}}{10^6} = \frac{161.28}{10^6} = 1.6128 \times 10^{-4}\;\text{m}^3/\text{h}$$

(Plant uptake is *negligible* compared to ventilation loss — a key insight!)

**Step 3: Total volumetric injection rate.**

$$Q_{inj} = 0.3 + 0.0001613 \approx 0.3002\;\text{m}^3/\text{h}$$

**Step 4: Convert to mass.**

$$E_{mass} = 1.98 \times 0.3002 = 0.594\;\text{kg/h}$$

$$\boxed{E_{mass} \approx 0.59\;\text{kg/h of pure CO}_2}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Daily Light Integral (DLI) from Instantaneous PPFD

The DLI is the time integral of PPFD over the photoperiod:

$$DLI = \int_{0}^{t_{photo}} PPFD(t)\, dt \;\;[\mu mol/m^2]$$

Convert to mol/m²/day:

$$DLI_{mol} = \frac{1}{10^6}\int_{0}^{t_{photo}} PPFD(t)\, dt$$

**For constant PPFD assumption:**

$$DLI = \frac{PPFD_{avg} \times t_{photo}}{10^6}\;\;[mol/m^2/day]$$

**Example:** If average PPFD over a 12-h photoperiod is 250 μmol/m²/s:

$$DLI = \frac{250 \times 12 \times 3600}{10^6} = \frac{10{,}800{,}000}{10^6} = 10.8\;mol/m^2/day$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **DLI Reference Values:**
> - Tomato: 12 – 30 mol/m²/day
> - Lettuce: 12 – 17 mol/m²/day
> - Rose: 15 – 25 mol/m²/day
> - Cucumber: 12 – 20 mol/m²/day

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Decision Logic for CECS

| If PV | And setpoint | Control action | Actuator triggered |
|---|---|---|---|
| T > T_sp | Daytime | Open vents, run fan, shading | Exhaust fans, roof vents |
| T < T_sp | Daytime | Close vents, start heating | Boiler, hot water pipes |
| RH > RH_sp | Any | Increase ventilation, dehumidify | Fans + heater (reheat mode) |
| RH < RH_sp | Any | Misting, fogging, reduce venting | High-pressure foggers |
| CO₂ < C_sp | Day, vents closed | Open CO₂ solenoid valve | CO₂ injector |
| CO₂ < C_sp | Day, vents open | **Disable CO₂** (wasteful) | None |
| Light < L_sp | Daytime | Turn on supplemental lamps | LED / HPS arrays |
| Light < L_sp | Night (photoperiodic) | Low-intensity cyclic lamps | Incandescent / LED strip |
| Soil moisture < θ_sp | Any | Start drip irrigation | Solenoid valve + pump |

### 3.5 PID Tuning — Quick Ziegler–Nichols Method

For a closed-loop step response where the system oscillates at ultimate gain $K_u$ with period $T_u$, the recommended PID gains are:

$$\begin{aligned}
K_p &= 0.6 \, K_u \\
T_i &= 0.5 \, T_u \\
T_d &= 0.125 \, T_u
\end{aligned}$$

where $T_i = K_p / K_i$ and $T_d = K_d / K_p$ (time-form Ziegler–Nichols).

### 4.2 Mermaid State Machine — CO₂ Enrichment Logic

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Idle
    Idle --> CheckTime: Start of photoperiod
    CheckTime --> ReadSensors: Within light period
    CheckTime --> Idle: Night
    ReadSensors --> CompareSetpoint
    CompareSetpoint --> InjectCO2: CO2 below setpoint
    CompareSetpoint --> StopInject: CO2 at or above setpoint
    CompareSetpoint --> Idle: Vents open over threshold
    InjectCO2 --> ReadSensors: After 30 s delay
    StopInject --> ReadSensors: After 60 s delay
    Idle --> [*]: End of day
```

### 4.3 Block Schematic — PID Control Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SP1[Setpoint SP] --> sum1((+))
    PV1[Process Variable PV] --> sum2((-))
    sum2 --> sum1
    sum1 --> err1[Error e t]
    err1 --> pid1[PID Controller]
    pid1 --> out1[Controller Output u t]
    out1 --> plant1[Greenhouse Actuator Process]
    plant1 --> meas1[Sensor]
    meas1 --> PV1
    plant1 --> env1[(Greenhouse Microclimate)]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the loop:** The negative feedback from PV back to the summing junction is the heart of closed-loop control. Without the negative sign, the controller would *amplify* errors instead of correcting them — a classic "runaway" greenhouse system.

### 4.4 NDIR CO₂ Sensor — Internal Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    irsrc1[Infrared Source 4.26 micron] --> chop1[Chopper Wheel Modulated]
    chop1 --> cell1[Sample Gas Cell with CO2]
    cell1 --> filt1[Optical Bandpass Filter]
    filt1 --> det1[Thermopile Detector]
    det1 --> amp1[Pre Amplifier]
    amp1 --> adc1[ADC Converter]
    adc1 --> mc1[Microcontroller with Linearization LUT]
    mc1 --> out1[Analog 4-20 mA or Digital Modbus Output]
    cell1 -.reference.-> refdet1[Reference Detector]
    refdet1 --> mc1
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**Q1.** Define the term **CO₂ compensation point** and state its typical value for C₃ plants. Why is it significant in greenhouse CO₂ management?

**Model Answer:**
The CO₂ compensation point is the concentration of carbon dioxide at which the rate of photosynthesis exactly equals the rate of respiration, resulting in **net CO₂ exchange = 0**. For most C₃ plants, this value lies between **50 and 100 ppm**. It is significant because:
- It defines the **lower limit below which plants cannot survive in a closed environment** for long.
- It serves as the **theoretical baseline** for designing CO₂ enrichment strategies — the setpoint must be *well above* this value.
- In sealed greenhouses overnight (no photosynthesis), CO₂ can drop toward this level, stressing crops.

*(3 marks — 1 for definition, 1 for value, 1 for significance)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Supplementary]**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | PPFD | DLI |
|---|---|---|
| **Full name** | Photosynthetic Photon Flux Density | Daily Light Integral |
| **Definition** | Rate of photon delivery per unit area per second | Cumulative photons delivered per unit area per day |
| **Units** | μmol/m²/s | mol/m²/day |

| **Tomato typical** | 400 – 700 μmol/m²/s (peak sun) | 12 – 30 mol/m²/day |
