### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Main End-Semester]**

**Question A (14 Marks):**

**(a)** Describe the various **methods of CO₂ generation/enrichment** in greenhouses with their relative advantages and limitations. (7 marks)

**(b)** Explain the working principle of an **NDIR CO₂ sensor** with a neat functional block diagram. (7 marks)

---

### Model Solution — Question A

**(a) CO₂ Generation Methods (7 marks):**

1. **Liquid CO₂ from Bulk Tanks (3 marks)**
   - Stored under pressure in insulated cryogenic tanks.
   - Vaporized and metered through a pressure regulator and solenoid valve.
   - **Advantages:** Highest purity (99.9%), precise control, no combustion by-products, no ethylene risk, simple distribution via polyethylene tubes.
   - **Limitations:** Highest capital and recurring cost; logistics of supply; cost rises sharply with scale.

2. **Combustion of Fossil Fuels — Natural Gas/Propane/Paraffin (2 marks)**
   - Burned in a CO₂ generator placed centrally; flue gas (~5–12% CO₂) is mixed with air.
   - **Advantages:** Provides CO₂ *and* useful heat — dual benefit in cold climates; lower running cost than liquid CO₂.
   - **Limitations:** Produces water vapor (raises RH), possible CO (carbon monoxide) and ethylene if combustion incomplete; burner must be regularly serviced.

3. **Dry Ice / Solid CO₂ (1 mark)**
   - Sublimates directly to gas; very pure.
   - **Limitations:** Very expensive per kg; impractical for large houses; used mainly in research and emergency enrichment.

4. **Chemical Generation — NaHCO₃ + Acid or CaCO₃ + Acid (1 mark)**
   - Reaction: $2\,NaHCO_3 + H_2SO_4 \rightarrow Na_2SO_4 + 2\,H_2O + 2\,CO_2\uparrow$
   - **Limitations:** Labor intensive, hard to control rate, scaling issues — used only in laboratory situations.

---

**(b) NDIR CO₂ Sensor Working Principle (7 marks):**

**Principle — Beer–Lambert Law of IR Absorption:**

$$I = I_0 \cdot e^{-\alpha \cdot c \cdot \ell}$$

where $I$ = transmitted intensity, $I_0$ = incident intensity, $\alpha$ = absorption coefficient, $c$ = CO₂ concentration, $\ell$ = path length.

CO₂ has a strong absorption band centered at **λ = 4.26 μm** in the mid-infrared. This is the molecular "fingerprint" used for detection.

**Functional Stages:**

1. **IR Source (1 mark):** A heated filament (typically a black-body source at ~600 K) emits broadband IR. A narrow bandpass filter passes only the 4.26 μm wavelength.

2. **Chopper / Modulator (1 mark):** A rotating wheel chops the IR beam at a fixed frequency (typically 1–10 Hz). This converts DC absorption to AC, which can be AC-amplified to eliminate DC drift and 1/f noise.

3. **Sample Cell (1 mark):** The chopped beam passes through a tube containing the greenhouse air sample. CO₂ molecules absorb energy proportionally to their concentration. Path length is usually 5–20 cm.

4. **Detector (1 mark):** A thermopile or pyroelectric detector converts transmitted IR into a small AC voltage signal.

5. **Reference Channel (1 mark):** A second detector monitors source intensity (no absorption) to compensate for source aging and temperature drift.

6. **Signal Processing (1 mark):** Pre-amplifier → lock-in amplifier locked to chopper frequency → ADC → microcontroller applies linearization (LUT) and outputs **4–20 mA** or **Modbus RTU** signal.

7. **Output (1 mark):** Linearized concentration in ppm, with typical accuracy ±50 ppm and response time <30 s.

*(Total: 7 marks distributed as above)*

---

**Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice:**

**(a)** Discuss the various **types of supplemental lighting systems** used in greenhouses, comparing HPS, MH, and modern LED lamps. (7 marks)

**(b)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **architecture of a Computerized Environmental Control System (CECS)**. Describe any one control algorithm used. (7 marks)

---

### Model Solution — Question B

**(a) Supplemental Lighting Systems (7 marks):**

| Feature | HPS (High Pressure Sodium) | MH (Metal Halide) | LED |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Lamp efficacy (μmol/J)** | 1.0 – 1.7 | 0.8 – 1.4 | 2.5 – 4.0 |
| **Dominant spectrum** | Yellow-red (peak 590 nm) | Blue-rich (peak 450 nm) | Tunable (full spectrum possible) |
| **Lifespan (h)** | ~24,000 | ~15,000 | >50,000 |
| **Heat output** | High (~70% as IR) | High | Low (~20%) |
| **Best growth stage** | Flowering, fruit set | Vegetative | All stages (tunable) |
| **Initial cost** | Moderate | Moderate | High |
| **Lifetime cost** | Moderate–High | Moderate | Lowest |
| **Dimming** | Limited | Limited | Smooth 0–100% |

**Key Points to Include in Answer (1–2 marks each):**
- HPS lamps have a high red:far-red ratio favourable for flowering, but their high heat load increases cooling demand. (1 mark)
- MH lamps are blue-rich and promote compact vegetative growth; they extend lamp spacing issues. (1 mark)
- LEDs dominate modern installations because of high efficacy, low heat, dimmability, and spectrum customization. (1 mark)
- **PAR** range is 400–700 nm; **PPFD** measures instantaneous flux; **DLI** is cumulative daily integral. (1 mark)
- **Photoperiodic lighting** uses very low intensity (5–10 μmol/m²/s) for day-length control, distinct from supplemental photosynthetic lighting. (1 mark)
- Light uniformity requires correct spacing; typical spacing rule = 0.7 × mounting height. (1 mark)
- Calculations using $DLI = PPFD_{avg} \times t_{photo} / 10^6$ for design verification. (1 mark)

---

**(b) CECS Architecture & Control Algorithm (7 marks):**

**Block Diagram Components (1 mark each, total 4):**
- **Sensor layer:** Temperature, RH, CO₂, PAR, soil moisture, pH, EC sensors.
- **Signal conditioning & DAQ:** Amplification, filtering, analog-to-digital conversion.
- **Controller (PLC / Microcontroller / Industrial PC):** Runs the control algorithm; stores setpoints and tuning parameters.
- **Actuator layer:** Fans, vents, heaters, coolers, CO₂ injectors, lamps, irrigation valves, foggers.
- **HMI (Human-Machine Interface):** Touchscreen for operator setpoint entry, alarm acknowledgement, trend graphs.
- **Communication layer:** Modbus, CAN bus, Ethernet/IP for connecting distributed subsystems.
- **Data logger & cloud uplink:** Long-term storage, remote monitoring via smartphone/PC.

**Control Algorithm — PID (3 marks):**

The most widely used control law in CECS is the **PID (Proportional-Integral-Derivative)** controller:

$$u(t) = K_p \, e(t) + K_i \int_0^t e(\tau)\, d\tau + K_d \,\frac{de(t)}{dt}$$

- **P-action (proportional):** $K_p \cdot e(t)$ — responds to *current* error. Larger $K_p$ → faster response but risk of oscillation.
- **I-action (integral):** $K_i \int e \, dt$ — accumulates *past* error. Eliminates steady-state offset (the "droop" of pure P-control).
- **D-action (derivative):** $K_d \cdot \frac{de}{dt}$ — predicts *future* error from rate of change. Adds damping, reduces overshoot.

**Example — Temperature control in a greenhouse:**
- Setpoint $T_{sp} = 25\,°C$, $PV = 23\,°C$, error $e = 2\,°C$.
- $K_p = 30\%/°C$ → proportional output = 60%.
- Integral accumulates over time → if PV stays at 23 °C, integral grows until heater output reaches 100%.
- Derivative acts when temperature starts rising fast (e.g., sun coming out) → pre-emptively reduces heater output to prevent overshoot.

**Tuning (Ziegler–Nichols Ultimate Cycle):**

$$\begin{aligned}
K_p &= 0.6 \, K_u \\
T_i &= 0.5 \, T_u \\
T_d &= 0.125 \, T_u
\end{aligned}$$

where $K_u$ is the gain at which the loop oscillates and $T_u$ is the period of that oscillation.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing C₃ vs C₄ plants:** Many students write 1000–1500 ppm enrichment for *all* crops. Remember: C₃ plants (tomato, cucumber, lettuce) respond strongly; C₄ plants (maize, sugarcane) are *CO₂-insensitive* and enrichment is wasteful.
> 2. **Mixing PPFD and DLI units:** Examiners deduct heavily for unit errors. PPFD is *per second*; DLI is *per day in mol*. Always show the $10^6$ conversion.
> 3. **Forgetting to stop CO₂ during venting:** A classic 2-mark question — never inject CO₂ when vents are open beyond 10–20% threshold.
> 4. **Wrong sensor principle:** Writing "thermocouple measures light" or "NDIR measures humidity" — always pair sensor with its physical principle correctly.
> 5. **Skipping the diagram:** A block diagram is *worth 2–3 marks* in any 7-mark question. Never describe in prose what you can draw.
> 6. **PID sign errors:** The feedback must be *negative* (PV subtracted from SP). Reversing the sign causes the system to *destabilize* — a guaranteed 1-mark loss if missed.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

**CO₂ Enrichment:**
- ☐ Photosynthesis reaction: $6\,CO_2 + 6\,H_2O \to C_6H_{12}O_6 + 6\,O_2$
- ☐ Compensation point: 50–100 ppm (C₃)
- ☐ Enrichment setpoint: 800–1500 ppm during photoperiod only
- ☐ Sources: Liquid CO₂ (purest), combustion (cheapest bulk), dry ice (lab), chemical (lab)
- ☐ **STOP CO₂ injection when vents open >10–20%** — key rule
- ☐ Enrichment rate formula: $E = \rho \cdot [ACH \cdot V \cdot \Delta C / 10^6 + U \cdot A \cdot 22.4 \cdot 3.6 \times 10^{-3} / 10^6]$
- ☐ CO₂ density at STP: **1.98 kg/m³**
- ☐ Enrichment works only during *daytime* (no photosynthesis at night)

**CO₂ Monitoring:**
- ☐ NDIR principle: Beer–Lambert law, absorption at 4.26 μm
- ☐ Reference channel for drift compensation
- ☐ Chopper wheel for AC modulation (eliminates 1/f noise)
- ☐ Output: 4–20 mA analog or Modbus RTU digital
- ☐ Calibration required every 6–12 months; zero calibration using N₂, span using certified span gas

**Lighting:**
- ☐ PAR waveband: 400–700 nm
- ☐ PPFD unit: μmol/m²/s
- ☐ DLI unit: mol/m²/day; formula: $DLI = PPFD_{avg} \times t_{photo} \times 3600 / 10^6$
- ☐ LED efficacy: 2.5–4.0 μmol/J (highest among common types)
- ☐ HPS best for flowering; MH for vegetative; LED universal
- ☐ Photoperiodic lighting uses 5–10 μmol/m²/s (very low intensity)

**Instrumentation:**
- ☐ Match each sensor to its principle: NDIR–CO₂, thermistor–T, capacitive–RH, quantum–PAR, TDR–moisture, glass electrode–pH, thermopile–radiation
- ☐ 4–20 mA industrial current loop standard (robust against voltage drop and noise)
- ☐ Signal chain: Sensor → Transducer → Signal conditioner → ADC → Controller

**CECS:**
- ☐ Closed-loop feedback: $e(t) = SP - PV$
- ☐ PID: $u(t) = K_p e + K_i \int e\, dt + K_d \, de/dt$
- ☐ Ziegler–Nichols: $K_p = 0.6 K_u$, $T_i = 0.5 T_u$, $T_d = 0.125 T_u$
- ☐ On/Off control causes oscillation; P-only has steady-state offset
- ☐ HMI for operator interface; SCADA for multi-greenhouse networks
- ☐ UPS + generator backup for critical actuators (fans, heaters)
- ☐ Data logging for traceability and climate recipe reproducibility

**Golden Formula Triad (Most Frequently Asked):**

$$ACH = \frac{Q}{V_{gh}}\;;\; DLI = \frac{PPFD \cdot t_{photo} \cdot 3600}{10^6}\;;\; u(t) = K_p e + K_i\!\int\!e\,dt + K_d\, \frac{de}{dt}$$

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Watering, Fertilization, Root Substrate and its Pasteurization, Containers and Benches, Plant Nutrition, Alternative Cropping Systems
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEAGT721 - DESIGN AND MAINTENANCE OF GREEN HOUSE
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Watering, Fertilization, Root Substrate and its Pasteurization, Containers and Benches, Plant Nutrition, Alternative Cropping Systems

# Module 3 — Greenhouse Production Engineering & Soilless Cultivation

## 1. Core Technical Definitions & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Watering in Greenhouses
**Formal Definition (KTU 2024):** *Greenhouse watering* is the controlled application of water to crops in a protected cultivation environment to maintain optimal root-zone moisture, taking into account the absence of natural rainfall, the modified microclimate, and the specific physiological stage of the crop.

**Conceptual Analogy:** Think of a greenhouse as a "hospital ICU for plants." Just as an ICU patient receives precisely measured IV fluids at controlled intervals, greenhouse plants depend entirely on the grower to deliver water — they cannot rely on rain. Giving too much is like drowning the patient; giving too little is like dehydration. The grower becomes the plant's "weather system."

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Standard Metrics (KTU Board-Relevant):**
> - Field Capacity (FC) of substrate: **60–70 %** by volume
> - Optimal irrigation volume: **1–2 L/m²/day** for mature vegetables
> - Recommended irrigation water EC: **< 0.75 dS/m**
> - Acceptable pH range: **5.5 – 6.5**

---

### 1.2 Fertilization
**Formal Definition:** *Fertilization* in greenhouse horticulture refers to the scientifically managed application of essential macro- and micro-nutrients to plants via the root substrate, foliar sprays, or fertigation, calibrated to the crop's growth stage, yield target, and substrate analysis.

**Conceptual Analogy:** If water is the "drink" for the plant, fertilizer is the "balanced diet." Just as a human child needs calcium for bones and iron for blood, a tomato plant needs calcium to prevent blossom-end rot and iron to keep leaves green. In greenhouses, we cannot let the plant scavenge randomly from the soil — we *prescribe* the exact diet.

---

### 1.3 Root Substrate (Growing Media)
**Formal Definition:** A *root substrate* is any medium — other than natural field soil — used to physically anchor plant roots and supply water, air, and nutrients in a controlled horticulture system. Substrates are characterized by their porosity, water-holding capacity, cation exchange capacity (CEC), pH buffering, and sterility.

**Conceptual Analogy:** A root substrate is like the "mattress" of the plant. A good mattress is firm enough to support the body (anchorage), porous enough to allow airflow (root aeration), retains some moisture but does not flood (water holding + drainage), and is free of pests/dust mites (sterility). Field soil is a "dirty old mattress" — substrates are engineered, clean, and predictable.

> [!NOTE]
> **Common Greenhouse Substrates:**
> Peat moss, perlite, vermiculite, coco coir, rockwool, pine bark, sand, expanded clay pellets (LECA), and polyurethane foam.

---

### 1.4 Pasteurization of Substrate
**Formal Definition:** *Pasteurization* is the heat or chemical treatment of root substrate to eliminate harmful pathogens (fungi, bacteria, nematodes), insect eggs, and weed seeds, while preserving beneficial thermophilic microflora. Standard treatment target: heating the substrate to **70 °C for 30 minutes** (USDA standard).

**Conceptual Analogy:** Pasteurizing substrate is like "boiling the baby bottle." You don't want to sterilize it so completely that even good bacteria die — you just want to kill the harmful germs. Milk pasteurization works the same way.

---

### 1.5 Containers and Benches
**Formal Definition:**
- **Container:** A vessel (pot, bag, trough, slab) that physically holds the root substrate and confines the root zone.
- **Bench:** A raised structure inside the greenhouse used to hold containers, improve ergonomics, optimize light, and enable efficient drainage and air circulation around crops.

**Conceptual Analogy:** Containers are "plant apartments" (each plant gets its own bounded space with controlled inputs). Benches are the "building floors" that hold these apartments at a convenient working height for the gardener.

---

### 1.6 Plant Nutrition
**Formal Definition:** *Plant nutrition* is the study of how plants absorb, translocate, assimilate, and utilize essential chemical elements (nutrients) for growth, development, fruiting, and reproduction. The **17 essential elements** are divided into **macronutrients** (needed in large amounts) and **micronutrients** (needed in trace amounts).

**Conceptual Analogy:** Plant nutrition is the "ingredient list" of a plant's biochemistry. Just as a cake needs flour, sugar, eggs, and a pinch of salt, a plant needs nitrogen, phosphorus, potassium, and tiny amounts of zinc or boron to build proteins, DNA, and enzymes.

---

### 1.7 Alternative Cropping Systems
**Formal Definition:** *Alternative cropping systems* in greenhouse horticulture refer to non-traditional cultivation methods that do not rely on natural field soil. They include **hydroponics** (roots in nutrient solution), **aeroponics** (roots in air/mist), **aquaponics** (integration with fish culture), and **vertical farming** (multi-tier indoor production).

**Conceptual Analogy:** Traditional farming is like cooking in a "field kitchen" — messy, weather-dependent, and inefficient. Alternative cropping systems are like "molecular gastronomy" — precise, controlled, clean, and reproducible.

> [!TIP]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** KTU expects students to clearly distinguish between *Open Substrate Systems* (NFT, DFT) and *Closed Substrate Systems* (rockwool slabs with recirculation) — this is a frequently asked question.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Greenhouse Watering — Theoretical Breakdown

### 2.1.1 Why Greenhouse Watering is Unique
- **Zero rainfall input** → 100 % of water must be supplied artificially.
- **Higher temperatures & VPD** → Transpiration can be **2–3 ×** higher than field conditions.
- **Restricted root volume** in containers → water buffering is minimal.
- **Quality control critical** — Salinity buildup is rapid due to lack of leaching by rain.

### 2.1.2 Watering Methods (Hierarchical Classification)

| Method | Principle | Crop Suitability |
|---|---|---|
| Manual Hand Watering | Hoses / cans | Small greenhouses, nurseries |
| Overhead Sprinklers | Spray from above | Beds, low tunnels |
| Drip Irrigation | Slow release at root zone | Pots, bags, slabs (most common) |
| Ebb & Flow (Flood-Drain) | Periodic flooding of bench | Bedding plants, herbs |
| NFT (Nutrient Film Technique) | Thin film of flowing solution | Leafy greens |
| DFT (Deep Flow Technique) | Deep static solution | Lettuce, spinach |
| Sub-Irrigation | Capillary mat from below | Potted ornamentals |
| Aeroponic Mist | Nutrient mist to bare roots | High-value research crops |

### 2.1.3 The 'Why' Behind Each Method
- **Drip** → saves water, allows fertigation, no foliage wetting → less disease.
- **Ebb & Flow** → uniform watering, low labor, but risk of root pathogens spreading.
- **NFT** → maximum oxygenation, super-fast growth, but catastrophic if pump fails.

---

## 2.2 Fertilization — Engineering View

### 2.2.1 The Three Pillars of Fertilization
1. **Source** — fertilizer type (straight, compound, controlled-release).
2. **Rate** — kg/ha or ppm in solution.
3. **Timing** — frequency tied to growth stage.

### 2.2.2 Fertigation Formula (High Yield for KTU)

**Concentration in ppm formula:**

$$C_{ppm} = \frac{\text{Mass of fertilizer (mg)}}{\text{Volume of solution (L)}}$$

**Conversion: ppm to kg/ha** (assuming 1 ha irrigation depth = 1 mm/m² ≈ 10 m³/ha per mm):

$$\text{kg/ha} = \frac{C_{ppm} \times \text{Irrigation depth (mm)}}{100}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rule of Thumb:** A 100 ppm N solution = ~0.1 g N per litre of water.

### 2.2.3 Electrical Conductivity (EC) as a Fertility Indicator

$$EC \left(\frac{dS}{m}\right) \approx \frac{TDS (ppm)}{640}$$

| Crop | Optimal EC (dS/m) |
|---|---|
| Tomato | 2.5 – 3.5 |
| Cucumber | 1.8 – 2.5 |
| Lettuce | 1.2 – 1.8 |
| Roses | 1.5 – 2.5 |

---

## 2.3 Root Substrate — Engineering Properties

### 2.3.1 The Four Must-Have Properties

| Property | Ideal Range | Engineering Significance |
|---|---|---|
| Total Porosity | **70 – 90 %** | Air + water storage volume |
| Air-Filled Porosity | **15 – 25 %** | Prevents root suffocation |
| Water-Holding Capacity | **50 – 70 %** | Drought buffering |
| Bulk Density | **0.1 – 0.8 g/cm³** | Handling weight |

### 2.3.2 Cation Exchange Capacity (CEC)

$$CEC = \frac{\text{cmol of exchangeable cations}}{100 \text{ g of dry substrate}}$$

> Higher CEC = greater nutrient buffering (clay, peat = high CEC; sand, perlite = low CEC).

### 2.3.3 Common Substrate Composition (Example Mix)

- **Peat : Perlite : Vermiculite = 2 : 1 : 1** → Standard propagation mix.
- **Coco Coir : Perlite = 3 : 1** → Popular tomato slab alternative.

---

## 2.4 Pasteurization — Engineering Process

### 2.4.1 Why Pasteurize?
- Eliminate *Pythium*, *Rhizoctonia*, *Fusarium*, nematodes.
- Kill weed seeds.
- Reduce insect larvae.

### 2.4.2 Methods of Pasteurization

| Method | Temperature/Agent | Time | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Steam (USDA Standard) | 70 °C | 30 min | Most reliable |
| Hot Water drench | 80 – 90 °C | Until drained | Risk of waterlogging |
| Solarization | 60 – 70 °C (sun) | 4 – 6 weeks | Cost-free but slow |
| Chemical (Methyl Bromide – banned) | – | – | Phased out under Montreal Protocol |
| Chemical (Basamid – Dazomet) | 35 – 50 g/m² | 2 – 3 weeks | Modern alternative |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Concept — Beneficial Microflora:** Above 82 °C, beneficial *Trichoderma* and *Bacillus* are destroyed → re-inoculation needed. This is why pasteurization (not sterilization) is the engineering target.

---

## 2.5 Containers and Benches — Structural Engineering

### 2.5.1 Container Selection Criteria
- **Drainage holes** — must allow ≥ 15 % of volume to drain per watering.
- **Color** — black for outdoor (blocks UV); white for greenhouse (reflects heat).
- **Material** — plastic (cheap, light), terracotta (breathable, heavy), fabric (smart pots — air-pruning roots).

### 2.5.2 Bench Engineering Standards
- **Height:** 70 – 90 cm (ergonomic working height).
- **Width:** ≤ 1.5 m (one-sided reach) or ≤ 2.5 m (double-sided).
- **Slope for drainage:** 1 – 2 % (≈ 1 cm per metre).
- **Load capacity:** 100 – 150 kg/m² (substrate + water + plants).
- **Spacing between benches:** ≥ 60 cm (worker + trolley access).

---

## 2.6 Plant Nutrition — Essential Elements

### 2.6.1 The 17 Essential Elements

| Category | Elements | Form Absorbed |
|---|---|---|
| **Primary Macronutrients** | Nitrogen (N), Phosphorus (P), Potassium (K) | NO₃⁻, NH₄⁺, H₂PO₄⁻, K⁺ |
| **Secondary Macronutrients** | Calcium (Ca), Magnesium (Mg), Sulfur (S) | Ca²⁺, Mg²⁺, SO₄²⁻ |
| **Micronutrients** | Fe, Mn, Zn, Cu, B, Mo, Cl, Ni | Ions/chelates |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Law of the Minimum (Liebig's Law):** Growth is limited by the scarcest essential nutrient, regardless of the abundance of the others. *"A chain is only as strong as its weakest link."*

### 2.6.2 Mobility-Based Deficiency Diagnosis

| Nutrient | Mobility in plant | Deficiency appears first in |
|---|---|---|
| N, P, K, Mg | Mobile | Older (lower) leaves |
| Ca, Fe, B, Cu, S | Immobile | Younger (upper) leaves |

---

## 2.7 Alternative Cropping Systems — The Big Picture

### 2.7.1 Classification

1. **Hydroponics** (water-based)
   - Open system: NFT, DFT (no recirculation)
   - Closed system: Rockwool recirculation, Ebb & Flow
2. **Aeroponics** (air-based with mist)
3. **Aquaponics** (fish + plants symbiosis)
4. **Vertical Farming** (stacked trays, LED-driven)

### 2.7.2 Why Choose Alternative Systems?
- **Water saving** → up to **90 %** less water vs. soil.
- **Yield increase** → 3 – 10 × higher per unit area.
- **No soil-borne disease** → sterile root zone.
- **Year-round production** → climate independent.
- **Urban feasibility** → can be done in warehouses.

### 2.7.3 Engineering Limitation
- **Power dependency** — one hour pump failure in NFT = crop loss.
- **Capital cost** — 5 – 20 × higher initial investment.
- **Skill demand** — requires trained operators.

---

### 📋 KTU Formula & Boundary Condition Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Equation | Units / Range |
|---|---|---|
| Fertigation concentration | $C_{ppm} = \dfrac{m_{fert}(mg)}{V_{sol}(L)}$ | ppm = mg/L |
| ppm to kg/ha | $\text{kg/ha} = \dfrac{C_{ppm} \times d}{100}$ | d = depth in mm |
| EC to TDS | $TDS = EC \times 640$ | ppm vs. dS/m |
| Bulk Density | $\rho_b = \dfrac{M_{dry}}{V_{total}}$ | g/cm³ |
| Porosity | $P(\%) = \dfrac{V_{voids}}{V_{total}} \times 100$ | % (ideal 70–90) |
| Pasteurization target | T = 70 °C, t = 30 min | USDA Std. |
| Optimal pH | 5.5 – 6.5 | – |
| Optimal EC | 1.2 – 3.5 dS/m | Crop dependent |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Fertigation Concentration Calculation

**Problem:** A greenhouse operator wants to prepare **1000 litres** of nutrient solution containing **150 ppm Nitrogen** using **Calcium Nitrate [Ca(NO₃)₂·4H₂O]** which is **15.5 % N by mass**.

### Step 1 — Convert ppm to mg per litre
$$C_{ppm} = 150 \text{ ppm} = 150 \text{ mg N per Litre of water}$$

### Step 2 — Compute total N needed for 1000 L
$$m_{N,total} = 150 \frac{mg}{L} \times 1000 \text{ L} = 150{,}000 \text{ mg} = 150 \text{ g}$$

### Step 3 — Account for purity of fertilizer
Since the fertilizer is only **15.5 % N** by mass:

$$m_{fert} = \frac{m_{N,total}}{\text{Fraction of N}} = \frac{150 \text{ g}}{0.155}$$

### Step 4 — Numerical Evaluation
$$m_{fert} = 967.74 \text{ g} \approx 968 \text{ g of Ca(NO}_3)_2 \cdot 4H_2O$$

### Step 5 — Engineering Conclusion
> To make 1000 L of 150 ppm N solution, dissolve **968 g of Calcium Nitrate tetrahydrate** in water, then make up the final volume to 1000 L.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Porosity of a Root Substrate

**Given:** A sample of coco coir occupies a measuring cylinder with:
- Total volume, $V_{total} = 250 \text{ cm}^3$
- Solid particle volume, $V_{solid} = 50 \text{ cm}^3$
- Water holding at saturation, $V_{water} = 160 \text{ cm}^3$

### Step 1 — Compute void volume
$$V_{voids} = V_{total} - V_{solid} = 250 - 50 = 200 \text{ cm}^3$$

### Step 2 — Total Porosity
$$P_{total} = \frac{V_{voids}}{V_{total}} \times 100 = \frac{200}{250} \times 100 = 80 \%$$

### Step 3 — Air-Filled Porosity (after free drainage)
$$V_{air} = V_{voids} - V_{water} = 200 - 160 = 40 \text{ cm}^3$$

$$\text{Air-Filled Porosity} = \frac{40}{250} \times 100 = 16 \%$$

### Step 4 — Water-Holding Capacity
$$WHC = \frac{V_{water}}{V_{total}} \times 100 = \frac{160}{250} \times 100 = 64 \%$$

### Step 5 — Engineering Verdict
> The coco coir substrate has **Total Porosity 80 %**, **Air Porosity 16 %**, and **WHC 64 %** — all within the **ideal range**. ✅ It is suitable for greenhouse tomato cultivation.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Steam Pasteurization Time–Temperature Relationship

**USDA Rule:** Pathogens are killed when substrate reaches **70 °C for 30 minutes**.

The **thermal death time (TDT)** follows first-order Arrhenius kinetics:

$$\log \left(\frac{N}{N_0}\right) = -\frac{k \cdot t}{2.303}$$

Where:
- $N$ = surviving pathogen count
- $N_0$ = initial pathogen count
- $k$ = thermal death rate constant (at 70 °C, $k \approx 1.5 \text{ min}^{-1}$ for *Pythium*)
- $t$ = time (minutes)

### Step 1 — Required log reduction
For 99.9 % kill (3-log reduction):
$$\log \left(\frac{N}{N_0}\right) = \log(0.001) = -3$$

### Step 2 — Solve for time t
$$-3 = -\frac{1.5 \times t}{2.303}$$

$$t = \frac{3 \times 2.303}{1.5} = \frac{6.909}{1.5} \approx 4.6 \text{ min}$$

### Step 3 — Apply Engineering Safety Factor
Because substrate has non-uniform heating (cold pockets), multiply by **safety factor 6.5**:
$$t_{design} = 4.6 \times 6.5 \approx 30 \text{ min}$$

> This is why the **USDA standard is 70 °C / 30 min** — it includes a safety margin to ensure pathogen kill at the *coldest* point in the substrate pile.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Substrates

| Property | Peat | Perlite | Vermiculite | Coco Coir | Rockwool |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Bulk Density (g/cm³) | 0.1 | 0.1 | 0.4 | 0.08 | 0.07 |
| Total Porosity (%) | 85 | 75 | 80 | 85 | 95 |
| WHC (%) | 70 | 35 | 60 | 70 | 80 |
| Air Porosity (%) | 15 | 40 | 20 | 15 | 15 |
| CEC (cmol/kg) | 100 | 1.5 | 25 | 60 | 1.0 |
| pH | 3.5 – 4.5 | 7.0 | 7.0 | 5.5 – 6.5 | 7.0 |
| Renewable? | No (slow) | Yes (volcanic) | Yes (mineral) | Yes (coconut) | No (energy-intensive) |
| Cost | Medium | Low | Low | Low | High |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering takeaway:** Rockwool has high WHC but zero CEC — meaning **all nutrition must be supplied via fertigation** (no buffering). Peat is highly buffered but acidic — needs **liming**.

---

## 3.5 Derivation: Irrigation Water Requirement (Penman-Monteith Simplified)

$$ET_c = K_c \times ET_0$$

Where:
- $ET_c$ = Crop evapotranspiration (mm/day)
- $K_c$ = Crop coefficient (e.g., Tomato = 1.15)
- $ET_0$ = Reference evapotranspiration (mm/day)

**Example:** In a Kerala greenhouse, $ET_0 = 5 \text{ mm/day}$, $K_c = 1.15$:
$$ET_c = 1.15 \times 5 = 5.75 \text{ mm/day}$$

**Convert to litres per plant** (plant spacing 0.5 m × 0.5 m, area = 0.25 m²):
$$V_{plant} = 5.75 \times 0.25 = 1.44 \text{ L/plant/day}$$

> This is the **engineering basis** for drip irrigation scheduling in greenhouses.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Greenhouse Irrigation & Fertigation System Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Water Source] --> B[Sand Filter]
    B --> C[Screen Filter 130 micron]
    C --> D[Fertilizer Injector A and B]
    D --> E[EC and pH Sensor]
    E --> F[Manifold and Solenoid Valves]
    F --> G1[Drip Line Bench 1]
    F --> G2[Drip Line Bench 2]
    F --> G3[Drip Line Bench 3]
    G1 --> H1[Container Substrate]
    G2 --> H2[Container Substrate]
    G3 --> H3[Container Substrate]
    H1 --> I1[Drainage Tray]
    H2 --> I2[Drainage Tray]
    H3 --> I3[Drainage Tray]
    I1 --> J1[Collection Sump]
    I2 --> J1
    I3 --> J1
    J1 --> K{Recirculate or Discharge}
    K -- Recirculate --> L[UV Sterilizer] --> D
    K -- Discharge --> M[Waste]
```

---

## 4.2 Root Substrate Selection Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Choose Root Substrate] --> Q1{Crop Type}
    Q1 -- Vegetables --> Q2{Growing System}
    Q1 -- Ornamentals --> Q3
    Q2 -- Slab --> S1[Rockwool or Coco Slab]
    Q2 -- Pot --> S2[Peat and Perlite Mix 2:1]
    Q2 -- NFT --> S3[No substrate inert mat]
    Q3 -- Potted --> S4[Peat Bark Mix]
    Q3 -- Cut Flowers --> S5[Perlite or LECA]
    S1 --> P[Check EC pH Pasteurize]
    S2 --> P
    S3 --> P
    S4 --> P
    S5 --> P
    P --> Done[Plant Ready]
```

---

## 4.3 Pasteurization Process Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Raw Substrate] --> B[Pre moisten 60 percent]
    B --> C[Fill Pasteurization Chamber]
    C --> D[Steam Injection or Hot Water]
    D --> E{Temperature Sensor 70 C}
    E -- Not Reached --> D
    E -- Reached --> F[Hold for 30 Minutes]
    F --> G[Cool Down Phase]
    G --> H[Re inoculate Beneficial Microbes]
    H --> I[Ready for Planting]
```

---

## 4.4 Alternative Cropping Systems Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Root[Alternative Cropping Systems] --> A1[Hydroponics]
    Root --> A2[Aeroponics]
    Root --> A3[Aquaponics]
    Root --> A4[Vertical Farming]
    A1 --> B1[Open System NFT DFT]
    A1 --> B2[Closed System Rockwool Recirc]
    A1 --> B3[Ebb and Flow]
    A2 --> C1[High Pressure Mist]
    A2 --> C2[Ultrasonic Fogger]
    A3 --> D1[Recirculating Aquaponics]
    A3 --> D2[Decoupled Aquaponics]
    A4 --> E1[Stacked Trays]
    A4 --> E2[LED Driven A Frame]
    A4 --> E3[Container Farm]
```

---

## 4.5 Plant Nutrient Deficiency Diagnostic Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Plant Shows Deficiency] --> B{Symptom Location}
    B -- Older Leaves --> C[Mobile Nutrient]
    C --> C1[Yellowing Uniform N]
    C --> C2[Purple Tint P]
    C --> C3[Leaf Edge Burn K]
    C --> C4[Interveinal Yellow Mg]
    B -- Younger Leaves --> D[Immobile Nutrient]
    D --> D1[Tip Burn Ca]
    D --> D2[Interveinal Chlorosis Fe]
    D --> D3[Distorted Growth B]
    D --> D4[Whiptail Mo]
```

---

## 4.6 Greenhouse Bench Layout — Top View Schematic

```mermaid

    subgraph GH[Greenhouse Span]
        direction LR
        W1[Walkway] --- B1[Bench A 1.2m wide]
        B1 --- W2[Walkway 0.7m]
        W2 --- B2[Bench B 1.2m wide]
        B2 --- W3[Walkway 0.7m]
        W3 --- B3[Bench C 1.2m wide]
        B3 --- W4[Walkway]
    end
    B1 --> D1[Slope 1 percent to Drain]
    B2 --> D2[Slope 1 percent to Drain]
    B3 --> D3[Slope 1 percent to Drain]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define root substrate. List any four ideal properties of a good greenhouse substrate. **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Set B]**

**Model Answer:**
A *root substrate* is a soilless medium used in greenhouses to physically anchor plant roots and to supply water, air, and nutrients in a precisely controlled manner.

Four ideal properties:
1. **High total porosity (70 – 90 %)** — for both air and water storage.
2. **Optimum water-holding capacity (50 – 70 %)** — drought buffering.
3. **Adequate air-filled porosity (15 – 25 %)** — prevents anaerobic root rot.
4. **Good cation exchange capacity (CEC)** — retains and slowly releases nutrients.

*Additional relevant properties: low bulk density, pathogen-free, stable pH, free from weeds/toxins.*

---

### Question 2: What is substrate pasteurization? State the USDA standard for steam pasteurization. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer:**
*Substrate pasteurization* is the partial sterilization of a growing medium to destroy harmful pathogens (fungi, bacteria, nematodes), insect eggs, and weed seeds, while preserving beneficial thermophilic microflora.

**USDA Standard:** Heat the substrate to a uniform temperature of **70 °C and hold for 30 minutes** at the coldest point of the pile.

*Key distinction:* Pasteurization (not sterilization) — sterilization at > 100 °C destroys all microbes, requiring re-inoculation; pasteurization retains a beneficial microbial community.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### ✅ Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks

#### (a) Explain in detail the various methods of irrigation used in greenhouse cultivation. Highlight the advantages of drip irrigation. **[7 Marks]** **[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

**Model Answer:**

**Major Greenhouse Irrigation Methods:**

1. **Manual Hand Watering**
   - Simple hose and watering can.
   - Suitable for small nurseries and research plots.
   - *Disadvantage:* labour-intensive, non-uniform.

2. **Overhead Sprinklers / Boom Irrigation**
   - Sprinklers mounted on a moving boom that travels across the greenhouse.
   - Provides uniform coverage; can also be used for foliar fertilizer/pesticide.
   - *Disadvantage:* wets foliage → increases fungal disease risk (e.g., *Botrytis*).

3. **Drip Irrigation** (Most widely used in protected cultivation)
   - Water delivered via emitters (1 – 8 L/hr) directly to the root zone.
   - Operates at low pressure (1 – 2 bar).
   - **Advantages:**
     - Water saving up to **50 – 70 %** vs. flood irrigation.
     - No leaf wetting → reduced disease.
     - Allows **fertigation** (fertilizer + water simultaneously).
     - Precise control of volume and timing.
     - Compatible with automation and EC/pH sensors.
   - *Disadvantage:* emitter clogging — needs good filtration.

4. **Ebb-and-Flow (Flood and Drain)**
   - Bench is periodically flooded with nutrient solution, then drained.
   - Substrate absorbs water by capillarity.
   - **Advantage:** uniform, low labour, no emitters.
   - **Disadvantage:** risk of *Pythium* spread if contaminated.

5. **NFT (Nutrient Film Technique)**
   - A thin film (2 – 3 mm) of nutrient solution flows continuously through channels holding bare roots.
   - **Advantage:** maximum oxygenation → fast growth.
   - **Disadvantage:** total crop loss if pump stops for > 1 hr.

6. **DFT (Deep Flow Technique)**
   - Solution depth 5 – 20 cm; roots submerged.
   - **Used for** lettuce, spinach.

7. **Sub-Irrigation / Capillary Mat**
   - Plants absorb water from a moist mat beneath pots by capillary action.
   - Excellent for ornamentals.

8. **Aeroponic Mist**
   - Roots suspended in air, misted with nutrient solution every few minutes.
   - Highest oxygenation; used for research and high-value crops.

> **Conclusion:** Drip irrigation is the most popular method in greenhouses due to its water economy, compatibility with fertigation, and disease suppression.

---

#### (b) Discuss the essential plant nutrients, their classification, and the role of each macronutrient in plant growth. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**The 17 Essential Elements** (as defined by Arnon & Stout, 1939):
- A plant cannot complete its life cycle without the element.
- The element must be directly involved in plant metabolism.

**Classification:**

**(I) Macronutrients (required in large quantities):**
1. **Primary:** Nitrogen (N), Phosphorus (P), Potassium (K)
2. **Secondary:** Calcium (Ca), Magnesium (Mg), Sulfur (S)

**(II) Micronutrients (trace amounts):**
Iron (Fe), Manganese (Mn), Zinc (Zn), Copper (Cu), Boron (B), Molybdenum (Mo), Chlorine (Cl), Nickel (Ni)

**Roles of Macronutrients:**

| Nutrient | Form Absorbed | Key Functions |
|---|---|---|
| **Nitrogen (N)** | NO₃⁻, NH₄⁺ | Component of chlorophyll, amino acids, proteins, nucleic acids; promotes vegetative growth. |
| **Phosphorus (P)** | H₂PO₄⁻, HPO₄²⁻ | Energy transfer (ATP), root development, flowering, seed formation. |
| **Potassium (K)** | K⁺ | Enzyme activation, stomatal regulation, sugar translocation, disease resistance. |
| **Calcium (Ca)** | Ca²⁺ | Cell wall stability, membrane integrity, prevents blossom-end rot in tomato. |
| **Magnesium (Mg)** | Mg²⁺ | Central atom of chlorophyll; activates enzymes. |
| **Sulfur (S)** | SO₄²⁻ | Component of amino acids (cysteine, methionine), vitamins. |

**Liebig's Law of the Minimum:** A plant's growth is limited by the nutrient in shortest supply — even if all other nutrients are abundant, deficiency of one will limit yield.

**Deficiency Diagnosis Principle:**
- *Mobile nutrients (N, P, K, Mg)* → deficiency appears first in **older leaves** (the plant translocates them to growing parts).
- *Immobile nutrients (Ca, Fe, B)* → deficiency appears first in **younger leaves**.

---

### ✅ Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks

#### (a) Define root substrate. Explain in detail the different types of root substrates used in greenhouses with their merits and demerits. **[7 Marks]** **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** A *root substrate* is a soilless growing medium used in protected cultivation to provide physical support, water, air, and nutrients to plant roots under controlled conditions.

**Classification of Substrates:**

**(A) Organic Substrates:**

1. **Peat Moss (Sphagnum Peat)**
   - *Merits:* Very high WHC (70 %), high CEC (100 cmol/kg), sterile, lightweight.
   - *Demerits:* Acidic (pH 3.5 – 4.5, needs liming), non-renewable, decomposes over time.

2. **Coco Coir (Coconut fiber)**
   - *Merits:* pH 5.5 – 6.5 (ideal), high WHC, renewable, low cost.
   - *Demerits:* May contain high Na and Cl (needs buffering), low CEC compared to peat.

3. **Pine Bark / Wood Bark**
   - *Merits:* Excellent for orchids, long-lasting, good drainage.
   - *Demerits:* Low WHC, may contain phenolic toxins if not composted.

4. **Rice Husk / Sawdust**
   - *Merits:* Cheap, locally available in Kerala.
   - *Demerits:* Decomposes rapidly, may tie up nitrogen.

**(B) Inorganic Substrates:**

5. **Perlite (Volcanic glass, heated)**
   - *Merits:* Sterile, lightweight, excellent aeration (40 % air porosity).
   - *Demerits:* Zero CEC, no nutrients, dust hazard.

6. **Vermiculite (Hydrated Mg-Al-Iron silicate)**
   - *Merits:* High WHC (60 %), good CEC, lightweight.
   - *Demerits:* Compresses over time, can release K and Mg.

7. **Rockwool (Spun basalt)**
   - *Merits:* Sterile, very high WHC (80 %), uniform structure.
   - *Demerits:* Non-biodegradable, zero CEC, energy-intensive to manufacture, disposal issue.

8. **Expanded Clay Pellets (LECA)**
   - *Merits:* Reusable, pH-neutral, excellent drainage.
   - *Demerits:* Heavy, expensive, low WHC.

9. **Sand**
   - *Merits:* Cheap, available, good drainage.
   - *Demerits:* Heavy, low WHC, low CEC.

10. **Polyurethane Foam**
    - *Merits:* Synthetic, very high WHC, used in vertical farming.
    - *Demerits:* Non-biodegradable, costly.

**Ideal Mix Example:** *Peat : Perlite : Vermiculite (2 : 1 : 1)* for nursery propagation; *Coco Coir : Perlite (3 : 1)* for tomato slabs.

---

#### (b) What is pasteurization? Explain steam pasteurization of root substrate in detail. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** *Pasteurization* of substrate is the process of selectively eliminating harmful pathogens, nematodes, insect eggs, and weed seeds by heat or chemical treatment, while retaining beneficial microflora.

**Need for Pasteurization:**
- Recirculating irrigation and intensive monoculture allow buildup of *Pythium*, *Rhizoctonia*, *Fusarium*, *Phytophthora*.
- These pathogens cause damping-off, root rot, and wilt.
- Weeds and insect larvae also need to be eliminated.

**Steam Pasteurization — Process Details:**

**Step 1 — Pre-moistening**
The substrate is moistened to **60 – 70 % of WHC** to ensure efficient heat transfer (dry substrate is a poor conductor).

**Step 2 — Loading into Pasteurization Chamber**
Substrate is placed in:
- A stationary bin with steam injection pipes at the base, OR
- A concrete pad covered with a heat-resistant tarpaulin, OR
- A specialized aerated-steam cart.

**Step 3 — Steam Injection**
Saturated steam at **~ 100 °C** is injected from the base. A temperature probe is placed at the **coldest point** (usually the top corner of the pile).

**Step 4 — Temperature Hold**
The substrate at the coldest point is brought to **70 °C and held for 30 minutes** (USDA standard).
- At 70 °C, most mesophilic pathogens and nematodes are killed.
- Beneficial *Trichoderma* and *Bacillus* survive (they are thermotolerant).
- Above **82 °C**, beneficial microbes are also destroyed.

**Step 5 — Cooling**
Steam is shut off and substrate is allowed to cool naturally to **~ 25 °C** over 12 – 24 hours.

**Step 6 — Re-inoculation (Optional but Recommended)**
Compost tea, *Trichoderma viride*, or mycorrhizal inoculants are added to re-establish beneficial microflora.

**Advantages of Steam Pasteurization:**
- Uniform heat penetration.
- No toxic chemical residues.
- Does not alter substrate pH significantly.
- Safe for workers when properly handled.

**Disadvantages:**
- High fuel/energy cost.
- Time-consuming (8 – 24 hours for a batch).
- Risk of overheating if poorly monitored.

**Alternative Methods (in brief):**
- **Hot water drench** (80 – 90 °C) — risk of waterlogging.
- **Solarization** (sun heat under polythene for 4 – 6 weeks) — low cost but climate-dependent.
- **Chemical fumigation** (Basamid / Dazomet) — modern replacement for banned methyl bromide.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. **Mixing up Pasteurization and Sterilization:** Pasteurization = 70 °C / 30 min (beneficial microbes survive). Sterilization = > 100 °C (all microbes die, including beneficial). This distinction is a **2-mark question** in KTU.
> 2. **Confusing CEC of peat vs. perlite:** Many students write perlite as having high CEC — it has **almost zero CEC**. Rockwool also has zero CEC. Peat and coco coir have high CEC.
> 3. **Forgetting the safety factor in pasteurization time:** The 30-minute time is at the *coldest point* of the substrate pile, not at the steam inlet. Many students miss this.
> 4. **Using soil EC and water EC interchangeably:** Soil EC is measured in saturation extract; water EC is direct. Values are different.
> 5. **Writing drip irrigation as overhead sprinklers:** Drip = at root zone; sprinkler = above canopy. KTU frequently tests this distinction.
> 6. **Confusing mobile vs. immobile nutrient deficiencies:** Older vs. younger leaf symptoms — many students reverse these.

---

## 5.3 📝 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### 🔑 Quick Revision Checklist

- **Greenhouse watering** is 100 % artificial — drip irrigation is the standard, with EC and pH as control parameters.
- **Fertigation** delivers fertilizer via irrigation water; key formula: $C_{ppm} = \dfrac{m_{fert}}{V_{sol}}$.
- **Root substrate** must have porosity 70 – 90 %, WHC 50 – 70 %, air porosity 15 – 25 %, stable pH 5.5 – 6.5.
- **Peat** — high CEC, acidic; **Perlite** — zero CEC, high air porosity; **Coco coir** — neutral pH, renewable; **Rockwool** — high WHC, zero CEC, non-biodegradable.
- **Pasteurization (not sterilization)** is the engineering target: **70 °C for 30 minutes** at the coldest point.
- **Above 82 °C** → beneficial microbes killed → re-inoculation needed.
- **Containers** must have drainage holes; **Benches** must slope 1 – 2 %; bench width ≤ 1.5 m for one-sided access.
- **Liebig's Law of the Minimum** governs plant nutrition: the most deficient nutrient limits growth.
- **17 essential elements** = 9 macronutrients + 8 micronutrients.
- **Mobile nutrients** (N, P, K, Mg) → deficiency in older leaves.
- **Immobile nutrients** (Ca, Fe, B, Cu, S) → deficiency in younger leaves.
- **Alternative cropping systems**: Hydroponics, Aeroponics, Aquaponics, Vertical Farming.
- **NFT** = open system, high risk on pump failure; **Rockwool recirculation** = closed system, water-efficient.
- **Aeroponics** uses mist only — highest oxygenation, very high water saving.
- **Aquaponics** integrates fish culture — fish waste → plant nutrient, plants → fish water purification (symbiosis).
- **Vertical farming** is space-efficient — uses LED lighting and stacked trays; capital-intensive but high yield per m².

### 🎯 KTU High-Yield Memory Hooks

| Term | Single-Line Hook |
|---|---|
| Pasteurization | **70 / 30** (70 °C, 30 min) |
| Substrate pH | **5.5 to 6.5** |
| EC range | **1.2 to 3.5 dS/m** |
| Total Porosity | **70 to 90 %** |
| Air Porosity | **15 to 25 %** |
| WHC | **50 to 70 %** |
| Bench slope | **1 to 2 %** |
| Bench height | **70 to 90 cm** |
| Air space N deficiency | **Older leaves yellow** |
| Air space Ca deficiency | **Tip burn, young leaves** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Exam Tip:** When asked to "compare" two things (e.g., hydroponics vs. aquaponics, peat vs. rockwool), always present a **table** with at least 5 parameters. Examiners award bonus marks for clean tabulation. Avoid long paragraphs in 14-mark questions — use bullet points and headings to maximize readability and marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Plant Tissue Culture, Chemical Growth Regulation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEAGT721 - DESIGN AND MAINTENANCE OF GREEN HOUSE
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Plant Tissue Culture, Chemical Growth Regulation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 3 — PLANT TISSUE CULTURE & CHEMICAL GROWTH REGULATION

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Plant Tissue Culture (PTC) — Formal KTU Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plant Tissue Culture (PTC)** is the *in vitro* (Latin: "within the glass") aseptic cultivation of plant cells, tissues, organs, or whole plants under controlled nutritional and environmental conditions on a chemically defined nutrient medium. The discipline is rooted in the **cell theory** of Schleiden (1838) and Schwann (1839) and the foundational concept of **totipotency** proposed by **Gottlieb Haberlandt** in 1902.

The technique exploits the **genetic totipotency** of plant cells — the inherent capacity of *every living plant cell* to dedifferentiate, divide, and regenerate into a complete, fertile plant, given the right chemical and physical cues.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Cellular LEGO®"

Imagine every plant cell as a fully equipped **LEGO® brick** containing the entire instruction manual (the genome) of a 10-story LEGO castle. In nature, most bricks are already locked into a specific wall, beam, or window, so they only "play" their assigned role. In tissue culture, we gently **unlock** these bricks using special chemical "keys" (plant growth regulators), strip the brick back to a blank baseplate (callus), and then ask it to rebuild the *entire castle* from scratch — roots, stems, leaves, and flowers — all inside a sterile glass jar (the culture vessel). The greenhouse, in this analogy, is the **"nursery school"** where the rebuilt plants are hardened before being planted in the field.

### 1.3 Chemical Growth Regulation — The "Master Switchboard"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Chemical Growth Regulation** in horticulture refers to the deliberate exogenous application of **Plant Growth Regulators (PGRs)** — also called **plant growth substances** or **bioregulators** — to manipulate physiological processes such as cell division, elongation, differentiation, flowering, fruiting, senescence, and stress response inside the controlled environment of a greenhouse.

PGRs are classified into two broad chemical families:

| Family | Origin | Key Members |
|---|---|---|
| **Native (Endogenous) Hormones** | Synthesized by the plant itself | Auxins, Cytokinins, Gibberellins, Abscisic Acid, Ethylene |
| **Synthetic Regulators** | Lab-synthesized analogs | 2,4-D, NAA, IBA, BAP, Kinetin, MH, CCC |

### 1.4 Why This Topic Matters in Greenhouse Engineering

For a **KTU greenhouse design student**, PTC and PGR chemistry intersect with the built environment in three crucial ways:
1. **Propagation Labs** — Greenhouses house **mist chambers**, **acclimatization tunnels**, and **culture rooms** with strict **temperature (25 ± 2 °C)**, **humidity (60–80 % RH)**, and **photoperiod (16/8 h light/dark)** control.
2. **Micropropagation Scale-up** — Commercial nurseries produce **1,000–100,000+** disease-free plantlets per batch; understanding media chemistry drives **operational cost (OPEX)** decisions.
3. **Precision Dosing** — PGR concentrations are typically in **mg L⁻¹** or **µM**; a greenhouse fertigation system must deliver these with **±2 % dosing accuracy** — a direct civil/MEP design constraint.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Foundational Principles of PTC

1. **Totipotency** — every somatic cell carries the full genetic blueprint.
2. **Dedifferentiation** — mature, specialized cells can revert to a meristematic, undifferentiated state (forming **callus**).
3. **Redifferentiation** — under the right hormonal cues, the callus re-organizes into specialized organs (roots = *rhizogenesis*; shoots = *caulogenesis*).

## 2.2 Stages of Micropropagation (KTU-Favorite Diagram Topic)

| Stage | Common Name | Goal | Typical Duration | Medium State |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Stage 0** | **Pre-propagation** | Stock plant selection & maintenance | Weeks–months | Greenhouse-grown mother plant |
| **Stage I** | **Inoculation / Initiation** | Surface sterilization & explant establishment | 1–4 weeks | Solid MS + antioxidant |
| **Stage II** | **Multiplication** | Axillary shoot proliferation | 4–8 weeks (cycled) | Solid MS + BAP (0.5–2.0 mg L⁻¹) |
| **Stage III** | **Rooting** | *In vitro* rhizogenesis | 2–6 weeks | ½ MS + IAA / NAA (0.1–1.0 mg L⁻¹) |
| **Stage IV** | **Hardening / Acclimatization** | Transfer to ex-vitro conditions | 4–8 weeks | Sterile soil-less mix, high RH |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** Examiners often frame Stage IV (hardening) as a *greenhouse design* problem — students must justify why **foggers, shade nets (50–75 %)**, and **polytunnels** are critical during acclimatization. The "**transplant shock**" is caused by abrupt changes in **heterotrophic → autotrophic** nutrition and the absence of the *in vitro* high-humidity environment.

## 2.3 Murashige & Skoog (MS) Medium — The Industry Standard

Formulated in **1962** by Toshio Murashige and Folke Skoog, MS medium is the **most cited** plant tissue culture formulation worldwide. Key macronutrient levels:

| Nutrient | Chemical Form | Concentration (mg L⁻¹) | Role |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Nitrogen (N)** | NH₄NO₃ + KNO₃ | **1,650 + 1,900** | Amino acid/protein synthesis |
| **Potassium (K)** | KNO₃ | **1,900** | Enzyme activation, osmoticum |
| **Phosphorus (P)** | KH₂PO₄ | **170** | Energy transfer (ATP) |
| **Calcium (Ca)** | CaCl₂·2H₂O | **440** | Cell wall stability |
| **Magnesium (Mg)** | MgSO₄·7H₂O | **370** | Chlorophyll core ion |
| **Iron (Fe)** | Fe-EDTA | **36.7** | Electron transport |
| **Sucrose** | C₁₂H₂₂O₁₁ | **30,000** | Carbon source + osmoticum |

> [!TIP]
> The **sucrose** concentration of **3 % (w/v)** is *not* accidental. Plant cells in culture cannot photosynthesize efficiently due to low light. Sucrose provides both **energy** and an **osmotic potential (Ψs ≈ -0.15 MPa)** that mimics the apoplastic environment.

## 2.4 The Five Classes of Plant Growth Regulators (PGRs) — Master Cheat Sheet

| Class | Discovery | Major Roles | Greenhouse Applications | Common Forms |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Auxins** | Charles Darwin (1880s); isolated by F.W. Went (1928) | Cell elongation, apical dominance, rooting, tropism | Rooting hormone for cuttings; fruit set | **IAA, IBA, NAA, 2,4-D** |
| **Cytokinins** | Folke Skoog & Carlos Miller (1955) | Cell division, shoot initiation, delay senescence | Shoot multiplication in tissue culture | **Kinetin, BAP, Zeatin, TDZ** |
| **Gibberellins** | Eiichi Kurosawa (1926); Yabuta (1938) | Stem elongation, seed germination, fruit size | Breaking dormancy; flower induction | **GA₃, GA₄+₇** |
| **Abscisic Acid (ABA)** | Addicott et al. (1963) | Stomatal closure, dormancy, stress response | Anti-transpirant sprays | **S-ABA** |
| **Ethylene** | Gane (1934) | Fruit ripening, abscission, senescence | Banana ripening chambers | **Ethephon (2-CEPA)** |

### 2.5 The Auxin : Cytokinin Ratio — The "Master Switch"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Skook & Miller's Law (1957):** The morphogenic fate of callus is dictated by the **Auxin : Cytokinin ratio** in the medium.

| Auxin : Cytokinin Ratio | Resulting Morphogenesis |
|---|---|
| **High Auxin / Low Cytokinin** | **Root formation (Rhizogenesis)** |
| **Low Auxin / High Cytokinin** | **Shoot formation (Caulogenesis)** |
| **Balanced (≈ 1 : 1)** | **Callus proliferation (undifferentiated mass)** |

This single rule is responsible for **more than 30 %** of KTU-style viva questions on tissue culture.

### 2.6 The Five Pillars of Sterilization in PTC

1. **Autoclave** — media at **121 °C, 15 psi, 15–20 min**.
2. **Surface sterilization** of explant — **0.1 % HgCl₂ (1–3 min)** or **70 % ethanol (30 s)**.
3. **Laminar Air Flow (LAF) cabinet** — **Class II, Type A2**, HEPA-filtered at **0.3 µm**.
4. **Aseptic transfer tools** — flame-sterilized forceps/scalpels.
5. **Culture room hygiene** — **70 % ethanol** wipe-down, restricted access.

> [!NOTE]
> **Hormesis Principle:** Heat-labile PGRs (e.g., IAA, GA₃, ABA) are **thermolabile** and **must NOT be autoclaved**. They are added to autoclaved-cooled (50–55 °C) media via **0.22 µm membrane filtration** under LAF.

## 2.7 Engineering Real-World Utility

| Engineering Domain | How PTC/PGR Connects |
|---|---|
| **Greenhouse HVAC design** | Stage IV hardening requires **>85 % RH** → drives fogger capacity (g h⁻¹ m⁻²) |
| **Irrigation/fertigation** | Drip systems must inject PGR stock at **0.5–2.0 mL L⁻¹** with anti-precipitation mixing |
| **Lighting engineering** | Culture rooms use **cool-white fluorescent @ 40–60 µmol m⁻² s⁻¹ PPFD**, 16 h photoperiod |
| **Process economics** | Single plantlet cost in India: **₹ 8–25**; commercial break-even ≥ **5,000 plantlets per batch** |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Protocols & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Step-by-Step Protocol — Surface Sterilization of an Explant

This is a **classic 7-mark KTU question**. The examiner expects you to list the **seven sequential steps** with **timing, concentration, and rationale**.

| Step | Action | Typical Specification | Purpose / Why |
|---|---|---|---|
| **1** | **Selection of mother plant** | Healthy, disease-free, juvenile | Reduces endogenous microbial load |
| **2** | **Pre-treatment** | Tap water rinse 30 min → mild Teepol®/soap | Removes soil & dust |
| **3** | **First alcohol dip** | **70 % ethanol, 30 s** | Surface wetting, kills most vegetative microbes |
| **4** | **Rinse with sterile water** | 3 × changes, 1 min each | Removes residual alcohol |
| **5** | **Mercuric chloride treatment** | **0.1 % HgCl₂, 1–3 min** (alternatives: 1 % NaOCl, 10–15 min) | Kills fungal spores & bacteria |
| **6** | **Final sterile water rinse** | 4–5 × changes, 1 min each | Removes toxic Hg²⁺/ClO⁻ residues |
| **7** | **Inoculation on MS medium** | Within LAF, ≤ 30 min exposure | Establishes aseptic culture |

## 3.2 Analytical Calculation — MS Medium Stock Solution Preparation

### 3.2.1 Problem Statement

> *"Prepare 1 litre of full-strength MS medium. The macronutrient stock is concentrated **20×** (i.e., 50 mL stock gives 1 L working medium). How much stock is needed to prepare 2.5 L of working medium? If sucrose must be **30 g L⁻¹** and agar **8 g L⁻¹**, calculate the total quantities required."*

### 3.2.2 Step-by-Step Worked Solution

**Step 1: Stock volume for macronutrients**

$$
V_{\text{stock}} = C_{\text{stock}} \times V_{\text{final}} \div C_{\text{working}}
$$

Substitute values:

$$
V_{\text{stock}} = \frac{50 \text{ mL}}{1 \text{ L}} \times 2.5 \text{ L}
$$

$$
V_{\text{stock}} = 125 \text{ mL}
$$

**Step 2: Sucrose mass for 2.5 L**

$$
M_{\text{sucrose}} = 30 \text{ g L}^{-1} \times 2.5 \text{ L}
$$

$$
M_{\text{sucrose}} = 75 \text{ g}
$$

**Step 3: Agar mass for 2.5 L**

$$
M_{\text{agar}} = 8 \text{ g L}^{-1} \times 2.5 \text{ L}
$$

$$
M_{\text{agar}} = 20 \text{ g}
$$

**Step 4: Final volume of distilled water**

$$
V_{\text{water}} = 2500 \text{ mL} - 125 \text{ mL} = 2375 \text{ mL}
$$

> [!TIP]
> The KTU examiner often asks: *"Why is the agar added AFTER pH adjustment but BEFORE autoclaving?"*
> **Answer:** Agar melts at ≈ **100 °C** and solidifies at ≈ **45 °C**. Adding it before autoclaving ensures **sterile solidification** in the culture vessel. The pH must be adjusted to **5.7 ± 0.1** using **0.1 N NaOH or 0.1 N HCl** *before* autoclaving because **high-temperature hydrolysis** would otherwise drop pH by **0.3–0.5 units**.

## 3.3 Analytical Calculation — PGR Dilution from Stock

### 3.3.1 Problem Statement

> *"You have a **100 mg mL⁻¹ BAP stock solution in 1 N NaOH**. You need to prepare **1 litre of MS medium containing 1.5 mg L⁻¹ BAP**. How much stock do you add?"*

### 3.3.2 Worked Solution

**Step 1:** Apply the dilution equation $C_1 V_1 = C_2 V_2$

$$
V_1 = \frac{C_2 V_2}{C_1}
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute

$$
V_1 = \frac{1.5 \text{ mg L}^{-1} \times 1 \text{ L}}{100{,}000 \text{ mg L}^{-1}}
$$

$$
V_1 = 1.5 \times 10^{-5} \text{ L}
$$

**Step 3:** Convert to microlitres

$$
V_1 = 1.5 \times 10^{-5} \text{ L} \times 10^6 \, \mu\text{L/L} = 15 \, \mu\text{L}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Since this is a **microlitre**-level addition, the working stock should be **intermediately diluted** to **1 mg mL⁻¹** (i.e., 100× dilution), so you add **1.5 mL of the diluted stock** per litre of medium. This is the **standard operating procedure** in any commercial PTC lab.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Callus Induction vs. Shoot Multiplication

| Parameter | Callus Induction Medium | Shoot Multiplication Medium |
|---|---|---|
| **MS strength** | Full MS | Full MS |
| **Auxin (2,4-D)** | **1.0 – 3.0 mg L⁻¹** | 0.0 – 0.1 mg L⁻¹ |
| **Cytokinin (BAP)** | 0.0 – 0.5 mg L⁻¹ | **1.0 – 3.0 mg L⁻¹** |
| **Auxin : CK ratio** | High | Low |
| **Expected response** | Friable yellow/white callus | Multiple shoot buds |
| **Sub-culture interval** | 21 – 28 days | 21 – 30 days |
| **Typical tissue** | Leaf, petiole, hypocotyl | Pre-existing meristem / callus-derived |

## 3.5 Common Pathogen-Contamination Diagnostics

| Observation in Culture | Probable Cause | Rectification |
|---|---|---|
| **Bacterial ooze (cloudy halo)** | Endophytic bacteria; insufficient HgCl₂ exposure | Re-sterilize with 0.2 % HgCl₂ for 2 min |
| **Fungal mycelium (white/cottony)** | Spore contamination from laminar bench | Discard culture; fumigate LAF with formalin |
| **Browning of explant** | Phenol oxidation → medium browning | Add **PVP (Polyvinylpyrrolidone, 0.5 %)** or **citric acid (150 mg L⁻¹)** |
| **Hyperhydricity (vitrification)** | High cytokinin, low agar, high humidity | Reduce BAP, increase agar to 10 g L⁻¹, vent vessel |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Generalized PTC Workflow

> **Diagram Note:** This block-level functional flow maps the **5-stage micropropagation pipeline** with explicit transitions and decision nodes, which is the most frequently tested KTU concept.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Stage 0: Mother Plant Selection in Greenhouse] --> B[Stage 1: Explant Excision 1 to 2 cm Segment]
    B --> C[Surface Sterilization 70 percent EtOH then 0.1 percent HgCl2]
    C --> D{Inoculation under LAF Cabinet}
    D --> E[Stage 2: Callus Induction on MS plus 2,4-D 2 mg per L]
    E --> F{Callus Quality Check}
    F -->|Friable healthy| G[Stage 3A: Shoot Multiplication on MS plus BAP 2 mg per L]
    F -->|Necrotic or browning| X[Discard and Retry]
    G --> H{Cytokinin Auxin Ratio}
    H -->|High CK| I[Multiple Shoot Buds]
    H -->|High Auxin| J[Stage 3B: Rooting on half MS plus IAA 0.5 mg per L]
    I --> J
    J --> K[Stage 4: Hardening in Mist Chamber RH 90 percent]
    K --> L[Stage 5: Field Transfer to Greenhouse or Open Field]
    L --> M[Commercial Plantlet Ready for Market]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Auxin : Cytokinin Morphogenetic Switch

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Callus / Expl] --> Q{Add Auxin plus Cytokinin}
    Q -->|Auxin High and CK Low| R1[Root Formation]
    Q -->|Auxin Low and CK High| R2[Shoot Formation]
    Q -->|Balanced Ratio| R3[Callus Maintenance]
    Q -->|Both Low| R4[No Response or Senescence]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Functional Architecture of a Greenhouse-Integrated PTC Lab

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph zoneA[ZONE A: PRE CULTURE AREA]
    A1[Greenhouse Mother Plants] --> A2[Shade Net 50 percent]
    A2 --> A3[Tagging and Pruning Bay]
    end

    subgraph zoneB[ZONE B: INOCULATION ROOM]
    B1[Wash Station] --> B2[Sterilization Cabinet]
    B2 --> B3[Laminar Air Flow Bench]
    B3 --> B4[Culture Tubes Flasks Jars]
    end

    subgraph zoneC[ZONE C: GROWTH ROOM]
    C1[Temperature 25 plus minus 2 C] --> C2[Photoperiod 16 h Light 8 h Dark]
    C2 --> C3[Cultural Racks with Fluorescent Lamps]
    C3 --> C4[Multiplication and Rooting Stage]
    end

    subgraph zoneD[ZONE D: HARDENING ZONE]
    D1[Mist Chamber RH 90 percent] --> D2[Shade Tunnel]
    D2 --> D3[Secondary Hardening 50 percent Shade]
    D3 --> D4[Field Ready Plantlets]
    end

    A3 --> B1
    B4 --> C1
    C4 --> D1
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Site of Synthesis & Action of Major PGRs

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Apical Meristem and Young Leaves] -->|Synthesize| B1[Auxin IAA]
    B1 --> B1a[Cell Elongation]
    B1 --> B1b[Apical Dominance]
    B1 --> B1c[Root Initiation]

    A2[Root Tips and Developing Seeds] -->|Synthesize| B2[Cytokinin Zeatin]
    B2 --> B2a[Cell Division]
    B2 --> B2b[Shoot Initiation]
    B2 --> B2c[Delay Senescence]

    A3[Young Leaves and Immature Seeds] -->|Synthesize| B3[Gibberellin GA3]
    B3 --> B3a[Stem Elongation]
    B3 --> B3b[Seed Germination]
    B3 --> B3c[Fruit Enlargement]

    A4[Roots and Mature Leaves under Stress] -->|Synthesize| B4[Abscisic Acid ABA]
    B4 --> B4a[Stomatal Closure]
    B4 --> B4b[Seed Dormancy]
    B4 --> B4c[Stress Tolerance]

    A5[All Tissues as Gas] -->|Synthesize| B5[Ethylene C2H4]
    B5 --> B5a[Fruit Ripening]
    B5 --> B5b[Leaf Abscission]
    B5 --> B5c[Senescence]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]**

**Q1. Define totipotency. Mention its significance in plant tissue culture. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**
Totipotency is the inherent potential of a living plant cell to express its entire genetic information and develop into a complete, fertile plant. The concept was proposed by **Gottlieb Haberlandt (1902)**. Its significance in PTC lies in enabling **clonal propagation**, **genetic transformation**, **germplasm conservation**, and **production of disease-free planting material** in commercial greenhouse nurseries.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**

**Q2. List any three auxins and three cytokinins commonly used in plant tissue culture media. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**
**Auxins** — IAA (Indole-3-Acetic Acid), IBA (Indole-3-Butyric Acid), NAA (Naphthalene Acetic Acid), 2,4-D (2,4-Dichlorophenoxyacetic Acid).
**Cytokinins** — Kinetin, BAP (6-Benzylaminopurine), Zeatin, TDZ (Thidiazuron).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse "MS medium" with a complete solution.** Examiners deduct 1 mark if you forget to mention the **pH adjustment to 5.7 ± 0.1** and the **autoclaving protocol (121 °C / 15 psi / 15 min)**.
> 2. **Always write the units** — *mg L⁻¹*, *µM*, or *ppm* — for any PGR concentration. Numerical answers without units lose **0.5 – 1 mark**.
> 3. **Stages of micropropagation** must be remembered in order: *0 → I → II → III → IV*. Reversing Stage II and III is a common **2-mark deduction**.
> 4. **Skoog & Miller's ratio** is mandatory to cite for any morphogenesis question. Not citing it is treated as **incomplete** by KTU examiners.

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** — *Internal Choice 1*

> **[KTU University Exam – July 2024 Model Question]**

**(a) Explain the general procedure of plant tissue culture. Add a note on the composition of MS medium. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The general procedure of plant tissue culture involves five sequential stages:

1. **Selection of explant and mother plant** — Healthy, juvenile, disease-indexed stock is selected from the greenhouse (Stage 0).
2. **Surface sterilization** — Sequential wash in tap water, **70 % ethanol (30 s)**, **0.1 % HgCl₂ (1–3 min)**, followed by **4–5 sterile water rinses**.
3. **Inoculation** — Aseptically transferred to culture medium inside a **Laminar Air Flow (LAF)** cabinet.
4. **Incubation** — Placed in a **culture room at 25 ± 2 °C, 16 h photoperiod, 40–60 µmol m⁻² s⁻¹ PPFD**.
5. **Sub-culturing, rooting, and hardening** — Periodic sub-cultures (every 3–4 weeks), rhizogenesis on **½ MS + IAA 0.5 mg L⁻¹**, and finally acclimatization in a **mist chamber with 90 % RH**.

**Murashige & Skoog (MS) Medium Composition (1962):**
- **Macronutrients** (mg L⁻¹) — NH₄NO₃ 1,650; KNO₃ 1,900; KH₂PO₄ 170; CaCl₂·2H₂O 440; MgSO₄·7H₂O 370.
- **Micronutrients** (mg L⁻¹) — H₃BO₃ 6.2; MnSO₄·4H₂O 22.3; ZnSO₄·7H₂O 8.6; KI 0.83; Na₂MoO₄·2H₂O 0.25; CuSO₄·5H₂O 0.025; CoCl₂·6H₂O 0.025.
- **Iron source** — FeSO₄·7H₂O 27.8 + Na₂EDTA 37.3 (chelated form).
- **Organic supplements** — Thiamine HCl 0.5, Pyridoxine HCl 0.5, Nicotinic acid 0.5, Glycine 2.0, Myo-inositol 100, **Sucrose 30,000 mg L⁻¹**.
- **Solidifying agent** — **Agar 8,000 mg L⁻¹** (or Gelrite 2,000 mg L⁻¹ for clear media).
- **pH adjusted to 5.7 ± 0.1** and **autoclaved at 121 °C, 15 psi, 15 min**.

**(b) Describe the role of plant growth regulators in tissue culture. Explain the Skoog and Miller hypothesis. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

Plant Growth Regulators (PGRs) are **exogenous chemical messengers** added to culture media at **0.01 – 10 mg L⁻¹** to direct morphogenesis. They replace the natural hormonal signals of the intact plant, allowing the *in vitro* system to mimic developmental programs:

- **Auxins** (IAA, IBA, NAA, 2,4-D) drive cell elongation, callus formation, and rhizogenesis.
- **Cytokinins** (BAP, Kinetin, Zeatin) drive cell division, shoot bud initiation, and counteract apical dominance.
- **Gibberellins** (GA₃) are used sparingly to elongate shoot internodes.
- **ABA** is used in somatic embryogenesis to induce desiccation tolerance.
- **Ethylene** is generally avoided (gas accumulates in sealed vessels, causing "**leaf yellowing**").

**Skoog & Miller Hypothesis (1957):**
Folke Skoog and Carlos Miller at the University of Wisconsin demonstrated that the **morphogenetic fate** of tobacco pith callus is governed by the **relative ratio of auxin to cytokinin** in the medium:

- **High auxin : low cytokinin → root formation (rhizogenesis).**
- **Low auxin : high cytokinin → shoot formation (caulogenesis).**
- **Balanced ratio → undifferentiated callus proliferation.**

This was the **first quantitative morphogenetic rule** in plant biology and remains the cornerstone of modern commercial micropropagation protocols.

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** — *Internal Choice 2*

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**

**(a) Explain the different types of plant tissue culture techniques. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

PTC is broadly classified into **seven major types**, based on the explant and the target product:

| Type | Explant | Outcome | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Callus culture** | Parenchyma, leaf | Undifferentiated callus | Secondary metabolite production, transgenic studies |
| **Cell suspension culture** | Friable callus | Single cells / small aggregates in liquid | Large-scale bioactive compound production (e.g., shikonin) |
| **Organ culture** | Anther, ovule, root tip | Whole organ development | *In vitro* flowering, embryo rescue |
| **Meristem culture** | 0.1 – 0.5 mm apical dome | Virus-free plants | Production of pathogen-indexed planting material (e.g., banana, potato) |
| **Anther / Pollen culture** | Anther or microspore | Haploid plantlets | Production of homozygous lines in breeding |
| **Protoplast culture** | Cell-wall-less cells | Somatic hybrids via PEG/electro-fusion | Hybrid creation across sexual barriers (e.g., pomato) |
| **Somatic embryogenesis** | Embryogenic callus | Bipolar embryos | Artificial seeds, mass propagation |
| **Virus elimination via thermotherapy + meristem tip culture** | 0.2 mm meristem | Virus-free germplasm | Commercial banana, sugarcane, potato nurseries |

**(b) Discuss the role of auxins and cytokinins in green house cultivation. List four synthetic auxins and four synthetic cytokinins. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Role in Greenhouse Cultivation:**

**Auxins:**
- **Rooting of cuttings** — IBA / NAA at 1,000 – 3,000 ppm (quick-dip) or 50 – 200 ppm (prolonged soak).
- **Fruit set** — 2,4-D at 1 – 2 ppm in tomato, brinjal to prevent flower drop.
- **Apical dominance management** — Topical NAA sprays in rose and chrysanthemum promote lateral branching.
- **Weed control** — 2,4-D in lawn maintenance (negative role, often restricted).

**Cytokinins:**
- **Branching and bushy growth** — BAP at 100 – 500 ppm foliar spray in roses, lilies, and orchids.
- **Delay of senescence** — Kinetin sprays extend shelf-life of cut flowers by 2 – 4 days.
- **Breaking bud dormancy** — Thidiazuron (TDZ) at 5 – 50 ppm to force bud break in grape and kiwi.
- **Enhancement of female flower ratio** — In cucumber and zucchini, BAP increases pistillate flowers, boosting yield.

**Synthetic Auxins:** **NAA, IBA, 2,4-D, MCPB, 2,4,5-T** (restricted in many countries).

**Synthetic Cytokinins:** **Kinetin, BAP (Benzylaminopurine), TDZ (Thidiazuron), CPPU (Forchlorofenuron).**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024**

- [ ] **Totipotency** = Haberlandt (1902); **Cell theory** = Schleiden & Schwann (1838).
- [ ] **MS medium** = Murashige & Skoog, **1962**; key N source = **NH₄NO₃ 1,650 + KNO₃ 1,900 mg L⁻¹**; **sucrose = 3 %**; **pH = 5.7 ± 0.1**; **autoclave = 121 °C, 15 psi, 15 min**.
- [ ] **Skoog & Miller (1957) ratio rule** → High auxin = roots, High cytokinin = shoots, Balanced = callus.
- [ ] **Five stages of micropropagation** → 0 → I (initiation) → II (multiplication) → III (rooting) → IV (hardening). **Stage 0 = greenhouse mother plant**; **Stage IV = mist chamber acclimatization** — both are direct greenhouse design concerns.
- [ ] **Sterilization triad** → **0.1 % HgCl₂ (1–3 min) + 70 % EtOH (30 s) + LAF Class II Type A2 HEPA filtration at 0.3 µm**.
- [ ] **Heat-labile PGRs** (IAA, GA₃, ABA) → **filter-sterilize via 0.22 µm**, do **not** autoclave.
- [ ] **Culture room conditions** → **25 ± 2 °C, 16/8 h photoperiod, 40–60 µmol m⁻² s⁻¹ PPFD, RH 60–80 %**.
- [ ] **Hardening chamber** → **RH 85–95 %, shade net 50–75 %, gradual reduction over 4–6 weeks**.
- [ ] **Five PGR classes** → **Auxin, Cytokinin, Gibberellin, ABA, Ethylene**. Synthetic analogs (2,4-D, BAP, IBA, Kinetin, TDZ) are commercially favored.
- [ ] **MS dilution formula:** $V_{\text{stock}} = (C_{\text{working}}/C_{\text{stock}}) \times V_{\text{final}}$ — commit to memory.
- [ ] **Ethylene** is the **only gaseous** plant hormone → **C₂H₄**; applied commercially as **Ethephon (2-chloroethyl phosphonic acid)**.
- [ ] **Hyperhydricity** is a tell-tale sign of **excess BAP + low agar + high humidity**; reduce BAP, vent culture vessel.
- [ ] **PVP (0.5 %)** is added to media to prevent **phenolic browning** of explants rich in polyphenols (e.g., mango, grapevine, oil palm).
- [ ] **Thermotherapy + meristem tip culture** = gold standard for **virus elimination** in commercial greenhouse nurseries.

> **End of Module 3 Notes — OEAGT721 (KTU 2024 Scheme)**
