# MODULE 3: DISEASE CONTROL IN GREENHOUSES

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)**
> **Greenhouse Disease Control** refers to the integrated set of preventive, curative, and suppressive agronomic, biological, chemical, and environmental management practices employed within a protected cultivation system to minimize the incidence, severity, and spread of pathogens (fungi, bacteria, viruses, nematodes, phytoplasmas) on greenhouse-grown crops, thereby ensuring yield stability, produce quality, and economic viability of the protected structure.

### 1.1 Conceptual Analogy — The "Greenhouse as a Hospital ICU" Intuition

Imagine a **greenhouse** as a **hospital Intensive Care Unit (ICU)** for plants. Just as an ICU must be sterile, climate-controlled, and constantly monitored to prevent hospital-acquired infections (nosocomial infections), a greenhouse must be:

- **Sealed & Clean** (sanitation protocols),
- **Climate-Regulated** (temperature, humidity, ventilation),
- **Monitored Continuously** (scouting for early symptoms),
- **Treated Strategically** (fungicides, biocontrol agents).

If any one of these pillars fails — say, humidity rises to **> 90%** — it is like leaving a surgical wound exposed to bacteria. Disease outbreaks become inevitable.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight (KTU High-Yield Concept)**
> In open-field agriculture, the environment is largely uncontrollable. In a greenhouse, the **environment is 100% manageable**, which means most plant diseases are *engineer-preventable* through environmental manipulation rather than reactive chemical spraying. This is the foundational philosophy of greenhouse plant pathology.

### 1.2 The Disease Triangle — Foundation Concept

A plant disease can only manifest when **three components** intersect simultaneously:

| Component | Description in Greenhouse Context |
|-----------|-----------------------------------|
| **Host** | A susceptible crop variety (e.g., susceptible tomato cultivar) |
| **Pathogen** | Virulent fungal/bacterial/viral agent (e.g., *Botrytis cinerea*) |
| **Favourable Environment** | Optimal temperature, high relative humidity, free moisture on leaves |

> [!TIP]
> **Control Strategy Implication:** In a greenhouse, we can break the triangle by attacking **any one of the three vertices** — but the easiest and most sustainable is to manipulate the **environment** (humidity, temperature, air circulation).

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Thresholds in Greenhouse Disease Control

- **Relative Humidity Threshold for Disease Suppression:** **< 85%** (for most foliar fungal pathogens)
- **Optimal Leaf Wetness Duration to Avoid:** **< 4 hours** continuous
- **Air Circulation Velocity:** **0.3 to 0.5 m/s** inside canopy
- **Spore Germination Temperature (most fungi):** **15 °C – 25 °C**
- **Botrytis cinerea optimal growth:** **18 °C – 22 °C** at **RH > 93%**

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Greenhouse Diseases

Greenhouse diseases are classified based on the **causal organism**:

### A. Fungal Diseases (Most Prevalent in Greenhouses)

1. **Damping-off** (*Pythium spp., Rhizoctonia solani, Fusarium spp.*)
   - Affects: Seedlings at germination / early growth stage
   - Symptoms: Water-soaked stem at soil line, seedling collapse
   - Favours: High soil moisture, low light, cold media

2. **Grey Mould / Botrytis Blight** (*Botrytis cinerea*)
   - Affects: Tomato, cucumber, lettuce, ornamentals, strawberry
   - Symptoms: Grey fuzzy sporulation on senescent tissues
   - Favours: **RH > 93%**, free moisture, wounds

3. **Powdery Mildew** (*Sphaerotheca spp., Erysiphe spp., Oidium spp.*)
   - Affects: Cucurbits, tomato, pepper, rose
   - Symptoms: White powdery growth on upper leaf surface
   - Unique feature: Thrives even at **low humidity (50–70%)**

4. **Downy Mildew** (*Pseudoperonospora cubensis, Peronospora spp.*)
   - Affects: Cucumber, rose, lettuce
   - Symptoms: Angular yellow lesions on upper surface, purplish sporulation below
   - Favours: Cool temperatures (15–20 °C), leaf wetness

5. **Fusarium Wilt & Crown Rot** (*Fusarium oxysporum*)
   - Soil-borne, vascular pathogen
   - Symptoms: One-sided yellowing, wilting, vascular browning

6. **Verticillium Wilt** (*Verticillium dahliae*)
   - Similar to Fusarium; affects tomato, eggplant, ornamentals

7. **Sclerotinia / White Mould** (*Sclerotinia sclerotiorum*)
   - Cottony white mycelium, hard black sclerotia
   - Affects: Lettuce, tomato, beans, cucumber

8. **Anthracnose** (*Colletotrichum spp.*)
   - Affects: Cucumber, tomato, pepper, mango (in greenhouse nurseries)

### B. Bacterial Diseases

1. **Bacterial Wilt** (*Ralstonia solanacearum*)
2. **Bacterial Spot** (*Xanthomonas spp.*)
3. **Bacterial Soft Rot** (*Erwinia / Pectobacterium spp.*)
4. **Bacterial Canker** (*Clavibacter michiganensis* on tomato)

### C. Viral Diseases (Most Destructive; No Cure)

1. **Tomato Mosaic Virus (ToMV)**
2. **Cucumber Mosaic Virus (CMV)**
3. **Tomato Yellow Leaf Curl Virus (TYLCV)** — transmitted by whiteflies
4. **Tomato Spotted Wilt Virus (TSWV)** — transmitted by thrips
5. **Tobacco Mosaic Virus (TMV)**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Syllabus Highlight:** Viral diseases cannot be cured by chemicals. Control is exclusively through **prevention** (vector control, sanitation, resistant varieties).

### D. Nematode Diseases

- **Root-Knot Nematodes** (*Meloidogyne spp.*) — cause galls on roots
- Favoured by warm, moist soils; common in continuous cropping systems

---

## 2.2 Principles of Disease Control — Six Strategic Pillars

| # | Strategy | Mechanism | Example |
|---|----------|-----------|---------|
| 1 | **Avoidance** | Prevent contact between host & pathogen | Use disease-free seeds, raised benches |
| 2 | **Exclusion** | Prevent pathogen entry into greenhouse | Foot baths, screen vents, restricted access |
| 3 | **Cultural Control** | Modify growing environment/practices | Spacing, pruning, irrigation timing |
| 4 | **Biological Control** | Use beneficial organisms | *Trichoderma*, *Bacillus subtilis* |
| 5 | **Chemical Control** | Use fungicides/bactericides | Mancozeb, Copper, Bordeaux mixture |
| 6 | **Genetic / Host Resistance** | Use resistant cultivars | TMV-resistant tomato varieties |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Tip:** The most marks are awarded for explaining the **"Integrated Disease Management (IDM)"** approach, which combines 2 or more of the above strategies.

---

## 2.3 Environmental Management — The Engineer's Primary Tool

The single most powerful disease control method in a greenhouse is **environmental manipulation**, because the engineer/designer controls the climate.

### A. Humidity Control

- **Ventilation** (natural or forced) to remove moisture
- **Heating + Ventilation** combination to drop RH without condensation
- **Horizontal Air Flow (HAF) fans** to maintain **0.3–0.5 m/s** air movement

### B. Temperature Control

- Avoid temperature inversions where leaf temperature falls below dew point
- Maintain day-night temperature differential (**DIF**)

### C. Irrigation Management

- Use **drip irrigation** instead of overhead sprinklers
- Irrigate in the **morning** so foliage dries before night
- Avoid waterlogging

### D. Plant Spacing & Canopy Management

- Proper spacing increases air circulation
- Pruning lower leaves (e.g., in tomato) reduces soil splash onto foliage

### E. Sanitation

- Remove infected plant debris immediately
- Disinfect tools (10% NaOCl, 70% ethanol)
- Sterilize growing media

---

## 2.4 Greenhouse Sterilization & Soil/Media Disinfection

### A. Steam Sterilization

Most effective and chemical-free method. Soil is heated to **70 °C – 80 °C** for **30 minutes**.

### B. Solarization

- Moist soil covered with transparent polyethylene during hot summer months
- Soil temperature reaches **50–60 °C** at 5 cm depth
- Duration: **4–6 weeks** during peak summer

### C. Chemical Fumigation

- **Methyl Bromide** (banned under Montreal Protocol, 2005)
- **Formalin (Formaldehyde)** — 2% solution
- **Chloropicrin** — broad-spectrum
- **Basamid (Dazomet)** — granular fumigant

### D. Substrate Pasteurization

- Cocopeat, perlite, vermiculite can be steam-treated

---

## 2.5 Biological Control Agents (Biocontrol)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Biological control is a key NEP 2020 / KTU 2024 topic for sustainable agriculture.

| Biocontrol Agent | Target Pathogen | Mode of Action |
|------------------|-----------------|----------------|
| *Trichoderma harzianum* | *Pythium, Fusarium, Rhizoctonia* | Mycoparasitism, competition |
| *Bacillus subtilis* | *Botrytis, Powdery Mildew* | Antibiotic production |
| *Pseudomonas fluorescens* | Soil-borne pathogens | Siderophore production |
| *Ampelomyces quisqualis* | Powdery mildew | Hyperparasitism |
| *Coniothyrium minitans* | *Sclerotinia* | Parasitizes sclerotia |

**Application Methods:**
- Seed treatment
- Soil drenching
- Foliar spray
- Root dip
- Incorporated into growing media

---

## 2.6 Chemical Control — Fungicides & Bactericides

### Classification of Fungicides Used in Greenhouses

| Group | Example | Target | Notes |
|-------|---------|--------|-------|
| **Contact / Protectant** | Mancozeb, Chlorothalonil, Copper | Surface pathogens | Must be applied before infection |
| **Systemic** | Metalaxyl, Carbendazim, Benomyl | Internal infection | Single-site; resistance risk |
| **Ergosterol Biosynthesis Inhibitors (EBI)** | Triadimefon, Myclobutanil | Powdery mildew, rust | Systemic |
| **Strobilurins (QoI)** | Azoxystrobin | Broad-spectrum | Resistance risk |
| **Bio-fungicides** | *Trichoderma*, *Bacillus* | Multiple | Organic-compatible |

### Antibiotics for Bacterial Diseases

- **Streptocycline** (100–200 ppm) + Copper
- **Tetracycline**

### Virus Control — No Direct Chemical

- Only **vector control** (insecticides against whiteflies, thrips, aphids)
- **Reflective mulches** to repel vectors
- **Yellow/blue sticky traps**

---

## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet & Threshold Table

| Parameter / Formula | Symbol | Value / Unit | Application |
|---------------------|--------|--------------|-------------|
| **Critical Relative Humidity for Botrytis** | $RH_{crit}$ | **> 93%** | Grey mould outbreak threshold |
| **Disease Triangle** | $D = f(H, P, E)$ | Boolean intersection | Disease occurs only when H ∩ P ∩ E exist |
| **Dew Point Equation (Magnus form)** | $T_{dp}$ | ${}^{\circ}C$ | Used to set heating setpoints |
| **DIF (Day-Night Temp Difference)** | $DIF = T_{day} - T_{night}$ | °C | Controls internode elongation, disease pressure |
| **Air Velocity for HAF** | $V_{air}$ | 0.3 – 0.5 m/s | Prevents microclimate stagnation |
| **Soil Solarization Temperature** | $T_{soil}$ | 50 – 60 °C @ 5 cm | Pathogen kill threshold |
| **Steam Sterilization Temperature** | $T_{steam}$ | 70 – 80 °C | For 30 min at coolest point |
| **Leaf Wetness Duration (Critical)** | $t_{w}$ | > 4 hours | Spore germination threshold |
| **Conidial Germination Temperature** | $T_{germ}$ | 15 – 25 °C | Most foliar fungi |
| **Bordeaux Mixture** | $CuSO_4 : Ca(OH)_2 : H_2O$ | 1 : 1 : 100 (1%) | Classic protectant fungicide |

### Dew Point Calculation (Engineering Standard)

$$T_{dp} = \frac{b \cdot \gamma(T, RH)}{a - \gamma(T, RH)}$$

where:

$$\gamma(T, RH) = \frac{a \cdot T}{b + T} + \ln\!\left(\frac{RH}{100}\right)$$

Constants: $a = 17.625$, $b = 243.04\,^{\circ}C$

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations, Worked Examples & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1: Dew Point & Heating Setpoint Calculation

### Problem Statement

Inside a greenhouse at **2:00 AM**, the air temperature is **$T = 18\,^{\circ}C$** and relative humidity is **$RH = 95\%$**. The grower wants to use a **heating + ventilation strategy** to prevent condensation on the leaves. Determine the **dew point temperature** and the **minimum heating setpoint** required to prevent leaf wetness.

### Solution (Step-by-Step)

**Step 1: Compute the intermediate term $\gamma$.**

$$\gamma(T, RH) = \frac{a \cdot T}{b + T} + \ln\!\left(\frac{RH}{100}\right)$$

Substitute $a = 17.625$, $b = 243.04$, $T = 18$, $RH = 95$:

$$\gamma = \frac{17.625 \times 18}{243.04 + 18} + \ln(0.95)$$

$$\gamma = \frac{317.25}{261.04} + (-0.0513)$$

$$\gamma = 1.2153 - 0.0513 = 1.1640$$

**Step 2: Compute the dew point $T_{dp}$.**

$$T_{dp} = \frac{b \cdot \gamma}{a - \gamma}$$

$$T_{dp} = \frac{243.04 \times 1.1640}{17.625 - 1.1640}$$

$$T_{dp} = \frac{282.90}{16.461}$$

$$\boxed{T_{dp} \approx 17.19\,^{\circ}C}$$

**Step 3: Engineer Interpretation.**

Since the dew point is **17.19 °C** and leaf temperature is approximately equal to air temperature (**18 °C**), the leaves are only **0.81 °C** above dew point — condensation is imminent.

**Step 4: Recommended Heating Setpoint.**

To maintain a **2 °C safety margin** above dew point:

$$T_{setpoint} = T_{dp} + 2 = 17.19 + 2 = 19.19\,^{\circ}C$$

> [!TIP]
> **Board Tip:** This problem tests whether the student understands that **heating alone can suppress fungal disease** by keeping leaf surfaces dry — a classic KTU 2024 design-oriented question.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2: Disease Triangle & Vector Control (Botrytis Outbreak)

### Scenario

A tomato greenhouse in Wayanad has:
- **Host:** Susceptible tomato variety "Pusa Ruby"
- **Pathogen:** *Botrytis cinerea* spores detected (10 conidia/cm²)
- **Environment:** Night temperature **18 °C**, RH **96%**, leaf wetness **6 hours**

**Question:** Will an outbreak occur? Justify using the disease triangle and recommend control measures.

### Solution

**Step 1: Apply the disease triangle $D = H \cap P \cap E$.**

| Vertex | Status |
|--------|--------|
| Host (H) | **Present** — susceptible variety present |
| Pathogen (P) | **Present** — inoculum detected |
| Environment (E) | **Favourable** — $T = 18\,^{\circ}C \in [18, 22]$, $RH = 96\% > 93\%$, $t_w = 6\,h > 4\,h$ |

Since all three vertices are present, **disease WILL occur**. Outbreak probability $\approx 1.0$.

**Step 2: Recommended Control Hierarchy.**

1. **Immediate (within 24 hours):**
   - Turn on **heating** to raise $T$ to 20 °C and drop RH to < 85%
   - Activate **HAF fans** (set $V_{air} = 0.4\,m/s$)
   - Ventilate to expel humid air

2. **Curative (within 48 hours):**
   - Apply **fungicide**: Carbendazim 50% WP @ 0.1% (systemic) or Mancozeb 75% WP @ 0.25% (contact)
   - Remove infected plant debris and burn

3. **Preventive (next cycle):**
   - Switch to **resistant variety** (e.g., hybrid tomato with grey-mould tolerance)
   - Use **drip irrigation** (no overhead watering)
   - Implement **biological control** (*Trichoderma viride* @ 5 g/L as soil drench)

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Decision Flow for Disease Identification & Control

| Symptom Observed | Likely Pathogen | Confirmatory Test | First-Line Control |
|------------------|-----------------|-------------------|-------------------|
| Damping-off of seedlings | *Pythium, Rhizoctonia* | Microscopic ID | Sterilize media; drench with *Trichoderma* |
| Grey fuzzy growth on flowers/leaves | *Botrytis cinerea* | Visual + microscope | Reduce RH; Mancozeb spray |
| White powdery coating on leaves | *Erysiphe* (Powdery Mildew) | Visual | Sulphur dust; Triadimefon |
| Yellow angular leaf spots with downy growth underneath | *Pseudoperonospora* (Downy Mildew) | Microscope | Metalaxyl + Mancozeb; ventilation |
| Vascular browning, one-sided wilt | *Fusarium oxysporum* | Lab culture | Resistant variety; soil solarization |
| White cottony mycelium + black sclerotia | *Sclerotinia sclerotiorum* | Visual | Remove debris; *Coniothyrium minitans* |
| Yellow leaf curl + whiteflies | TYLCV (virus) | ELISA / PCR | Whitefly control; resistant variety |
| Root galls, stunted growth | *Meloidogyne* (nematode) | Root examination | *Paecilomyces lilacinus*; soil solarization |

---

## 3.4 Step-by-Step Procedure — Soil Solarization Protocol

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Procedural Knowledge** — KTU frequently tests sequential procedures.

**Step 1:** Select a sunny summer period (April–May in Kerala).

**Step 2:** Clear the bed of all plant debris and weeds.

**Step 3:** Plough the soil to a fine tilth; remove stones and clods.

**Step 4:** Irrigate the bed to **field capacity** (moisture is essential for heat conduction).

**Step 5:** Cover with **transparent polyethylene sheet (25–50 µm)**; bury the edges to seal.

**Step 6:** Leave for **4–6 weeks**. Soil temperature at 5 cm depth reaches **50–60 °C**.

**Step 7:** Remove the sheet, lightly cultivate, and allow aeration for 2–3 days before planting.

**Effectiveness:** Kills most fungi, nematodes, weed seeds; less effective against deep pathogens or *Fusarium* chlamydospores.

---

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define the 'Disease Triangle' and explain its significance in greenhouse plant disease management.**

**Model Answer:**

The Disease Triangle is a foundational concept in plant pathology that states a disease can occur only when three factors coincide:

1. **A susceptible host plant** (genetically vulnerable crop),
2. **A virulent pathogen** (fungal, bacterial, viral, or nematode agent), and
3. **A favourable environment** (appropriate temperature, humidity, leaf wetness, and pH).

**Significance in Greenhouses:** The greenhouse engineer has direct control over the **environment** vertex — temperature, relative humidity, ventilation, irrigation method, and plant spacing. By manipulating any one vertex, disease can be prevented. Since environment is the easiest to manipulate under protected cultivation, the Disease Triangle guides growers to **prioritise climate control over chemical spraying**, reducing input costs and producing residue-free crops.

---

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**List any three biocontrol agents used in greenhouse disease management with their target pathogens.**

**Model Answer:**

| # | Biocontrol Agent | Target Pathogen / Disease |
|---|------------------|---------------------------|
| 1 | *Trichoderma harzianum* | *Pythium* spp., *Fusarium* spp., *Rhizoctonia solani* (damping-off, wilt) |
| 2 | *Bacillus subtilis* | *Botrytis cinerea* (grey mould), Powdery mildew |
| 3 | *Ampelomyces quisqualis* | Powdery mildew (hyperparasite) |

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - Students often **omit the disease triangle** while describing IDM — this is a guaranteed 2-mark loss.
> - Writing only the **names of fungicides** without mentioning **mode of action / group / target pathogen** leads to part-mark deduction.
> - For questions on **biological control**, do not forget to mention the **application method** (seed treatment, soil drench, foliar spray) — examiners specifically look for this.
> - In **viral disease questions**, never suggest a curative chemical. Mention **vector control + resistant variety + sanitation** only.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**(a)** Explain the **Integrated Disease Management (IDM) approach** in greenhouses with reference to **fungal diseases**. Discuss **cultural, biological, and chemical** control methods in detail. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **classification of greenhouse diseases** based on the causal organism with suitable examples. Add a note on **viral diseases** and their management. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for Part (a) — IDM for Fungal Diseases

**1. Definition of IDM:**
Integrated Disease Management is a sustainable crop protection strategy that combines multiple control methods (cultural, biological, chemical, genetic, and physical) in a compatible manner to keep pest populations below the economic threshold level, while minimizing environmental and health risks.

**2. Cultural Control Methods:**

- **Use disease-free planting material** — purchase certified, disease-indexed seedlings or raise from healthy mother plants.
- **Sanitation** — removal and burning of infected debris, dead leaves, and senescent flowers; this eliminates the primary inoculum source for *Botrytis cinerea* and *Sclerotinia*.
- **Proper plant spacing** — wider spacing improves air movement, reduces leaf wetness duration, and lowers humidity within the canopy.
- **Drip irrigation** instead of overhead sprinklers — keeps foliage dry.
- **Crop rotation** — avoid continuous monoculture; rotate with non-host crops (e.g., rotate tomato with marigold to suppress *Fusarium* and nematodes).
- **Pruning and training** — remove lower leaves of tomato, cucumber, and pepper to prevent soil splash onto foliage.
- **Weed management** — weeds serve as alternate hosts for many pathogens and vectors.

**3. Biological Control Methods:**

- ***Trichoderma harzianum*** — applied as seed treatment (10 g/kg seed) or soil drench (5 g/L water); controls *Pythium, Fusarium, Rhizoctonia* through mycoparasitism, competition for nutrients, and production of antifungal metabolites.
- ***Bacillus subtilis*** — foliar spray (2 g/L); effective against *Botrytis cinerea* and powdery mildew; produces antibiotics (iturin, surfactin).
- ***Pseudomonas fluorescens*** — soil application; controls root rots via siderophore-mediated iron competition.
- ***Ampelomyces quisqualis*** — hyperparasite of powdery mildew fungi; reduces primary inoculum.

**4. Chemical Control Methods:**

- **Protectant (Contact) fungicides:** Mancozeb 75% WP @ 0.25%, Chlorothalonil 75% WP @ 0.2%, Copper oxychloride 50% WP @ 0.3%. These form a protective film on leaf surface and must be applied *before* infection.
- **Systemic fungicides:** Carbendazim 50% WP @ 0.1%, Metalaxyl 8% + Mancozeb 64% WP @ 0.25%, Triadimefon 25% WP @ 0.1%.
- **Rotation of fungicide groups** is essential to prevent pathogen resistance — alternate EBI (Triadimefon) with QoI (Azoxystrobin) and contact (Mancozeb).

**5. Conclusion:**
IDM is not a single technique but a **strategic combination** of methods tailored to the specific crop-pathogen-environment complex. Under greenhouse conditions, cultural and environmental methods take precedence, with biocontrol as a supportive layer and chemicals as a last resort.

#### Model Solution for Part (b) — Classification of Greenhouse Diseases

**1. Fungal Diseases** (most common; ~70% of greenhouse diseases):

- *Botrytis cinerea* — Grey mould (tomato, cucumber, ornamentals)
- *Sphaerotheca fuliginea* — Powdery mildew (cucurbits)
- *Pseudoperonospora cubensis* — Downy mildew (cucumber)
- *Fusarium oxysporum* — Vascular wilt (tomato, carnation)
- *Sclerotinia sclerotiorum* — White mould
- *Pythium / Rhizoctonia* — Damping-off of seedlings

**2. Bacterial Diseases:**

- *Ralstonia solanacearum* — Bacterial wilt
- *Xanthomonas campestris* — Bacterial leaf spot
- *Erwinia carotovora* — Soft rot of vegetables

**3. Viral Diseases:**

- Tomato Mosaic Virus (ToMV)
- Cucumber Mosaic Virus (CMV)
- Tomato Yellow Leaf Curl Virus (TYLCV)
- Tomato Spotted Wilt Virus (TSWV)

**4. Nematode Diseases:**

- *Meloidogyne incognita* — Root-knot nematode

**5. Note on Viral Diseases and Their Management:**

Viral diseases are the **most destructive** because:
- They are **systemic** — spread throughout the plant vascular system.
- They **cannot be cured** by any chemical (no antiviral is registered for plant use).
- They are **transmitted** by insect vectors (whiteflies, thrips, aphids) and through **mechanical means** (tools, hands, tobacco products).

**Management Strategies:**
- Use of **virus-resistant cultivars** (e.g., TYLCV-resistant tomato hybrids).
- **Vector control** through yellow/blue sticky traps, neem oil sprays, and insecticides (Imidacloprid 17.8% SL @ 0.3 ml/L).
- **Sanitation** — disinfecting tools with 10% NaOCl, restricting tobacco use in greenhouse.
- **Reflective mulches** (silver-coloured polyethylene) to repel vectors.
- **Roguing** — immediate removal and destruction of infected plants.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Alternative Choice]
**(a)** Discuss the **environmental management strategies** for disease control in greenhouses. Explain how **temperature, humidity, ventilation, and air circulation** are manipulated to suppress foliar fungal diseases. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **methods of soil and growing media sterilization** in greenhouses. Compare **steam sterilization, solarization, and chemical fumigation** with respect to efficacy, cost, and environmental impact. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for Part (a) — Environmental Management

**1. Temperature Management:**

- Maintain day temperature at **22–28 °C** and night temperature at **18–20 °C** for most greenhouse vegetables.
- Avoid temperature inversion (cold air pooling) which causes condensation.
- Use **thermal screens** at night for energy saving and uniform temperature distribution.

**2. Humidity Management (Most Critical):**

- Most foliar fungal pathogens require **RH > 85%** for spore germination.
- Strategies to reduce humidity:
  - **Ventilation** (natural side + roof vents, or forced exhaust fans).
  - **Heating + Ventilation combination** — heating raises air temperature, lowering RH without raising leaf temperature excessively.
  - **Avoid evening irrigation** so foliage is dry by night.
  - **Use of dehumidifiers** in high-value crops.
- **Target:** RH < 85% during night, leaf wetness duration < 4 hours.

**3. Ventilation Design:**

- Natural ventilation: Ridge + side vents; cross-ventilation.
- Forced ventilation: Exhaust fans + cooling pads.
- Ventilation rate: **0.04 to 0.06 m³/s per m²** of floor area.

**4. Air Circulation (Horizontal Air Flow — HAF):**

- Use of **HAF fans** placed horizontally to maintain uniform air movement.
- **Air velocity inside canopy: 0.3 – 0.5 m/s**.
- Prevents microclimates of stagnant, humid air around leaf surfaces — these are "infection courts" for *Botrytis* and *Sclerotinia*.

**5. Integrated Effect:**

- Combined control of T, RH, and air movement is more effective than chemical spraying for foliar fungal suppression.
- Cost-effective in the long term — reduces fungicide expenditure and residue on produce.

#### Model Solution for Part (b) — Soil & Media Sterilization

| Method | Principle | Efficacy | Cost | Environmental Impact |
|--------|-----------|----------|------|----------------------|
| **Steam Sterilization** | Moist heat at 70–80 °C for 30 min | Excellent — kills fungi, bacteria, nematodes, weed seeds, most viruses | High (boiler + piping) | Negligible — no chemical residue |
| **Solarization** | Solar heating through transparent PE; 50–60 °C at 5 cm depth for 4–6 weeks | Moderate — most fungi & nematodes; less effective on deep pathogens & chlamydospores | Low (only plastic sheet) | Very low — non-chemical, sustainable |
| **Chemical Fumigation** (Methyl Bromide, Chloropicrin, Dazomet) | Volatile toxic chemicals penetrate soil | Excellent — broad-spectrum | Moderate–High | High — methyl bromide banned (ozone depleter); others toxic to handlers |

**1. Steam Sterilization — Detailed Procedure:**

- Place perforated steam pipes 30 cm below soil surface.
- Cover soil with heat-resistant tarpaulin.
- Introduce steam until **deepest point reaches 70 °C** and hold for **30 minutes**.
- Allow soil to aerate for 2 weeks before planting (to avoid phytotoxicity from accumulated ammonia and manganese).

**2. Solarization — Detailed Procedure:**

- Plough and irrigate soil to field capacity.
- Cover with 25–50 µm transparent PE sheet; seal edges.
- Expose for **4–6 weeks** during peak summer (April–May in Kerala).
- Soil temperatures reach **50–60 °C at 5 cm** and **40–45 °C at 30 cm**.

**3. Chemical Fumigation:**

- **Methyl Bromide** — banned under Montreal Protocol (2005) due to ozone depletion.
- **Basamid (Dazomet)** — granular, releases methyl isothiocyanate; safer alternative.
- **Formalin** — 2% solution drenched on soil; effective but toxic.

**4. Substrate Pasteurization:**

- Coco peat, perlite, vermiculite can be steam-treated at 70 °C for 30 min.
- Rockwool is sterile by manufacturing process.

**5. Conclusion:**

In the context of **KTU 2024 NEP-aligned sustainable horticulture**, **steam sterilization and solarization are preferred** over chemical fumigation, in line with organic and residue-free certification requirements.

---

## 6. Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist**

- ✅ **Disease Triangle** = Host ∩ Pathogen ∩ Environment. Disease occurs only when all three coincide. Manipulate the **environment vertex** first in greenhouses.
- ✅ **Most critical greenhouse pathogens:** *Botrytis cinerea* (grey mould), Powdery mildew, Downy mildew, *Fusarium*, *Pythium*, TYLCV.
- ✅ **RH threshold for most foliar fungi:** **> 85%** (Botrytis: > 93%). Maintain RH < 85% by heating + ventilation.
- ✅ **Air velocity inside canopy:** **0.3 – 0.5 m/s** using HAF fans.
- ✅ **Leaf wetness duration** must remain **< 4 hours** to prevent spore germination.
- ✅ **Steam sterilization:** **70–80 °C for 30 min** at the coolest point.
- ✅ **Solarization:** Transparent PE for **4–6 weeks**; reaches **50–60 °C at 5 cm**.
- ✅ **Methyl Bromide is BANNED** (Montreal Protocol, 2005) — use Basamid / steam / solarization.
- ✅ **Biocontrol agents:** *Trichoderma* (soil pathogens), *Bacillus subtilis* (foliar), *Ampelomyces* (powdery mildew).
- ✅ **Viral diseases have NO cure** — control through resistant varieties + vector control + sanitation.
- ✅ **Fungicide rotation** is essential to prevent resistance — alternate Contact (Mancozeb) ↔ Systemic (Carbendazim) ↔ EBI (Triadimefon) ↔ QoI (Azoxystrobin).
- ✅ **IDM = Cultural + Biological + Chemical + Genetic** methods combined — not a single technique.
- ✅ **Drip irrigation > Overhead irrigation** for disease suppression in greenhouses.
- ✅ **Dew point + 2 °C safety margin** = recommended minimum heating setpoint.
- ✅ **Reflective (silver) mulch** repels whiteflies and reduces TYLCV transmission.
- ✅ **Yellow sticky traps** = whiteflies/aphids; **Blue sticky traps** = thrips.
- ✅ **Bordeaux mixture** = 1% (1:1:100 — CuSO₄ : Ca(OH)₂ : H₂O) — classic protectant.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Integrated Pest Management (IPM)** is a science-based, sustainable pest control strategy that combines multiple management practices and control methods (cultural, mechanical, biological, and chemical) in a compatible manner to keep pest populations below the **Economic Threshold Level (ETL)** while minimizing risks to human health, beneficial organisms, and the environment.

In the context of **protected cultivation (greenhouse systems)**, IPM is especially critical because the enclosed, humid, and warm microenvironment of a greenhouse accelerates pest reproduction cycles (often 2–3× faster than open-field conditions) and provides a refuge from natural predators. As per KTU 2024 syllabus, IPM in greenhouses is treated as a **systems-engineering problem** where biological, physical, and chemical components must operate in harmony.

### 1.2 Key Terminology (KTU Syllabus Glossary)

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Pest** | Any organism (insect, mite, fungus, bacterium, virus, weed) that causes economic damage to a crop |
| **Economic Injury Level (EIL)** | The lowest pest population density that will cause economic damage greater than the cost of control |
| **Economic Threshold Level (ETL)** | The pest density at which control measures should be applied to prevent the population from reaching the EIL |
| **Beneficial Organism** | Natural enemies of pests (parasitoids, predators, pathogens) |
| **Biocide** | A chemical substance that destroys living organisms |
| **Pheromone Trap** | A trap using species-specific sex-attractant chemicals to monitor or mass-trap pests |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Traffic Police" Model

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition:** Imagine a busy city intersection. You do not need to ban all cars to prevent accidents. Instead, you install traffic signals (cultural practices), speed-breakers (mechanical barriers), deploy traffic police (biological control agents), and use CCTV plus fines (monitoring + targeted chemical control). **IPM works exactly the same way for greenhouse pests** — it uses *multiple complementary layers* rather than relying on a single "spray and kill" chemical weapon. Each layer is a "line of defense" that, if one fails, the others still protect the crop.

The **strategic objective** is *suppression*, not *eradication* — keeping pest populations at tolerable, non-damaging levels indefinitely.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Pillars of IPM (KTU Mandatory Framework)

The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly groups IPM practices into **five functional pillars**. Each pillar represents a distinct pest-control philosophy and is engineered to be compatible with the others.

### Pillar 1 — Cultural Control (Preventive Agronomy)
This pillar modifies the greenhouse environment and crop husbandry to *prevent* pest establishment. It is the **first line of defense**.

- **Crop rotation** in adjacent/nearby fields
- **Sanitation:** removal of infected plant debris, weed hosts, and volunteer plants
- **Resistant cultivars:** using pathogen-tolerant or pest-repellent varieties
- **Spacing & pruning:** improving air circulation to reduce humidity-loving fungi
- **Use of certified disease-free seeds and planting material**
- **Manipulation of planting/harvesting dates** to avoid peak pest windows

### Pillar 2 — Mechanical / Physical Control
Direct, non-chemical removal or exclusion of pests using physical means.

- **Insect-proof nets** (50-mesh for whiteflies, 75-mesh for thrips) on greenhouse vents
- **Sticky traps** (yellow for whiteflies/aphids; blue for thrips)
- **Light traps** for nocturnal moths
- **Hand-picking** of large larvae
- **Soil solarization:** covering moist soil with transparent polyethylene for 4–6 weeks (raises soil temperature to **> 50 °C** to kill nematodes, fungi, and weed seeds)
- **Hot water treatment** of planting material
- **Vacuuming** of pests in enclosed greenhouses

### Pillar 3 — Biological Control (The "Living Pesticide")
Using living organisms — predators, parasitoids, and pathogens — to suppress pest populations. **In greenhouses, this is the most powerful IPM tool** because the enclosed environment protects released beneficials from wind, rain, and emigration.

| Biocontrol Agent | Type | Target Pest |
|---|---|---|
| *Phytoseiulus persimilis* | Predatory mite | Two-spotted spider mite (*Tetranychus urticae*) |
| *Encarsia formosa* | Parasitoid wasp | Greenhouse whitefly (*Trialeurodes vaporariorum*) |
| *Amblyseius swirskii* | Predatory mite | Thrips, whitefly eggs |
| *Neoseiulus cucumeris* | Predatory mite | Western flower thrips |
| *Aphidius colemani* | Parasitoid wasp | Aphids |
| *Trichoderma harzianum* | Beneficial fungus | Soil-borne pathogens (*Fusarium*, *Pythium*, *Rhizoctonia*) |
| *Bacillus thuringiensis* (Bt) | Beneficial bacterium | Lepidopteran larvae (caterpillars) |
| *Metarhizium anisopliae* | Entomopathogenic fungus | Whiteflies, thrips, beetles |

### Pillar 4 — Chemical Control (Last Resort, Used Judiciously)
Synthetic or botanical pesticides used only when pest populations exceed the ETL, and selected to be:
- **Selective** (kills target pest, spares beneficials)
- **Non-persistent** (degrades quickly, low residue)
- **Rotated** (different modes-of-action to prevent resistance)

Examples preferred in IPM: **neem oil (azadirachtin)**, **insecticidal soaps**, **horticultural oils**, **spinosad**, **abamectin**.

### Pillar 5 — Legal / Regulatory Control
Government-enforced quarantine, certification, and phytosanitary regulations preventing the introduction and spread of pests.

## 2.2 Pest Monitoring & Decision-Making (The "Brain" of IPM)

> [!NOTE]
> **The IPM Decision Pyramid:** The cornerstone of IPM is the principle that **NO pesticide should ever be applied without first scouting, identifying, and quantifying the pest population** relative to the ETL.

Monitoring tools include:
- **Yellow sticky traps** (standard: 1 trap per **100 m²**)
- **Blue sticky traps** (for thrips: 1 trap per **50 m²**)
- **Pheromone traps** (species-specific)
- **Visual scouting** (weekly leaf inspections: check 10 plants per 1000 m²)
- **Soil sampling** for nematode and pathogen analysis

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following equations and constants are mandatory for KTU 2024 ESE numerical/analytical questions on this topic.

### 2.3.1 Economic Threshold Level (ETL)

$$
ETL = \frac{C}{V \times D \times K}
$$

Where:

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $C$ | Cost of pest control (per unit area) | ₹ per hectare |
| $V$ | Market value of the crop (per unit yield) | ₹ per kg |
| $D$ | Estimated crop damage per pest individual | kg per pest |
| $K$ | Proportionate reduction in pest population expected from the control measure | dimensionless (0 < K ≤ 1) |

### 2.3.2 Economic Injury Level (EIL)

$$
EIL = \frac{ETL}{(1 - e^{-r \cdot t})}
$$

Where $r$ = intrinsic rate of natural increase of the pest population (per day) and $t$ = time lag between scouting and pest-control action (days).

### 2.3.3 Sticky Trap Density (KTU Greenhouse Standard)

$$
N_{traps} = \frac{A_{greenhouse}}{A_{trap\_service}}
$$

Where $A_{greenhouse}$ is the total greenhouse floor area (m²) and $A_{trap\_service}$ is the service area per trap:
- **Yellow sticky trap** for whiteflies: $A_{trap\_service} = 100 \text{ m}^2$
- **Blue sticky trap** for thrips: $A_{trap\_service} = 50 \text{ m}^2$

### 2.3.4 Biocontrol Agent Release Rate

$$
N_{release} = R \times A_{greenhouse}
$$

Where $R$ is the recommended release rate (agents per m²) — a KTU-tabulated value depending on the agent and pest pressure (e.g., *Encarsia formosa*: $R \approx 5$ wasps/m² for preventive release, $R \approx 15$ wasps/m² for curative release).

### 2.3.5 Soil Solarization Heat Energy Equation

$$
Q = m \cdot c \cdot \Delta T
$$

Where $Q$ = heat energy required (J), $m$ = mass of soil volume treated (kg), $c$ = specific heat capacity of moist soil (≈ **2,100 J·kg⁻¹·K⁻¹**), and $\Delta T$ = target temperature rise (K).

### 2.3.6 Summary Table — All Formulas at a Glance

| Formula | Equation | Application |
|---|---|---|
| ETL | $ETL = C / (V \cdot D \cdot K)$ | Deciding *when* to spray |
| EIL | $EIL = ETL / (1 - e^{-rt})$ | Theoretical population at which damage = cost of control |
| Trap count | $N = A_{gh} / A_{svc}$ | Number of sticky/pheromone traps required |
| Release rate | $N_{rel} = R \cdot A_{gh}$ | Number of biocontrol agents to release |
| Solarization energy | $Q = m \cdot c \cdot \Delta T$ | Heat energy delivered to soil |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Commercial greenhouse IPM programs** in Kerala (capsicum, rose, gerbera) routinely achieve **60–80 % reduction in pesticide use** with no yield loss.
- **Resistance management:** IPM delays the evolution of pesticide-resistant pest biotypes — a critical issue in *Tetranychus urticae* (spider mite), which develops resistance within **8–10 generations**.
- **Export compliance:** IPM-certified produce fetches a **20–40 % premium** in EU and Gulf markets, where Maximum Residue Limits (MRLs) are tightly regulated.
- **Pollinator protection:** IPM preserves bumblebees (*Bombus impatiens*) used for pollination in tomato greenhouses.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Derivation: Trap Count for a Greenhouse

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem (KTU 2024 Model Numerical):** A capsicum greenhouse has a floor area of **800 m²**. Compute the number of yellow sticky traps required to monitor whiteflies, and the number of *Encarsia formosa* wasps to release for preventive biocontrol.

### Step 1 — Identify the given data
The problem statement provides:
- $A_{greenhouse} = 800 \text{ m}^2$
- Trap type = yellow sticky (service area $A_{trap\_service} = 100 \text{ m}^2$ per KTU standard)
- Biocontrol = *Encarsia formosa* (preventive release rate $R = 5 \text{ wasps/m}^2$)

### Step 2 — Apply the sticky-trap equation

$$
N_{traps} = \frac{A_{greenhouse}}{A_{trap\_service}}
$$

$$
N_{traps} = \frac{800 \text{ m}^2}{100 \text{ m}^2/\text{trap}}
$$

$$
N_{traps} = 8 \text{ traps}
$$

### Step 3 — Apply the release-rate equation

$$
N_{release} = R \times A_{greenhouse}
$$

$$
N_{release} = 5 \text{ wasps/m}^2 \times 800 \text{ m}^2
$$

$$
N_{release} = 4{,}000 \text{ wasps}
$$

### Step 4 — Distribute evenly and state the field recommendation

Place the **8 yellow sticky traps** uniformly — for a rectangular greenhouse, distribute them in a **4 × 2 grid** (4 along the length, 2 along the width), suspended **15–30 cm above the canopy** and replaced every **2 weeks**. Release the **4,000 wasps** in **2 weekly batches of 2,000** at dawn to maximize establishment.

## 3.2 Worked Derivation: Computing the Economic Threshold Level

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** For a capsicum crop valued at **₹40/kg**, a single whitefly adult is estimated to cause **0.005 kg** of yield loss. The cost of an insecticidal soap spray is **₹2,500 per 1,000 m²**, and the spray is expected to kill **80 %** of the population. Compute the ETL.

### Step 1 — Extract parameters
- $V = ₹40/\text{kg}$
- $D = 0.005 \text{ kg/pest}$
- $C = ₹2{,}500$ per 1,000 m²
- $K = 0.80$ (80 % kill)

### Step 2 — Substitute into the ETL formula

$$
ETL = \frac{C}{V \times D \times K}
$$

$$
ETL = \frac{2{,}500}{40 \times 0.005 \times 0.80}
$$

### Step 3 — Compute the denominator

$$
40 \times 0.005 = 0.20
$$

$$
0.20 \times 0.80 = 0.16
$$

### Step 4 — Final division

$$
ETL = \frac{2{,}500}{0.16} = 15{,}625 \text{ whiteflies per 1,000 m}^2
$$

### Step 5 — Engineering interpretation

Control measures should be initiated the moment weekly scouting records more than **15,625 whiteflies per 1,000 m²** (averaged across the yellow sticky trap counts). This is the *economic red line* — below it, spraying is uneconomical; above it, spraying pays for itself and then some.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Compatibility of IPM Components

The following matrix evaluates the *compatibility* of different control methods — a key KTU 2024 high-yield concept.

| Method A ↓ / Method B → | Cultural | Mechanical | Biological | Chemical |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Cultural** | ✓ | ✓✓ | ✓✓ | ✓ |
| **Mechanical** | ✓✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ |
| **Biological** | ✓✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✗ (avoid broad-spectrum) |
| **Chemical** | ✓ | ✓ | ✗ (selective only) | ✓ (rotate MoA) |

Legend: ✓✓ = synergistic (combine for additive effect); ✓ = compatible; ✗ = conflicting (avoid simultaneous use).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** The single most common error students make is recommending a **broad-spectrum pesticide** (e.g., imidacloprid, chlorpyriphos) alongside a biological control program. Such pesticides kill the released beneficials *as effectively as they kill the pests*, collapsing the entire IPM framework.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 IPM Decision Flow — Block-Level Functional Architecture

The following Mermaid flowchart maps the full IPM decision cycle as a Block-Level Functional Architecture. Each block represents a distinct functional module with defined inputs and outputs.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Greenhouse Crop System] --> A1[Module 1: Scouting and Monitoring]
    A1 --> A2[Module 2: Pest Identification]
    A2 --> A3[Module 3: Quantify Population Density]
    A3 --> A4{Decision: Population vs ETL}
    A4 -->|Below ETL| A5[Module 4a: No Action - Record Data]
    A4 -->|At ETL| A6[Module 4b: Initiate IPM]
    A4 -->|Above ETL| A7[Module 4c: Emergency Chemical Intervention]
    A5 --> A8[Module 5: Continue Routine Scouting]
    A8 --> A1
    A6 --> B1[Module 6a: Cultural Controls]
    A6 --> B2[Module 6b: Mechanical Controls]
    A6 --> B3[Module 6c: Biological Controls]
    A6 --> B4[Module 6d: Selective Chemical]
    B1 --> C1[Module 7: Integrated Action Plan]
    B2 --> C1
    B3 --> C1
    B4 --> C1
    C1 --> D1[Module 8: Re-Scout and Evaluate]
    D1 --> A1
    A7 --> D1
```

## 4.2 Schematic of a Greenhouse IPM Trap-and-Release Layout

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    GH1[Greenhouse Vent Insect Net 50 Mesh] --> GH2[Greenhouse Interior]
    GH2 --> T1[Yellow Sticky Trap Whitefly Monitoring]
    GH2 --> T2[Blue Sticky Trap Thrips Monitoring]
    GH2 --> T3[Pheromone Trap Moth Monitoring]
    GH2 --> B1[Encarsia formosa Whitefly Parasitoid Release]
    GH2 --> B2[Phytoseiulus Spider Mite Predator]
    GH2 --> B3[Trichoderma Soil Drench Root Pathogens]
    GH2 --> C1[Capsicum Crop Canopy]
    B1 --> C1
    B2 --> C1
    B3 --> C1
    T1 --> SC1[Weekly Scouting Log]
    T2 --> SC1
    T3 --> SC1
    SC1 --> DEC{ETL Decision}
    DEC -->|Below ETL| NOACT[No Action]
    DEC -->|Above ETL| CHEM[Selective Botanical Spray]
```

## 4.3 Conceptual Hierarchy of the Five IPM Pillars

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    IPM[Integrated Pest Management IPM] --> P1[Pillar 1: Cultural Control]
    IPM --> P2[Pillar 2: Mechanical or Physical Control]
    IPM --> P3[Pillar 3: Biological Control]
    IPM --> P4[Pillar 4: Chemical Control Judicious]
    IPM --> P5[Pillar 5: Legal or Regulatory Control]
    P1 --> E1[Prevention First Line]

    P3 --> E3[Living Pesticides]

```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] Define the term **Economic Threshold Level (ETL)**. How does it differ from the **Economic Injury Level (EIL)**?

**Model Answer:**

The **Economic Threshold Level (ETL)** is the pest population density at which control measures **must be initiated** to prevent the rising population from reaching the EIL. It is essentially the *trigger point* for action.

The **Economic Injury Level (EIL)** is the lowest pest density at which the **economic damage caused equals the cost of control** — i.e., the damage-cost crossover point.

**Key difference:** ETL ≤ EIL. The ETL is *always set below* the EIL because there is a time lag between scouting, decision-making, and the actual application of control measures. The ETL provides a *safety buffer* so that by the time the chemical or biocontrol agent takes effect, the pest population has not yet crossed the damage threshold. Mathematically:

$$
ETL = \frac{C}{V \cdot D \cdot K}
$$

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam — June 2024] List any **three advantages** of biological control over chemical control in greenhouse pest management.

**Model Answer:**

1. **Target specificity:** Biocontrol agents (e.g., *Phytoseiulus persimilis*) attack a narrow range of prey, preserving beneficials, pollinators, and non-target organisms. Broad-spectrum pesticides kill indiscriminately.
2. **No pesticide resistance:** Living organisms co-evolve with pests, so resistance does not develop — unlike the rapid resistance seen in *Tetranychus urticae* and whiteflies.
3. **Zero toxic residue:** Biocontrol leaves no chemical residue on produce, enabling compliance with strict export MRL standards and ensuring consumer safety.

*(Other acceptable points: self-perpetuating in the enclosed greenhouse environment; cost-effective over multiple crop cycles; environmentally sustainable.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** When answering Part B questions, students frequently **(a)** confuse ETL with EIL, **(b)** recommend broad-spectrum chemicals that destroy biocontrol agents, and **(c)** omit the *monitoring* pillar, which is the foundation of IPM. Always begin your answer with a **scouting and identification block** and end with a **monitoring-feedback loop**. Marks are reserved specifically for the *integration* of methods — a list of methods without integration discussion loses up to **4 marks**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks):** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **concept and principles of Integrated Pest Management (IPM)** as applied to protected cultivation. Discuss in detail the **five major components** of IPM.

**(b) [7 Marks)** With a neat schematic, describe the use of **yellow and blue sticky traps** in a greenhouse. A tomato greenhouse has a floor area of **1,200 m²**. Calculate the number of yellow sticky traps required for whitefly monitoring and the number of *Encarsia formosa* parasitoids required for a **curative release** (release rate = 15 wasps/m²).

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Concept:** IPM is a sustainable pest-control philosophy that combines multiple compatible control methods in a planned, science-based program to keep pest populations below the ETL while minimizing environmental, health, and economic risks. In greenhouses, the enclosed, warm, humid environment accelerates pest life cycles (often 2–3× faster than in open fields) and simultaneously *protects* released beneficials from emigration and weather — making greenhouse IPM particularly effective.

**Principles of IPM (KTU 2024 framework):**
1. **Prevention first** — design the system to keep pests out.
2. **Monitor continuously** — never spray without scouting.
3. **Threshold-based decisions** — act only when economically justified.
4. **Integration** — combine multiple methods for additive effect.
5. **Minimize chemical use** — pesticides are the last resort.
6. **Evaluate and adapt** — feedback loop to refine the program.

**Five Components (with greenhouse-specific examples):**

| # | Component | Greenhouse Example |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Cultural** | Sanitization, resistant cultivars, proper spacing, weed removal, drip irrigation to keep foliage dry |
| 2 | **Mechanical/Physical** | 50-mesh insect nets on vents, sticky traps, light traps, soil solarization, hot water treatment of seedlings |
| 3 | **Biological** | *Encarsia formosa* (whitefly), *Phytoseiulus persimilis* (spider mite), *Trichoderma* (root rot) |
| 4 | **Chemical (judicious)** | Neem oil, insecticidal soaps, *Bacillus thuringiensis*; rotate modes of action to delay resistance |
| 5 | **Legal/Regulatory** | Phytosanitary certification, quarantine of new plant material, FYM sterilization protocols |

**Conclusion:** IPM in greenhouses is a closed-loop engineering system requiring weekly scouting, data-driven threshold checks, and disciplined integration of cultural, mechanical, biological, and (only when necessary) chemical tools.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Sticky Trap Schematic and Function:**

- **Yellow sticky traps** (coated with insect glue, wavelength ≈ **570 nm**) are highly attractive to **whiteflies, aphids, leafminers, and fungus gnats**. In a greenhouse they serve two functions: **(i) monitoring** (population trend tracking) and **(ii) mass-trapping** (partial population reduction).
- **Blue sticky traps** (wavelength ≈ **470 nm**) selectively attract **thrips**, which are poorly attracted to yellow. Thrips are a major greenhouse pest as they also transmit **tospoviruses** (e.g., Tomato Spotted Wilt Virus).
- **Placement protocol:** Suspend traps **15–30 cm above the crop canopy**, angled slightly (≈ 45°) to maximize catch. Replace every **14 days** as the adhesive dries and dust accumulates.

**Numerical Calculation:**

**Step 1 — Trap count (yellow sticky, whitefly monitoring):**

Using the standard KTU formula:

$$
N_{traps} = \frac{A_{greenhouse}}{A_{trap\_service}} = \frac{1{,}200 \text{ m}^2}{100 \text{ m}^2/\text{trap}}
$$

$$
N_{traps} = 12 \text{ yellow sticky traps}
$$

**Step 2 — *Encarsia formosa* curative release:**

$$
N_{release} = R \times A_{greenhouse} = 15 \text{ wasps/m}^2 \times 1{,}200 \text{ m}^2
$$

$$
N_{release} = 18{,}000 \text{ parasitoid wasps}
$$

**Step 3 — Field deployment recommendation:**

Distribute the **12 traps** in a uniform grid (e.g., **4 × 3 layout** in a rectangular greenhouse). Release the **18,000 wasps** in **three weekly batches of 6,000 each** to ensure sustained parasitism throughout the whitefly life cycle. Re-scout after each release week and adjust future releases based on the new ETL check.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks):** [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **role of biological control in greenhouse IPM**. List **four commercially important biocontrol agents** with their target pests.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain **soil solarization** as a cultural IPM technique. A greenhouse bed has dimensions **50 m × 1 m × 0.30 m** (length × width × depth). Moist soil has a bulk density of **1,400 kg/m³** and a specific heat capacity of **2,100 J·kg⁻¹·K⁻¹**. Calculate the **heat energy (in MJ)** required to raise the soil temperature by **30 K**. State **two safety precautions** for solarization.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Role of Biological Control in Greenhouses:**

Greenhouses offer an **ideal closed environment** for biological control. Unlike open fields — where wind, rain, and emigration decimate released beneficials — the greenhouse *encloses and protects* the biocontrol agents, allowing them to establish, reproduce, and exert sustained pest suppression. Biological control is the **cornerstone of modern greenhouse IPM** and is often sufficient to manage pests *without any chemical intervention*.

**Advantages specific to greenhouses:**
- **Stable microclimate** (T, RH) supports beneficial survival
- **No pesticide drift** from neighbouring fields
- **Predictable release ratios** (no loss to migration)
- **Compatible with bumblebee pollination** programs

**Four Commercially Important Biocontrol Agents:**

| # | Biocontrol Agent | Type | Target Pest |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | *Encarsia formosa* | Parasitoid wasp | Greenhouse whitefly (*Trialeurodes vaporariorum*) |
| 2 | *Phytoseiulus persimilis* | Predatory mite | Two-spotted spider mite (*Tetranychus urticae*) |
| 3 | *Neoseiulus cucumeris* | Predatory mite | Western flower thrips (*Frankliniella occidentalis*) |
| 4 | *Trichoderma harzianum* | Beneficial fungus | Soil-borne pathogens (*Fusarium oxysporum*, *Pythium spp.*, *Rhizoctonia solani*) |

*(Acceptable alternates: *Amblyseius swirskii*, *Aphidius colemani*, *Bacillus thuringiensis*, *Metarhizium anisopliae*.)*

**Limitations:** Higher upfront cost, requires precise timing and identification, sensitive to broad-spectrum pesticide residues, slower knockdown than chemicals.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Soil Solarization — Concept:**

Soil solarization is a **non-chemical cultural IPM technique** that uses **solar radiant energy** trapped under a transparent polyethylene sheet to heat the soil to lethal temperatures (**> 50 °C** at 5 cm depth) for an extended period (**4–6 weeks** during peak summer). It is highly effective against **nematodes, soil-borne fungi, bacteria, insects, and weed seeds** in the top **20–30 cm** of the soil profile.

**Procedure:**
1. Plough and level the bed; remove debris and stones.
2. Irrigate to **field capacity** (moisture is essential — water conducts heat 20× better than air).
3. Cover with **25–50 µm transparent polyethylene** sheet; seal the edges with soil.
4. Expose to direct sunlight for **4–6 weeks** (May–June in Kerala).
5. Remove the sheet, allow to cool, and plant.

**Numerical Calculation:**

**Step 1 — Volume of the greenhouse bed:**

$$
V = L \times W \times D = 50 \text{ m} \times 1 \text{ m} \times 0.30 \text{ m} = 15 \text{ m}^3
$$

**Step 2 — Mass of soil:**

$$
m = \rho \times V = 1{,}400 \text{ kg/m}^3 \times 15 \text{ m}^3 = 21{,}000 \text{ kg}
$$

**Step 3 — Heat energy required:**

$$
Q = m \cdot c \cdot \Delta T
$$

$$
Q = 21{,}000 \text{ kg} \times 2{,}100 \text{ J·kg}^{-1}\text{·K}^{-1} \times 30 \text{ K}
$$

$$
Q = 1{,}323{,}000{,}000 \text{ J}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to MJ:**

$$
Q = \frac{1{,}323{,}000{,}000}{10^6} = 1{,}323 \text{ MJ}
$$

**Two Safety Precautions for Solarization:**
1. **Avoid skin contact with the hot polyethylene film** during removal — wear **gloves and long sleeves**; the sheet and underlying soil can exceed **60 °C**.
2. **Seal the sheet edges completely with soil** to prevent convective heat loss and to stop the escape of volatile fumigants that aid pathogen kill.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE**

- **IPM definition:** sustainable, multi-method, threshold-based pest control.
- **Five pillars:** Cultural, Mechanical, Biological, Chemical, Legal — *in that priority order*.
- **ETL = C / (V · D · K)** — *trigger* to act.
- **EIL** = damage = cost of control — *theoretical red line*.
- **ETL < EIL**, always.
- **Yellow sticky trap** → whiteflies, aphids; service area = 100 m².
- **Blue sticky trap** → thrips; service area = 50 m².
- **Soil solarization:** transparent PE, 4–6 weeks, **> 50 °C**, moist soil essential.
- **Biocontrol agents to memorize (KTU high-frequency):**
  - *Encarsia formosa* → whitefly
  - *Phytoseiulus persimilis* → spider mite
  - *Neoseiulus cucumeris* / *Amblyseius swirskii* → thrips
  - *Trichoderma harzianum* → soil fungi
  - *Bacillus thuringiensis* → caterpillars
- **Common exam trap:** never combine broad-spectrum chemicals with biocontrol — kills beneficials.
- **Specific heat of moist soil:** **2,100 J·kg⁻¹·K⁻¹**.
- **Golden rule:** *No spray without scouting; no release without identification.*
- **IPM = Suppression, NOT Eradication** — keep below ETL indefinitely.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Postproduction Quality and Handling** refers to the integrated sequence of engineering, biological, and management operations performed on a horticultural commodity **immediately after it leaves the production environment** (the greenhouse) and **before it reaches the final consumer**, with the objective of preserving intrinsic quality, ensuring food safety, minimizing quantitative losses, and extending marketable shelf life.

In the context of the KTU 2024 Scheme (OEAGT721), the term explicitly encompasses:

1. **Determination of harvest maturity** using physiological, morphological, and biochemical indices.
2. **Pre-conditioning** of the harvested commodity (curing, degreening, wilting, ethylene treatment).
3. **Pre-cooling** to rapidly remove field heat.
4. **Cleaning, disinfection, sorting, grading, and sizing.**
5. **Packaging** in field, transport, and retail containers.
6. **Storage** under ambient, evaporative-cool, refrigerated, modified-atmosphere (MA), or controlled-atmosphere (CA) conditions.
7. **Transportation** through an unbroken **cold chain**.
8. **Value addition** (minimal processing, drying, freezing, juice extraction).
9. **Quality evaluation** using physical, chemical, microbial, and sensory parameters.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** The university examiner expects students to recognize postproduction as the *second value-addition window* in the greenhouse value chain. Marks are awarded for linking each handling step to the corresponding **quality attribute** (firmness, colour, TSS, vitamin retention, weight loss, microbial load) it preserves.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain-English Intuition)

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Greenhouse Crop as a "Living Battery with an Expiry Timer"**
>
> Imagine that every freshly harvested tomato or capsicum is a small, ticking battery that is **continuously discharging** through the biochemical process of **respiration**, burning its own stored sugars, producing heat, water vapour, and carbon dioxide. The moment the fruit is detached from the mother plant, it is no longer being recharged.
>
> - **Temperature** is the *dial controlling the discharge rate* — every 10 °C rise roughly doubles the rate (Q₁₀ effect).
> - **Humidity** controls *water loss* (the battery's electrolyte drying up).
> - **Atmospheric composition** (O₂, CO₂, ethylene) is the *switchboard* — lowering O₂ and raising CO₂ slows the discharge, just like dimming the lights in a room.
>
> The job of the postproduction engineer is therefore to **slow the discharge, refill the electrolyte, and protect the casing** — that is, to manage temperature, humidity, and gas atmosphere so the "battery" still has usable energy when the consumer opens the package.

### 1.3 Why Postproduction Losses Matter (Quantitative Significance)

The global FAO estimate for **post-harvest losses in fruits and vegetables** is in the range of **30 % to 40 %** in developing economies. For greenhouse produce (tomato, cucumber, capsicum, cut flowers), the typical loss breakdown is:

| Loss Type | Typical Range | Primary Cause |
|-----------|---------------|---------------|
| Physiological (wilting, senescence) | **5 % to 15 %** | Improper temperature & RH |
| Pathological (microbial rot) | **10 % to 25 %** | Field heat + condensation |
| Mechanical (bruising, crushing) | **5 % to 15 %** | Rough handling, poor packaging |
| Cumulative commercial loss | **20 % to 50 %** | Sum of the above |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metric:** Industry benchmarks define acceptable post-harvest loss in a *well-managed greenhouse cold chain* at **≤ 5 %** from harvest to retail shelf. Anything beyond 10 % indicates a broken link, most often the **pre-cooling stage**.

---

## 1.4 Harvest Maturity — The Trigger that Initiates Postproduction

Postproduction handling cannot begin until the **correct harvest maturity stage** is identified. Picking too early yields an under-ripe commodity that will never develop full flavour; picking too late yields an over-ripe one with shortened shelf life.

### Classification of Maturity Indices

| Index Family | Example Parameter | Crop Application |
|--------------|-------------------|------------------|
| **Physical / Morphological** | Size, shape, firmness, colour, abscission layer | Tomato (breaker stage), mango (shoulder colour) |
| **Physiological** | Respiration peak (climacteric), ethylene evolution rate | Banana, tomato, apple |
| **Chemical / Biochemical** | Total Soluble Solids (TSS), titratable acidity (TA), TSS/TA ratio, starch index | Grape (TSS 16–18 %), citrus (TA ratio) |
| **Phenological** | Days after anthesis (DAA), heat units / Growing Degree Days (GDD) | Cucumber (10–14 DAA), tomato (45–60 DAA) |
| **Sensory** | Aroma volatiles, surface gloss, skin toughness | Cut flowers, herbs |

> [!NOTE]
> **Climacteric vs Non-Climacteric (KTU High-Yield Distinction):**
> - *Climacteric fruits* (tomato, banana, mango, apple) display a **respiratory peak** and **autocatalytic ethylene burst** at ripening; they can be harvested mature-green and ripened post-harvest.
> - *Non-climacteric fruits* (cucumber, capsicum, grapes, citrus, strawberry) show **no respiratory peak**; they must be harvested at full ripe-on-plant stage because they will not ripen further once detached.

---

## 1.5 The Seven-Step Quality-Preservation Chain (Engineering View)

The postproduction chain in a greenhouse crop production unit is engineered as **seven sequential stations**:

1. **Harvesting** (manual/knife/shear, with clean tools and shade collection trays).
2. **Field pre-conditioning** (removal of field heat via shade, mobile pre-coolers).
3. **Pre-cooling** (room, forced-air, hydro, vacuum).
4. **Grading & Sorting** (size, colour, defect; manual or electronic).
5. **Packaging** (corrugated boxes, punnets, MAP bags, clamshells).
6. **Storage** (cold rooms, MA/CA rooms, evaporative cool chambers).
7. **Transport & Retail Display** (reefer trucks, insulated vans).

> [!TIP]
> **Quality Decay Rule of Thumb (Hardenburg / Kader):** The relative perishability of fresh produce can be ranked by **respiration rate (mg CO₂ kg⁻¹ h⁻¹)** at 5 °C. High-respiration crops (strawberry, mushroom, broccoli) need aggressive pre-cooling within **2 hours** of harvest; low-respiration crops (apple, onion, garlic) tolerate delays of **24 hours** or more.

# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Respiration — The Central Biophysical Process

### 2.1.1 The Respiration Equation

Fresh horticultural produce continues aerobic respiration post-harvest using the generalized hexose substrate:

$$C_6H_{12}O_6 + 6O_2 \longrightarrow 6CO_2 + 6H_2O + Energy \;(Heat)$$

The **energy released** is partly captured in ATP, but a significant fraction escapes as **respiratory heat (Q)** which must be removed by refrigeration.

### 2.1.2 Respiration Rate (RR) — Definition

$$RR = \frac{dCO_2}{dt} \quad \text{(mg CO}_2 \; \text{kg}^{-1} \text{h}^{-1}\text{)}$$

Or equivalently by oxygen consumption:

$$RQ = \frac{CO_2 \; produced}{O_2 \; consumed} \quad \text{(Respiratory Quotient, dimensionless)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RQ = 1.0** for pure carbohydrate oxidation. **RQ > 1.1** indicates anaerobic or organic-acid metabolism (undesirable — fermentation). **RQ < 1.0** indicates lipid oxidation (typical in oil-rich seeds).

### 2.1.3 Temperature Dependence — Q₁₀ Rule

The Q₁₀ temperature coefficient describes how RR changes per 10 °C rise:

$$Q_{10} = \left(\frac{RR_2}{RR_1}\right)^{\frac{10}{T_2 - T_1}}$$

For most horticultural produce in the 5–25 °C range, **Q₁₀ ≈ 2.0 to 2.5**.

---

## 2.2 Pre-Cooling — Removal of Field Heat

### 2.2.1 Newton's Law of Cooling (Continuous Form)

The temperature of a horticultural commodity approaching ambient refrigeration temperature obeys:

$$\frac{dT}{dt} = -K \; (T - T_a)$$

Where:
- $T$ = product temperature (°C) at time $t$
- $T_a$ = cooling-medium (air/water) temperature (°C)
- $K$ = cooling coefficient (h⁻¹), a function of geometry, surface area, and surface heat-transfer coefficient.

### 2.2.2 Integrated Form (Cooling-Time Equation)

Integrating the differential equation between $T_i$ (initial) and $T_f$ (final):

$$t = \frac{1}{K} \ln\!\left(\frac{T_i - T_a}{T_f - T_a}\right)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Half-Cooling Time (HCT):** Defined as the time to remove **half** of the field heat, $T_f = (T_i + T_a)/2$:
>
> $$t_{1/2} = \frac{\ln 2}{K} \approx \frac{0.693}{K}$$
>
> **Seven-Eighths Cooling Time (SCT):** The time to remove **7/8** (87.5 %) of field heat, the practical "pre-cooling complete" benchmark used by ASHRAE:
>
> $$t_{7/8} = \frac{\ln 8}{K} \approx \frac{2.079}{K} = 3 \times t_{1/2}$$

### 2.2.3 Geometric Note on K

For a sphere of radius $r$, thermal conductivity $k$, density $\rho$, specific heat $C_p$, and surface heat-transfer coefficient $h$:

$$K \approx \frac{h}{\rho \, C_p \, r} \quad \text{(for BiOT } \ll 1\text{)}$$

Where BiOT = Biot number for transient cooling. The cooling coefficient scales **inversely with produce size**: smaller fruits cool faster.

---

## 2.3 Respiration Heat Load — Engineering for Refrigeration Capacity

The refrigeration plant of a greenhouse cold room must remove:

1. **Transmission heat** through walls, floor, ceiling (Q₁).
2. **Infiltration heat** from door openings (Q₂).
3. **Product-respiration heat** (Q₃) — unique to fresh-produce storage.
4. **Equipment & lighting heat** (Q₄).

The respiration-heat load Q₃ is calculated as:

$$Q_3 = m_{prod} \times RR_{CO_2} \times \frac{\Delta H_{CO_2}}{1000}$$

Where:
- $m_{prod}$ = mass of commodity in storage room (kg)
- $RR_{CO_2}$ = respiration rate at storage temperature (mg CO₂ kg⁻¹ h⁻¹)
- $\Delta H_{CO_2}$ = energy released per mg CO₂, typically **10.7 J mg⁻¹ CO₂** (≈ **10.7 kJ kg⁻¹** if RR is in g CO₂ kg⁻¹ h⁻¹, often simplified to **≈ 2.5 W per tonne** of high-respiration produce).

> [!NOTE]
> **Common Approximation (Kader / ASHRAE):** For high-respiration crops stored at 0 °C, 1 tonne of commodity generates **1.5 kW to 3 kW** of respiratory heat; this value **doubles every 7 °C** of storage temperature rise.

---

## 2.4 Modified Atmosphere Packaging (MAP) — Equilibrium Gas Equations

### 2.4.1 Mass Balance Inside a Sealed MAP Bag

For a hermetically sealed package containing respiring produce:

$$\frac{dO_2}{dt} = -R_{O_2} \cdot m + P_{bag} \cdot k_{O_2} \cdot (0.21 - y_{O_2})$$

$$\frac{dCO_2}{dt} = +R_{CO_2} \cdot m + P_{bag} \cdot k_{CO_2} \cdot (y_{CO_2,ext} - y_{CO_2})$$

At **steady state (equilibrium)**, the time derivatives vanish, giving:

$$y_{O_2,eq} = 0.21 - \frac{R_{O_2} \cdot m}{P_{bag} \cdot k_{O_2}}$$

$$y_{CO_2,eq} = y_{CO_2,ext} + \frac{R_{CO_2} \cdot m}{P_{bag} \cdot k_{CO_2}}$$

Where $P_{bag}$ is the permeability (transmission rate) of the packaging film (mL m⁻² day⁻¹ atm⁻¹), and $k$ is the gas-permeability coefficient.

### 2.4.2 Ethylene Effect — Inhibition of Ripening

Ethylene (C₂H₄) is a plant hormone that triggers ripening in climacteric produce. Above a threshold of **0.1 to 1 ppm** it accelerates senescence. Strategies:

| Strategy | Mechanism | Application |
|----------|-----------|-------------|
| Ventilation | Dilution | Storage rooms |
| KMnO₄ scrubber | Oxidation to CO₂ + H₂O | Cold rooms, transport |
| 1-MCP (1-Methylcyclopropene) | Irreversible receptor blocker | Tomato, banana, apple |
| Low-temperature storage | Reduces ACC synthase activity | Universal |
| Low-O₂ storage | Inhibits ethylene action | CA storage |

---
